Third inning batting for Penn State started with Jack Porter, who hit a fly ball out to left center for the first out. Infielder Spencer Barnett stepped up next, and he was walked on a 3-2 count. Infielder Kevin Karstetter struck out swinging, but Barnett stole second base in the process. DeMucci struck out to end the inning, Penn State still led 3-1 heading into the top of the 4th.

Fitzgerald continued a nice start, striking out Estrella, but threw four balls to get Outfielder Carter Back to first. Sutherlin then moved in line with a right side single. Miller singled, which led to a scored run from Carter, tightening the score to 3-2. Martinez hit a double, which led to two scores from Sycamores Sutherlin and Miller. Fitzgerald’s sixth strikeout brought us to the bottom of the 4th, Penn State trailed 4-3.

All three at-bats from Turcovski, Mercado, and Anderson got to a 3-2 count. All three also led to outs, a quick inning of batting for the Nittany Lions.

Fitzgerald bounced back with a quick three-pitch out to start the 5th. Followed by another nice strikeout. A swift ending for the Sycamore hitters with Estrella flying out.

Penn State continued their streak of 3-2 counts leading to outs in the 5th, with Molinaro grounding out for the 1st out. Armstrong continued his successful pitching, striking out Davis and forcing a flyout from Porter. The score stayed at 4-3 for Indiana State.

Beck flies one to right field for the first out of the 6th. Sutherlin bounced one off the wall for a double, continuing to be a thorn in Penn State’s side. Fitzgerald continued his back-and-forth play with his ninth and tenth strikeouts, which got the Lions back on offense.

Barnett starts off the inning walking to first after a 3-1 count. Armstrong continued his impressive pitching, striking out Karstetter. DeMucci also struck out, but Barnett stole his second base of the night to get to second. Penn State’s offensive hopes came to a close with a groundout by Turcovski.

A pitching change starts the 7th for the Nittany Lions. Right-handed pitcher Kyle Emmons stepped up to the mound in relief. Martinez flied out, followed by a double play. A swift defensive inning for Penn State, who still trailed 4-3.

Armstrong started his second straight inning with a walk for Mercado, then the Sycamores brought in right-handed pitcher Ryan Karst, their third of the night. Karst threw a wild pitch to get Mercado to second, followed by hitting Anderson with a ball.

Karst calmed down after a mound visit; Molinaro and Davis both flew out. Estrella made a run to the wall for the Nittany Lions' third out.

Emmons started his second inning of pitching with a long count strikeout. Porter makes a play with his back to the wall for the second out of the inning. Beck grounded out to get the Nittany Lions back on the offensive.

Karst continued his better throwing, and Barnett lined out. Followed by a strikeout of Karstetter. The inning ended quickly after a Jaconski groundout. Penn State trailed 4-3.

Penn State made another pitcher change. Right-handed pitcher Dimond Loosli came in for the last inning. Sutherlin continued his great performance with a single to start the 9th. Miller hit another base hit, which caused Penn State Coach Mike Gambino to come up for a mound visit.

Fulk grounded out but got Sutherlin and Miller in scoring position. Martinez was intentionally walked which led to a strikeout for Loosli. A wide right throw to first led to two runs for the Sycamores. Ortiz hit a two run single which grew the Indiana State lead to 8-3. Estrella ended his team's batting for the day with a groundout.

Turcovski flew out on his second pitch. Mercado got on base for the third time of the night with a single. Anderson nukes one left field for a two-run homerun. Penn State now trailed 8-5 in the bottom of the 9th.

The Sycamores changed pitchers again after the home run. Right-handed pitcher Hunter Small took to the mound in the last inning. Molinaro flew out to center field, and Davis kept the game alive with a single and a stolen base. Porter flew out to center field to end the game.

Up next

The Nittany Lions continue their three-game Bahamas series against Indiana State Saturday at 3 p.m. EST.

MORE BASEBALL CONTENT

title: 11 players see playing time in No. 16 Penn State men’s volleyball’s sweep of Merrimack
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
Amidst a six-match winning streak, Penn State welcomed Merrimack to Rec Hall for just one match on the weekend.

It was all Nittany Lions as they took down the Warriors in a dominant sweep, 3-0.

After the teams traded the first two points, Penn State showcased why it's the No. 16 team in the country.

A 4-0 run, spearheaded by middle blocker Owen Rose, put the Nittany Lions up 5-1 after two kills and a block.

After two points from the Warriors, the blue and white responded with a 4-0 run. Two tough angle swings from Matthew Luoma led the way in scoring.

Penn State continued its momentum in the opening set, as the blocking came alive once again. The middle blockers, Rose and Gaige Gabriel, gave issues to the Merrimack attack as an error from Blake Taschner forced a timeout with the Nittany Lions leading 13-5.

As the first set continued, setter Michael Schwob found outside hitter Bennett Wilson on a multitude of swings. Wilson, in his first career start, sent down his first career kill to showcase the depth of hitters in the blue and white.

Wilson soared high for a kill before going soft on his next attack to put Penn State at set point, leading 24-13.

In a scramble, Rose tapped the ball down as he flew by the Warriors’ front line to seal the set, 25-13.

The Nittany Lions maintained momentum in the second set. The swings of the hitters, whether behind the service line or on the attack, forced the Warriors out of system.

With Penn State leading 8-4, the Nittany Lions came out on top on a long rally that seemingly wasn’t going to end.

Similarly, with the blue and white ahead 12-6, another lengthy rally took place. Just like the first, Penn State came out on top as a huge momentum booster in the set.

That momentum carried into the rest of the set as an ace from Rose sent the match to a media timeout with the Nittany Lions leading 15-7.

As the lead held, coach Mark Pavlik emptied the bench to showcase the depth even more. Middle blocker Zach Stowe entered for Rose, while outside hitter Kyle Fagan stepped on to the court for Jaidyn Bethel.

With setter Jaxon Herr — a player that’s seen action in most matches — on the court, he found his fresh hitters. Stowe notched his first kill of the match on a quick set from Herr. The middle blocker sent down a heavy swing to give the blue and white a 22-15 lead.

Merrimack battled back into the set, but two service errors gave the Nittany Lions set point in the second set. A tough serve from Fagan forced an error on the attack, giving the blue and white a 25-19 lead in the second set.

With multiple backups in, Penn State allowed the Warriors to start the third set with some momentum.

Despite trailing 10-7, the Nittany Lions stormed back behind those newly inserted players. Fagan tallied his first career kill, which was followed by an ace from Stowe, which jump-started a 5-0 run.

It was all Penn State from there, as a kill from Tristan Hassell and a double block started another run to give the blue and white a 17-13 lead.

Hassell notched another kill and back-to-back errors from Merrimack gave the Nittany Lions match point with a chance for a sweep.

The Toronto, Ontario, native logged a service error and an error from the Warriors ended the match.

Penn State took the match 3-0 with a dominant offensive showing from all hitters.

Up next

The Nittany Lions will head to the West Coast to take on No. 2 Long Beach State at 10 p.m. EST on Saturday, March 7.

MORE VOLLEYBALL CONTENT

title: No. 3 Penn State women’s hockey shuts out Syracuse in Game 1 of AHA semifinal
author: Dominic Megats I The Daily Collegian
description:
After finishing first place in the AHA and earning a bye week, No. 3 Penn State returned to Pegula Ice Arena, beginning its quest to win its fourth-straight conference championship.

However, standing in their way is Syracuse and a best-of-three semifinal series. Unlike the previous few games, the Nittany Lions were able to return to four lines with returning Olympian forwards.

“It helps that we were able to run four lines and people who maybe were averaging a ton of minutes,” head coach Jeff Kampersal said. “We had three lines, and sometimes eight forwards; those four were playing an awful lot. So, it’s good to sort of get that rest.”

In the first game of the AHA semifinal round, all three returning Olympians scored a goal, leading to a 7-0 Game 1 win for Penn State.

Janecke returned to her first line center position, while Matilde Fantin and Nicole Hall winged the third line.

First period

Penn State made the first mistake of the game after Grace Tullock had her stick caught between the legs of a forward, leading to a tripping call. Syracuse found themselves on a man advantage five minutes into the game. The Nittany Lions' penalty kill stood strong after facing an early test and returned to full strength.

The Nittany Lions held their foot on the gas following a successful kill. The returning Janecke had two of the best scoring chances for Penn State after firing quick wrist shots from the slot. The glove of Ava Drabyk ultimately robbed both attempts.

However, the third time was the charm for the gold medalist. Abby Stonehouse sent over a one-time pass to Janecke in the right faceoff circle. After receiving the pass, the Orangeville, Illinois, native beat Drabyk’s glove and scored her first goal back from the Olympics.

Janecke wasn’t the only returning Olympian to score in the first period. Matilde Fantin decided to join the party almost two minutes later. The Como, Italy, native beat Drabyk’s glove off as she entered the offensive zone on a breakaway.

It didn’t take long for a pair of Olympians to return to Happy Valley and get their names on the scoresheet. The two goals were the only ones scored in the opening frame. Penn State headed into the locker room with a two-goal lead.

Second period

Much like the beginning of the first period, the second started with the Nittany Lions on the penalty kill. Janecke was given a delay of game penalty only 50 seconds into the middle frame. Penn State was successful once again against the man advantage and developed momentum following the kill.

The blue and white possessed the puck in its offensive zone for the majority of the period, racking up shots and scoring chances.

After constant pressure put on by Penn State’s offense, Mya Vaslet found the back of the net after her backhand shot beat the left pad of Drabyk. This is the Stittsville, Ontario, native’s first goal back from missing the previous series due to injury.

Janecke entered hat-trick watch after netting her second of the game, this time on the power play. After a lead pass from Leah Stecker into the offensive zone, the captain found the net after shooting stick side on Drabyk.

Following the fourth goal of the game, Drabyk was ejected from the contest after an altercation with Maddy Christian led to a major penalty and game misconduct for head contact. Bella Gould filled the role in replacement.

The Nittany Lions used the middle frame to double their lead, ending the second period up 4-0.

Third period

A common theme throughout the opening five minutes of the first two periods was Penn State penalties. The same continued in the third. Taya MacDonald was sent to the box after a heavy hit on the boards led to a body-checking penalty.

After successfully killing off the penalty, hats began to fly onto the ice after Janecke found the net for the third time in the game. Mikah Keller sent a pass that was tapped in off her stick and behind Gould.

“It’s just a different level, and to take what you’ve learned (at the Olympics) and apply it here, it becomes a lot easier to find ways to score,” Janecke said.

Hall rounded out all three Olympians, finding the net. Fantin found her fellow Olympic athlete as she crashed into the net. The Stockholm, Sweden, native tipped in her 10th goal of the season.

“It’s great to have those (returning) players back. I was just saying they’re a really special group. It’s all been a special year. The fact that four players that haven’t been with us for a little bit all got on the scoreboard, that was kind of cool.” Kampersal said.

The extra point was good for Penn State after Abby Stonehouse scored the seventh goal of the game for the Nittany Lions.

Penn State concluded Game 1 after a late power play chance that led to no addition to the scoreboard, ending the game with a 7-0 victory.

Up Next

Both teams will return to Pegula for Game 2 on Saturday at 3 p.m.

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: Two Penn State fraternities placed on interim suspension
author: Paloma Munoz | The Daily Collegian
description:
Delta Chi and Lambda Chi Alpha have been placed on interim suspension for alleged violations of university policies.

Delta Chi was suspended Jan. 27, and Lambda Chi Alpha was suspended Jan. 21, according to the Penn State Student Affairs website. The university has not released any details on which policies were allegedly violated.

Under interim suspension, fraternities have activities and privileges temporarily halted by either Penn State or their national governing bodies, according to Student Affairs. Both fraternities will be unable to hold meetings and events or recruit new members until the suspension is investigated and lifted.

Nine fraternities are currently suspended at Penn State.

MORE CAMPUS COVERAGE

title: No. 25 Penn State softball falls to No. 1 Tennessee despite leading early
author: Gianna Dowling | The Daily Collegian
description:
After defeating NC Central for its 10th straight win, Penn State returned to the field for its second Friday afternoon game with its toughest task yet this season: a meeting with undefeated No. 1 Tennessee.

The Lady Volunteers avoided the upset, defeating the Nittany Lions 4-1 behind a fifth inning grand slam

Right-hander Sage Mardjetko got the starting nod for Tennessee, shutting down the high-power Penn State offense with just one runner reaching via an infield error.

In the bottom of the first inning, right fielder Gabby Leach knocked a one-out single, but Abigail Britton and the Nittany Lions’ defense prevented any more Lady Volunteers from getting on base.

It was all pitching in the second frame, as Penn State went down in order with two strikeouts from Mardjetko. Britton one-upped the Tennessee pitcher, striking out the side in the bottom of the inning.

Nittany Lions’ second baseman Michela Barbanente worked a two-out walk for the blue and white’s first free base of the game, but Penn State couldn’t do anymore damage.

The Lady Volunteers threatened in the bottom of the inning after third baseman Maddi Rutan recorded an infield single and after a sacrifice bunt and groundout, Rutan found her way to third. Britton came up big, recording her fifth strikeout of the night.

As the game stood scoreless through three innings, it seemed like a pitcher's duel was in the air for the fans at Sherri Lee Parker Stadium, but then Allison Oneacre stepped up to the plate. The third baseman led off the fourth inning with a full count home run for her eighth long ball of the year.

Penn State couldn’t do any more harm for the rest of the frame, but held the slight edge over the nation's No.1 team heading into the bottom of the inning.

Following two walks and an infield single, Tennessee loaded up the bases with two outs. Makenzie Butt stepped up to the plate as a pinch hitter, grounding out to Oneacre and ending the threat as the Nittany Lions kept their 1-0 lead intact heading into the fifth frame.

Mardjetko continued to deal in the fifth inning, putting the blue and white down in order. The Lemont, Illinois, native tossed two strikeouts in the frame, putting herself at eight on the night.

The Lady Volunteers welcomed the top of the order at the bottom of the inning, and coach Clarisa Crowell made a call to the bullpen, bringing in left-handed freshman Brevyn Kellepouris.

A bunt and two singles allowed Tennessee to load up the bases with no outs. On a 2-0 count, second baseman Ella Dodge launched a grand slam, giving the Lady Volunteers the three-run lead.

Kellepouris and the Penn State defense retired the next three batters, but the damage was done as the Tennessee offense gave Mardjetko a 4-1 cushion heading into the sixth inning.

The Nittany Lions remained with just one hit through six at bats, after going down in order for the second straight inning.

Rutan worked a walk to start the bottom of the sixth frame, but couldn’t be moved around as the blue and white’s defense stood strong.

Penn State brought up its four, five and six hitters in the seventh inning with its last chance to break through against Mardjetko.

Designated player Brooke Klosowicz led off with a walk in just the second free base allowed by Mardjetko on the night.

A strikeout and groundout moved Klosowicz to second, but the Nittany Lions had just one out to work with. Catcher Madison Seidel stepped up to the plate, but became Mardjetko’s 10th strikeout victim of the night as the blue and white fell short of an upset.

Up Next

The Nittany Lions return to action at 12:30 p.m. Saturday to face Appalachian State.

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State track and field dominates in Big Ten Championship
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Editor's note: The story will be updated after each day's performances.

Following a record-breaking regular season, Penn State was ready for the postseason.

The Nittany Lions took the track for the Big Ten Indoor Track and Field championships in Indianapolis on Thursday, competing for the next three days with the last chance to make a national championship bid.

At the end of the Big Ten championship, the men’s squad was in second place with 96 points with the women’s team in ninth, earning three individual Big Ten titles.

Thursday

Senior Maddie Pitts got things going in her last Big Ten championship in the pentathlon 60-meter hurdles, where she recorded a personal-best 8.34 seconds for first place, taking the early lead with 1,052 points.

In the pentathlon high jump, Pitts earned another personal best, jumping 1.67 meters for seventh place, ultimately moving her to fourth overall with 1,870 points.

Following the pentathlon shot put, she moved back into the top three, as she threw 12.91 meters for third.

The men’s team got off to a hot start in the 600-meters, as the Nittany Lions earned four of the eight spots in the finals. Nebraska transfer Niko Schultz led the way with a meet record 1:15.22 — the third-best time in school history — followed by Allon Clay in a personal-best 1:15.47 and Handal Roban in fourth with 1:15.96. Kaleb Nastari just missed the finals with a 1:17.01 for ninth.

For the third time in four events, Pitts earned another personal best in the pentathlon long jump, finishing third in 6.24 meters.

In the men’s 200-meter prelims, sophomore Ajani Dwyer broke his No. 2 time in school history with a time of 20.51 seconds, finishing second and earning a spot in Saturday’s finals.

Charlotte Costich was nearly Penn State’s first scorer in the women’s 5,000-meters, as she finished one place shy in 16:07.86 for ninth place.

The men’s 5,000m put the first points on the board as State College native Nick Sloff was the fourth to cross the finish line in 13:49.93, but finished third after Oregon’s Cooper Burns was disqualified, earning six points for the Nittany Lions.

Pitts cashed in with her three personal bests as she finished in third place, earning Penn State’s first medal of the day as she set the school record with 4,384 points. Pitts ran 2:16.68 in the 800-meters as she capped off her historic day.

After one medal, Penn State came back and earned its second of the day in the women’s distance medley relay, as the squad of Hayley Kitching, Alexandria North, Boh Ritchie and Ada Rand finished third in 10:57.52.

The men’s DMR finished the day with their backup relay group of Yukichi Ishii, Matt DeMatteo, Luke Seymour and Ryan Watson, who ran 9:41.01 for fifth place.

Friday

The Nittany Lions’ success continued in Day 2, qualifying for 10 more events and earning another point for the women’s team.

Sophomore Hannah Riolo kicked off the action for the blue and white in the women’s high jump, nearly setting a personal best as she jumped 1.75 meters for eighth place and one point.

The women’s mile saw four Nittany Lions take the starting line, as sophomore Ada Rand and senior Sarah Pickering emerged with a spot in Saturday’s finals. Rand finished eighth overall with a time of 4:40.43, while Pickering finished 10th in 4:42.26.

The Nittany Lions earned another finals qualifier in the men’s 400-meter, as sophomore Jake Palermo ran 45.87 seconds for fourth place.

In the men’s weight throw, graduate student Collin Burkhart earned a spot in the finals with a throw of 20.67 meters, but fell to ninth place after scratching two of his throws in the finals.

For the second time in two days, Dwyer earned another bid to the finals, this time coming from the 60-meters. Dwyer won the race in 6.55 seconds, setting the facility record in the process.

Penn State qualified another two runners for the finals in the women’s 800-meter, as Kitching set the Big Ten championship meet record in 2:03.19, followed by Ritchie in sixth place in 2:05.22.

The men’s 800m saw even greater success, as Clay led the way with a facility record 1:46.31. Behind him was Roban in third in 1:47.26, Ishii in fourth with 1:47.47 and Schultz in fifth in 1:47.48 to finish the day

Day 3

Penn State wrapped up the last day of the Big Ten championship with a lot of success, but it wasn’t enough to clinch the team title.

Freshman Trip Campbell tied his personal best in the men’s high jump, jumping 2.15 meters to take home bronze in his first year. Junior Robert Allen also tied his personal-best for 2.09 meters, finishing ninth.

While she was seeded eighth entering the competition, junior Lizzie Schreiber fell just short of that mark, jumping 12.88 meters for ninth place.

In just her sophomore year, Skye Petrie-Cameron earned a big personal-best at the championships, throwing 16.45 meters for seventh place and the No. 4 spot in Penn State history.

After finishing eighth and 10th in the prelims, Rand and Pickering came back and earned slight improvements in the women’s mile finals, running 4:42.28 for seventh place and 4:42.95 for ninth place, respectively.

Palermo also earned an improvement in the men’s 400m finals, as he earned his first silver medal with a time of 45.73.

In the men’s 60m, Dwyer didn’t live up to his top-seeded time, but ultimately finished third in 6.55 seconds for his first bronze medal.

Penn State’s first champion came in the women’s 800m finals, as Kitching set another meet record in 2:00.55 to win her fourth individual Big Ten championship. Ritchie finished in fourth with a time of 2:02.41.

The men’s 800m saw even greater success, as the Nittany Lions swept the top three places, led by Roban. The St. Vincent and the Grenadines native earned his second individual title in 1:46.44, and was followed by Clay in 1:46.65 and Schultz in 1:46.75.

The blue and white earned its third straight title in the men’s shot put, as Ryan Henry threw 19.94 meters, the third-best in Penn State history, besting three-time Olympic medalist Joe Kovacs previous No. 3 mark.

In the men’s 600m finals, the Nittany Lions earned a second, fourth and sixth-place finish, as Roban once again led the way in 1:15.59.

Clay finished fourth in 1:16.57, followed by Schultz’ 1:16.75 for sixth.

Dwyer came back and earned his second bronze medal of the day, this time in the men’s 200m finals, running 20.54 seconds.

Sloff also earned his second fourth-place finish of the weekend in the men’s 3000-meters, as he improved on his No. 7 seed, running 8:01.94 to earn another five points for the blue and white.

The men’s squad wrapped up the Big Ten championships in the men’s 4x400m, finishing fifth place in 3:08.25. The squad was led by Palermo, Peyton Sommers, DeMatteo and Max DeAngelo

Up next

The Nittany Lions wait for the rest of the conference championships to finish before seeing the top 16 athletes from each event earn a spot in the NCAA championship meet.

So far, Penn State tentatively has Dwyer in the 60m and 200m, Palermo in the 400m, Roban, Clay and Ishii in the 800m, the distance medley relay team and Henry and Dylan Lambrecht in the men’s shot put.

The women’s squad has Kitching and Ritchie in the 800m and Pitts in the pentathlon.

MORE TRACK AND FIELD COVERAGE

title: Penn State men's gymnastics presented with chance to build momentum against Springfield at Rec Hall
author: Annie Gleba | The Daily Collegian
description:
No. 6 Penn State will take on No. 12 Springfield College on Sunday at 1 p.m. This matchup is the last time the Nittany Lions will see a non-Big Ten team until potential competition in the NCAA Championship.

They are coming into the meet with an overall record of 2-1 and placed first and tied for second in tournament play. In the most recent tournament the Nittany Lions visited, The Winter Cup, Landon Simpson placed 15th with an overall score of 75.250.

Simpson’s strongest performance in this competition came on the vault with a 13.800. The 3rd place winner in this skill also scored a 13.800, showing just how close his scoring was to the top competitors.

The junior sits in three out of the six high scores for his team. His high ranks are in floor, vault, and parallel bars.

Matthew Underhill has continued to stand out in his performances along with Simpson. He claims the top score in rings with a 14.7000 as well as vault with a 14.3000 on his team. The senior achieved all of these scores in early competitions, not able to break over 14.0000 in the month of February.

This season, Springfield College is sitting at a record of 1-1 overall, placing 2nd overall and 4th overall in tournament play. In their last competition, they took second place in the tri-meet at the University of Michigan and Arizona State University. They were able to mark a score of 302.450, but fell short by 16 points of Michigan's score.

The competition to watch for is the vault, Springfield’s Kaleb Palacio and Even Reichert, who have scored at 14.5000 and 13.9000. On Penn State’s side, Matthew Underill and Kellen Ryan have scored a 14.3000 and a 14.2000. These scores could influence the overall place of the teams in this competition.

For the blue and white, a win could set them up for a strong match against No.4 Michigan, which they will face off against on March 7th in Ann Arbor, Mich. Penn State’s average overall score is a 315.280, while Michigan’s is a 316.420.

Springfield is coming to Rec Hall with an average score of 304.570, so they will have to showcase their best routines and athletes to overcome the Nittany Lions.

Penn State alumnus and Olympic Athlete Stephen Nedoroscik will be in attendance for this meetup. Nedoroscik won a bronze medal for the United States, which was the first medal in over a decade and a half for the blue and white.

Sunday’s meet gives Penn State one of their last chances to sharpen its routines before returning to Big Ten competition. A strong performance at Rec Hall could build valuable momentum as the Nittany Lions prepare for their upcoming matchups.

MORE GYMNASTICS COVERAGE

title: Penn State LB Tony Rojas details rehab process, decision to return
author: Lexie Linderman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Tony Rojas walked into the locker room after practice on Sept. 30 with just one phrase running through his head — “there’s no way.”

But there was a way, and Rojas was in surgery two weeks later fixing a torn ACL that knocked him out for the rest of Penn State’s 2025 season.

“That was probably the hardest mental break I've ever had in my life, just going through that,” Rojas said Wednesday. “But people were there supporting me, and it was a good lesson for me to sit back and just see that from an outside perspective.”

Rojas’ injury coincided with the start of the Nittany Lions’ spiral into despair, as that same weekend they lost to UCLA. A week later, James Franklin was fired, and Penn State went on to lose six straight games.

The linebacker was vocal on social media regarding Franklin’s firing and eventually his hiring at Virginia Tech, showing support for his former head coach. But even though the Hokies reside in his home state of Virginia, there was never a thought in Rojas’ mind of following Franklin and leaving Penn State.

“(It came down to) not really wanting to go anywhere else and kind of settle in … It just felt like home,” Rojas said. “And I didn’t want to be in the predicament where once I graduate, it's awkward to go back to the college where I go for a year, and knowing that I can come back here anytime, it’s a good feeling.”

Rojas said there were a few schools that pursued him aggressively, but Penn State is home to him, and that was the end of those discussions.

With the decision on his future in the rear view mirror, Rojas’ focus is on rehab. He said whenever he isn’t in class, he’s rehabbing, and he has his eyes set on being a full-go for the summer with the possibility of a return toward the tail-end of spring practice.

But that doesn’t mean it’s been easy for Rojas, as the rehab process has been long and grueling. He did say, however, that he’s been “crushing” it the whole way through.

“I'll catch myself some days just not really wanting to go in because it's repetitive, but I’m past that,” Rojas said. “And just having Alex, the trainer, in there, very helpful, and he's done a lot of ACLs, so just having the mindset of fighting through it every day, because it gets repetitive and they can get boring, but if you don't do it from that perspective, it'll be all good.”

Rojas said he’s spoken with former Nittany Lion and current Tennessee Titan, Kevin Winston Jr., who partially tore his ACL in 2024 and played in just two games. Winston told Rojas he feels even better than before, and Rojas is starting to believe the same for himself.

Rojas has also been relying on podcasts, YouTube videos and articles to help his mindset. He said he tries to listen to a podcast or video every night to stay positive, which he finds to be beneficial.

Now that Rojas has jumped over the mental hurdles regarding his injury, he has a new challenge in front of him: getting to know his new teammates and coaches.

So far, he’s been enjoying the new era of Penn State and is confident the Nittany Lions will bounce back from the disaster that was 2025.

“We're all getting along,” Rojas said. “We still have a long way to go, but we'll have summer, and once spring ball hits, I feel like our bond and the puzzle pieces will finally start falling together. So I'm excited for that.”

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State softball extends win streak behind explosive offensive performance against NC Central
author: Ashley Beres | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State traveled down south to Rocky Top for the Tennessee Invitational and took on a slumping N.C. Central on Friday afternoon. The blazing hot Nittany Lions went undefeated at the SpaceU Classic and entered the game on a nine-game winning streak.

The Nittany Lions exploded offensively late and extended their winning streak, picking up a 6-0 win over N.C. Central.

Despite the blue and white’s bats pouring on runs throughout the start of the season, right-hander Madeline Janssen threw three scoreless innings. The Nittany Lions had bases loaded in the second inning with just one out, but they couldn’t plate any runs.

However, freshman right-hander Mackenzie Duncan mowed down the Eagles, allowing no baserunners until the fourth inning. The Winter Garden, Florida, native finally surrendered her first hit off a one-out single by Tyler Suttles, but retired the next two batters.

While the blue and white started slow offensively, the Nittany Lions finally broke the scoreless tie in the bottom of the fourth. Catcher Macy Chamberlin smashed a solo home run deep into center, giving Penn State a crucial lead, 1-0.

Duncan delivered another scoreless inning in the fifth, racking up two more strikeouts, but the Nittany Lions went down quietly in the bottom half. As the game turned to the later innings, both squads couldn’t waste any opportunities with base runners.

Daiyah Bailey ripped a two-out double to left field in the sixth inning, and Duncan suddenly faced the go-ahead run at the plate. Despite Suttles getting a hit in her previous at-bat, she flew out to center and ended the threat.

Penn State opened the scoring up later in the sixth, pouring in five insurance runs. First baseman Kirsten Finarelli poked a leadoff single to center, and designated player Kalli Karwowski advanced her into scoring position with a sacrifice bunt.

Feeling the pressure in a tight game, Janssen walked Chamberlin. Center fielder Cara Bohner came through with a clutch double to left center and plated Finarelli. Now up 2-0, the Nittany Lions began to build offensive momentum.

Right fielder Gabby Gradishar continued the scoring and smothered a two-RBI double deep into center. Pinch-hitting for Kaylie Walters, Bridget Nemeth delivered a timely RBI single into center, making Penn State’s lead 5-0.

Walters came back in to pinch run for Nemeth and stole second. With another runner in scoring position, freshman breakout star Allison Oneacre continued her offensive dominance. The Alpharetta, Georgia, native ripped an RBI single to center, and Penn State was now pouring it on, 6-0.

Duncan threw a complete game and closed the victory out in the seventh, extending the blue and white’s win streak to double digits. The freshman showed out, totaling nine strikeouts and allowing just two hits.

Up Next

Penn State faces its biggest test of the season against No. 1 Tennessee at 5:30 p.m. Friday.

MORE SOFTBALL CONTENT

title: Ex-JMU kickoff specialist Cristiano Rosa was ‘blown away’ by opportunity to transfer to Penn State
author: Lyle Alenstein | Wrestling Editor
description:
When kickoff specialist Cristiano Rosa announced his transfer commitment to Penn State, he posted on his Instagram a photo of himself as a little kid wearing a Penn State jersey.

The flag football league he played in named its teams after colleges, so after previously wearing the blue and white as a young boy, the Connecticut native is now playing for the actual Nittany Lions.

“Penn State's by far the biggest school in the Northeast, so I was blown away by the opportunity,” Rosa said.

Rosa played soccer his whole life but didn’t start kicking footballs until high school.

He started his collegiate career at Central Connecticut State in 2022, appearing in one game across two years, going 1-for-3 on field goals. He then transferred to James Madison, where he appeared in every game the last two seasons as the kickoff specialist, becoming the program’s all-time touchback leader with 114.

Following an appearance in the College Football Playoff this past year with the Dukes, which quickly ended with a 51-34 loss to Oregon, JMU head coach Bob Chesney took the head coaching gig at UCLA.

During the season, Rosa knew there was a good chance Chesney, the coaching staff and other players would pursue other opportunities because of the Dukes’ triumph on the field.

“I really enjoyed my time there, and I have nothing bad to say about JMU and my coaching staff while I was there,” Rosa said. “With that whole team and coaching staff kind of dismantling, I knew in the back of my mind that was a possibility with all the success we were having. And then once that happened, that was when I made the decision to move on and look elsewhere.”

Rosa had been in the portal once before, so there was some familiarity with how to handle it

The process still takes a toll, as he referred to it as “very stressful” as he was forced to move “very fast.” So when Matt Campbell and special teams coordinator Justin Lustig came calling, Rosa’s mind was made up.

“Coach Campbell and Coach Lustig, I really was a big fan of them, and I really liked them,” Rosa said. “The culture here was really attractive to me.”

The redshirt senior had especially high praise for Lustig, one of the few staffers to remain from a season ago. It’ll be Lustig’s third season in Happy Valley, and Rosa is fired up to work with him.

“He's the man. He's obviously very knowledgeable,” Rosa said. “Penn State special teams is always a well-oiled machine, and it was something I really wanted to be a part of.”

It’ll be a revamped special teams for the Nittany Lions next season. Kicker Ryan Barker is the only returning piece. Blaise Sokach-Minnick is expected to replace Tyler Duszansky at long snapper. Mississippi State transfer Nathan Tiyce likely will slot in for Gabe Nwosu at punter.

Rosa will likely take the place of Nwosu at kickoff specialist, and could also fill in his role as a long-range field goal kicker. Rosa said he hit from over 70 yards indoors in the fall.

While Rosa gets accustomed to his new team and role that comes with it, Nwosu has already reached out to help him get the lay of the land.

“I met him very briefly after getting here, and he kind of told me some things with kicking in the stadium, kicking on the practice field, small things here and there that can help me out,” Rosa said. “Obviously he did a great job here for years. So if I could do that for my year or two here, then I’d be really happy.”

While it’s a new team and group of players in his position room, Rosa’s already got a sense of what it takes to play special teams for the Nittany Lions.

Why? Because the JMU special teams strived to be like Penn State’s 2025 unit, which was one of the best in the country.

“This past year, they were No. 1 in the nation in special teams efficiency,” Rosa said. “That was always a stat at James Madison, we always kind of looked at and (ask) ‘how are we doing?’ We're making a positive impact on the game, and it's always fun when you can make a positive impact on the game and help win football games.”

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Column | The beauty of being alone
author: Samantha DiCamillo | The Daily Collegian
description:
One of my favorite sayings is “the greatest rewards come when you take the biggest risks.”

There’s always a sense of security with familiarity. Familiarity comes with a warm blanket of safety that keeps people from venturing outside of what they know. They stick to their usual routine, which repeats and repeats itself.

The word alone tends to terrify a majority of people. It’s associated with the awful feeling that you might have to navigate the world alone, but that’s not always true. Learning to be comfortable on your own is one of the hardest, most meaningful lessons.

When you decide to try something new, especially alone, there’s an overwhelming sense of exhilaration. The initial fear fades away as you begin to adjust to the unusual feeling of leaving your comfort zone. In one crucial moment, you made the decision that could change your life for the better.

Every person walks the path of life at their own pace, and that’s the same for becoming comfortable with being alone. My favorite way to try something unfamiliar is by planning to do an interesting activity by myself and committing to it. By keeping myself honest, I stick to the plan, no matter how I might feel.

Edinburgh, Scotland, is my home for the next couple of months, and it’s filled with so much to do. Time seems to be slipping by faster than I can experience everything Scotland has to offer.

There’s a lot of effort needed to take time out of my day to explore. But I push myself to do it anyway. The city holds plenty of history and is the perfect place for people to go on relaxing hikes.

Edinburgh is home to a 350-million-year-old extinct volcano named Arthur’s Seat. This massive volcano looks over the city and is said to hold legends of King Arthur. Public transport is very accessible in Scotland, allowing tourists and locals alike to easily access the Holyrood Park where it’s located. The hike takes about thirty minutes to reach the top.

The path laid for travelers is made up of substantial-sized stones, placed unevenly for those to climb to the top. It’s a moderately difficult hike, due to the steepness and length. As you climb, there are multiple viewpoints to stop and admire how far you have come.

If you succeed at making it to the very top, you’re able to admire the whole city from above. The mountains loom in the distance as the wind comes rushing at your face. It’s a stunning view and a peaceful journey, well worth the effort.

Numerous activities are great if you’re traveling alone. Cafe dates by yourself to limit procrastination at work, exercising, visiting a local museum, trying a different restaurant, or listening to live music are all excellent options.

If you’ve ever wanted to do something new, why wait? Potential regret is a feeling that fades with time, but memories last forever. Taking time out of your day to prioritize yourself is a great way to live a balanced lifestyle.

There’s a common misconception that it’s wrong to be alone when you’re in public. That’s not true, and it’s a barrier that needs to be broken. The people you get to meet and the memories you create make the risk worth it every time.

MORE ABROAD COLUMNS

title: Penn State women’s gymnastics looks for top spot in B1G 4 meet on Saturday
author: Sami Altmire | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State will participate in some friendly competition on Saturday to close out the month, going up against fellow Big Ten members Michigan, Nebraska and Washington.

This follows a rough patch for the Nittany Lions, losing against No. 2 LSU, and then three straight losses against Big Ten teams. The action is set to begin inside Rec Hall at 6 p.m.

The blue and white are regrouping after the most recent loss against Iowa on Saturday, in which it fell to the Hawkeyes 197.057-196.525. Penn State has been just behind its competitors, often being within .500 points of the winner.

This means that the Nittany Lions can’t afford to lose precious points off their routines, and every extra .025 points can get them to go all the way. When looking at who Penn State is taking on this weekend, No. 9 Michigan will be the mountain blue and white needs to scale.

The Wolverines have gone 6-3 this season, which puts them in a good position in the rankings, but two of those three losses have been against the same giants that the Nittany Lions have fallen to, Minnesota and Michigan State.

This shows there could be a gap in Michigan's abilities, and that Penn State is not as far behind as it might have thought.

Washington has been looking at trying to hold its own in the Big Ten season, taking down Ohio State and Rutgers, but the losses have been building for the Huskies. Minnesota was at the scene of the crime again, and teams like Illinois and Maryland gave Washington losses as well.

This shows there could be some struggle for the Huskies, as the blue and white and the Wolverines have taken down multiple of those teams fairly easily. Holding a No. 34 ranking, this weekend could be a test for the West Coasters.

Nebraska could be the dark horse of this meet. The Cornhuskers have a mix of wins and losses, losing to Big Ten top competitor, UCLA, and other teams like Maryland and Ohio State, but taking down Minnesota.

Nebraska has shown time and time again that it can show up and be successful, and it can make waves in competition, so there should be no counting it out just yet.

It will be an interesting quad-meet with a lot of room for possible upsets. Penn State has a chance for its first win in a few weeks, Michigan is trying to stay on top of the Big Ten rankings, Washington is trying to stay afloat and Nebraska is looking to break the predictions.

The blue and white should keep to what it knows well, which is the uneven bars and vault. Bars have always been the strongest apparatus for the Nittany Lions, with it often being where most of the team's points come from. Vault has also been a really strong focus for the majority of the Penn State roster, so a lot of solid points can come from there as well.

This weekend should provide a look into what the rest of the Big Ten season could turn into and what teams are really pushing for a place in the spotlight.

MORE GYMNASTICS COVERAGE

title: ‘He's had a great year battling and getting better’ | Penn State men’s basketball’s Josh Reed has breakout senior season
author: Myles Hannak | The Daily Collegian
description:
For Penn State, Josh Reed stands alone, not just on his team but in the current program’s landscape.

As the lone senior on his squad, he stands as the only player on both the men’s and women’s teams to have over 100 career appearances.

Reed surely has stories to tell about his lengthy hoops journey, but all of those pale in comparison to the peak narrative of his career: his one season of Penn State basketball excellence.

For a Nittany Lions unit led by Mike Rhoades entering 2026, this seniority was of utmost importance for a team still molding and figuring itself out with the most freshmen in the Big Ten. Throughout the season, this has been exactly Reed’s role.

“Everybody's paying to get older and more mature,” Rhoades said at local Big Ten media day. “We did it a different route. But what I love is they're very hungry. They're living in the gym. There's a high level of basketball character. And you could build on that without a doubt.”

Although the Atlanta native doesn’t exhibit the youth the boss references, on the court in 2026, Reed has displayed every other trait Rhoades mentioned, creating a career year in Happy Valley in his first and what’ll be his only year with the squad.

The blue and white saw a revolving door of personnel last season, with five outgoing transfers and Puff Johnson’s departure, who eventually made an appearance in 2026 due to a legal injunction.

The Nittany Lions had little idea who would step up as a vocal leader in this predicament. Still, Rhoades called on a Cincinnati fan favorite, Reed, as one of two additions via the portal, alongside forward Sasǎ Ciani. At times during his final season, fans chanted his name along with "MVP," but after earning his degree, to the surprise of some, Reed opted to depart.

The 6-foot-8 wing was a three-year bench piece, but averaged career highs in minutes, 17.6, and points, 4.8, in his final season there before entering the portal.

“Yeah, I mean, it can be really hard at first, just coming into somewhere new, adjusting to new teammates, new coaches,” Reed said on Feb. 2. “But once you get used to it, you settle in a little bit and feel a little bit better. Confidence gets higher, and then it just grows from there.”

Only two real rotational players returned for Penn State from last season, and while both guard Freddie Dilione V and Dominick Stewart endured their own jumps in usage, none has been quite as unprecedented as Reed’s.

Entering the season, the writing was on the wall for Reed’s emergence as the voice of Penn State on the floor; he represented the team at Big Ten Media Days in Chicago alongside other blue and white returnee Eli Rice and was named one of four captains.

Reed has been the third primary contributor for Penn State behind guards Dilione and the program’s all-time highest-ranked recruit, Kayden Mingo, who excelled in the system thrust into with Reed as his partner.

“Just kind of helping these guys along with whatever they need,” Reed said on Nov. 11. “But at the same time also being able to learn from them too, coming from a new place to here, just learn from guys like Dom and Fred, who were here last year, and just kind of helping those other freshmen along too.”

The senior, who made only nine starts before his Penn State career, has appeared in the first unit for every game but one so far this season and has been one of four to miss no game action.

Over those increased duties, Reed averages 27.5 minutes per game with efficient double-digit scoring, where he makes, on average, half of his shots. Where the senior really shines is his versatility. Since he’s so dependable, Reed has been used all over the floor for a Penn State unit that’s been extremely thin at times.

“Just coming into practice every day with an open mind, wanting to learn, and watching a lot of film, trying to learn from that as well,” Reed said on Nov. 10. “Then just trusting our coaches, trusting the game plan, knowing that they know best and they want what's best for us, but also just trusting in each other, and we know we'll be able to get it.”

Trusting the game plan is a big part of Reed’s emergence; he’s been thrust into the spotlight with the Nittany Lions. Recently, he replaced 7-footer Ivan Jurić in the starting unit after Jurić came down with the flu.

“It was actually pretty fun getting to play at a center spot. I feel like the way we played, it was a little bit different. I feel like that helped us,” Reed said on Feb. 3. “It was definitely fun doing that, something I've never done before, so maybe we could do it again sometime.”

Lucky for Reed, he’s been called to do this more than once this season, and there’s no reason to suggest the senior can’t handle the physicality, nearly doubling his rebounding average from last season to a figure that only trails Jurić on the season at four per game.

“I commend Josh Reed for playing multiple positions all year long at 30-40 lbs out of position, Rhoades said. “ And giving up that weight, and then playing 30, sometimes 35 minutes plus. And he's had a great year battling and getting better as the year went on.”

Reed’s been able to do whatever is asked, and he is in the midst of that hot streak of form. In his last 12 games, Reed scored double-digit points, the longest streak of his collegiate career, and flirted with his career-high of 19 points scored in the first round of the Big-12 tournament last season against Oklahoma State.

Eventually, in a disappointing home loss to Rutgers, Reed finally hit his mark, tying for a game-high 22 points on 70% shooting, including some big three-pointers, setting a new personal best.

For a unit competing in the new Big Ten Tournament format with all 18 teams participating, Reed leads Penn State with the most postseason experience, and his leadership and experience will definitely be cherished for a Penn State unit that has a chance to make a wave with nothing to lose.

Nothing better represents that sentiment than Reed, who will be moving on to the next chapter of his basketball story after this season. While brief, he left his mark in program history with a year to remember in his career and an influence on a group that needed it more than ever.

“Josh and Kayden. They have stepped up throughout the year as being, being the best on the court, leaders and communicators and connectors, those two,” Rhoades said. “Those two guys, they're guys we need to play at high levels, but they also have to help them take care of their teammates as well, and throughout the years, they've done it. They've done it the right way.”

Fans in the Bryce Jordan Center may not have much to root for amid a disappointing season, and haven’t echoed those MVP chants as Cincinnati did for Reed, despite him actually deserving those praises for the blue and white.

While just a blip, Reed has left his mark on Penn State basketball history, and his effect on the sprawling, youthful Nittany Lions cannot be understated.

For a school that consistently struggles with identity and narratives surrounding the hardwood, the senior stands in the face of that, with full confidence that the past doesn’t define the future.

With Penn State’s Senior Night upcoming against Ohio State, Reed will soon see his praises sung as the lone player on the Nittany Lions to be honored for the occasion.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: Iowa State transfer LBs embracing Penn State's rich tradition of being ‘LBU’
author: Lyle Alenstein | Wrestling Editor
description:
In Iowa State’s 20-17 win over TCU this past season, linebackers Cael Brezina, Caleb Bacon and Kooper Ebel recreated the viral meme of three Spider-Men pointing at each other in a circle.

So when the trio transferred to Penn State, Brezina wanted the photo edited to have the trio wearing Penn State jerseys with “LBU” at the top in white block letters. The ex-Cyclone linebackers all collaborated on an Instagram post with the graphic.

“We had that picture from the TCU game that we did the Spider-Man meme. We thought that was really funny,” Bacon said. “So we were like, might as well turn it into something cool that would commemorate what this program has done here at Penn State with developing linebackers.”

Each of the players had brief familiarity with “LBU” before they arrived. Brezina was aware of Micah Parsons and Abdul Carter wearing the No. 11. Ebel brought up LaVar Arrington.

But they quickly needed to learn more about the reputation of playing linebacker at Penn State as the next in line of players to represent the position.

“When I first got here, and I walked him down the big old hallway in our facility, there's a long line of All-Americans, first-team All-Americans,” Ebel said. “If you look up there, it feels like just about every other one of them is a linebacker.”

One of those former All-Americans is Dan Connor, someone Ebel said he remembers watching play alongside Hall of Fame linebacker Luke Kuechly with the Carolina Panthers.

Connor stuck around on staff despite seeing his role go from linebackers coach to the assistant in the group. All three of the Iowa State transfers said he’s helped them learn more about the history of the room, and they believe he’ll be able to help them in plenty of other areas as time goes on.

“I think he'll be super important when we start playing football, techniques to use, fundamentals, because he's done it at the highest level,” Brezina said. “He also knows what it means to play here and play at a high level here. I think that's really helpful and important. He’s a good dude, knows the area really well, so he's been a good resource to have.”

The threesome combined for 174 total tackles as the top linebackers for Iowa State in 2025. They project to once again see a key role on the defense, with Tony Rojas spearheading the unit.

Regardless of who clocks the most snaps, the relationship they built in Ames, Iowa, leaves them hopeful of building off each other and their new teammates to play for “LBU,” potentially becoming the next greats to play the position at Penn State.

“The three of us have done literally everything together for the last three years,” Ebel said. “So that's been awesome. We're super grateful and privileged to be able to stay together and come to Penn State, and we look forward to competing and meshing with the linebacker room here.”

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State women’s basketball closes season with trip to Indiana
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
On a three-game winning streak and playing the best basketball it has played all year, Penn State has one final shot to play into March.

The Lady Lions will travel to Bloomington to face Indiana at 2 p.m. on Saturday.

With its postseason chances hanging in the balance, Penn State looks to book its ticket to the Big Ten championship with a win, instead of relying on Purdue to lose to Northwestern on Sunday.

All-time matchups

The Lady Lions lead the Hoosiers all-time, with a 33-21 record against them.

Starting with their first matchup in 1982, the Lady Lions earned a win, followed by a loss the following year. For the next nine games, Penn State found the victory column, and did so seven more times after just its second loss came in 1998.

For the past eight years, however, Indiana has had total control of the blue and white, winning eight straight games — five coming by double digits.

Last year, Penn State opened its Big Ten season with a 75-60 loss against Indiana in early December. With only four bench points, the Lady Lions struggled in the second half as the Hoosiers came out of halftime and took a 15-point lead to win the game.

Outside of Gracie Merkle’s 16 points, Moriah Murray is the only other returner to score, recording just four points.

Shay Ciezki, the former Lady Lion who sits second in the Big Ten with 23.0 points per game this year, scored just five points last season’s matchup and will look to do a lot more damage on Saturday.

2025-26 Hoosiers

Like Penn State, Indiana had a strong start to its nonconference schedule, winning the first seven games before dropping its only out-of-conference matchup to Iowa State to end November.

However, their Big Ten schedule was very uneventful for the first month, as the Hoosiers lost their first ten conference games before defeating Northwestern on Feb. 1.

From there, Indiana won three games straight against the Wildcats, Wisconsin and Purdue, before losing to both USC and UCLA in its trip to California.

The Hoosiers have won two straight coming into Saturday’s game, upsetting Oregon and beating Rutgers on the road as they hope to keep their tournament hopes alive as well. With a win on Saturday, Indiana would clinch a Big Ten tournament appearance, but would need Purdue to lose if the Lady Lions emerge victorious.

Saturday’s matchup

In addition to the high intensity with the postseason on the line, Saturday will also be a showcase of two of the most elite athletes in the conference.

Kiyomi McMiller is coming off six straight 30-point games, tied for the best in Big Ten history with Iowa legend Caitlin Clark. Another hot performance would put her in the conference record books in just her sophomore season.

However, Ciezki is having a dominant season with Indiana and will have the last opportunity to face her old team on Saturday.

Ciezki is a projected third-round pick in the WNBA draft after skyrocketing this year. After averaging roughly 11 points per game her first three seasons, she doubled that in her final year with 23.0 points per game.

Both teams have their season on the line on Saturday, and will play with everything they have in the final game of the year.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: Gen Z is becoming nonchalant. Students, professors weigh in on why
author: Alli Satterfield | The Daily Collegian
description:
Whether tossing garbage in a trash can or talking to crushes online, Gen Z — a group which includes many Penn State students — really wants to be nonchalant.

The concept and act of being nonchalant, which is defined by Merriam Webster as “having an air of easy unconcern or indifference,” is a social media trend being put into action amongst young people. But, the question is why.

Some consider it a response to social pressure and online trends, while others speculate it may be a deeper psychological response. Celina Casapulla said she thinks the latter may be the case.

“I feel like people want to make it seem like they don’t care, so they’re not as bothered by things,” Casapulla, a fourth-year studying biology, said.

She said her peers use their nonchalance as a hardened emotional front. But, that’s not a behavior she can relate to, since Casapulla describes herself as “very chalant.”

Kaydee Oliver identifies similarly, and said she doesn’t see herself in the dismissive behavior of her peers. Oliver said the nonchalant trend is much more male-focused as well, and she credits the behavior to a desire to seem more masculine.

But, Oliver, a fourth-year studying kinesiology, said that isn’t her idea of masculinity.

“I feel like (showing emotion) is part of what makes us human,” Oliver said. “I think that people don’t realize that masculinity also means that you should show emotion.”

Jeff Brown said the trend isn’t new, and cites the nonchalance of Gen X as an example.

“There was this sort of common understanding that Gen Xers were checked out in a way that boomers, the generation before them, weren't,” Brown, an assistant teaching professor of biobehavioral health, said. “At some level, I think that too reflects a trend. It’s thought to be uncool to be too earnest about things, to engage.”

What’s considered to be uncool though isn’t totally decided in each of our brains, Brown said. That’s decided by a vast array of social factors, and he said we ultimately decide to do what rewards us.

Some rewards are physical, some practical and others grant us social rewards. This social reward typically comes in the form of validation or support from our peers, he said.

“You gain a degree of social capital by at least pretending, if not actually believing, that you shouldn’t be taking things too seriously,” Brown said. “Whether it’s academic obligations or social obligations, (it’s cool to) take a chill attitude towards everything.”

He also said nonchalance can be tied to addiction. A motivational syndrome, that’s specifically been tied to cannabis use, can make users more nonchalant about their daily activities because their addiction reduces dopamine signaling, and typically-positive stimuli don’t have the same appeal as their addictive behavior, according to Brown.

Since dopamine flow decreases, motivation generally does the same. Whatever motivation is left becomes disproportionately focused on whatever you’ve become addicted to. Brown said these addictions aren’t just what we typically think of either. Social media use can be a sort of addiction, and platforms can take advantage of that.

“They realize that the people who are most susceptible to paying for whatever is being advertised on their services, are people who are habitually compulsively using their platforms, maybe because they are addicted to using those platforms,” Brown said. “So, the lack of motivation kind of ties into addictive behavior.”

While it can be an issue, that doesn’t mean that all nonchalant students are addicts. Caring about what’s cool is just a part of growing up, Brown said.

MORE LIFESTYLE CONTENT

title: From the dairy farm to Penn State: Australian punter Nathan Tiyce lands in Happy Valley
author: Avery Hill | Football Editor
description:
Starting when he was 12 years old, Nathan Tiyce rode a push bike in his hometown of Gerringong, Australia, slightly south of Sydney to tend to calves at a dairy farm. He then spent five years working on a cropping farm. What he expected to be his future of working in agriculture is far from what'd come next.

“I have a bit of a different story to most others,” Tiyce told the Daily Collegian. “I didn’t play professional sports back in Australia, didn’t make it to high levels, I literally was working on a farm, seeing a post on Instagram of college football and decided I’ll give it a shot, and next minute I’m over here.”

The post was from Australian punter Josh Growden, who played at LSU, highlighting the Saints’ signing of Lou Hedley as an undrafted free agent in 2023 at age 30. Soon after, Tiyce emailed Prokick Australia, a training program for Australian punters, before moving eight hours away to train in Melbourne, Australia, for 16 months.

He’s now in the United States as a punter for a program he considered “gold” when he first learned about college football.

“107,000 people, the White Out game, the traditions they have here is just, it's well known,” Tiyce said. “It’s not just in America.”

Tiyce transferred into the Nittany Lions this offseason after one season at Mississippi State.

Playing rugby and in the Australian Football League, an Australian rules sport, the only punting he did was laying down drop punts. His year with the Bulldogs, where he averaged 39 yards per punt on 12 attempts, was his first real taste of American football.

Making the transition to college football, Tiyce had to adjust to the physical game and the intricacies that came with it.

“The detail that goes into a football team, like people outside, people that clean the facilities, to people that stock all the inventory,” Tiyce said. “It’s so much bigger than you can even fathom back in Australia.”

Part of this detail is the business side of football, one that landed Tiyce at Penn State.

While Prokick handled most of his portal recruitment, he "instantly" said yes when he found out special teams coach Justin Lustig and the Nittany Lions came calling.

“To not take that opportunity would just be like I’m wasting my time,” Tiyce said. “I couldn’t not accept it.”

Tiyce is set to follow in the footsteps of Penn State’s recently departed Australian punter, Riley Thompson. Thompson has been around the facility and has advised Tiyce to take advantage of the opportunity.

Before meeting in State College, Tiyce didn’t know Thompson personally, but he was familiar with him as the three-year Penn State leg that also came through Prokick.

You see, with Thompson joining the likes of Hedley, Michael Dickson, Cameron Johnston, Mitch Wishnowsky and many other Australians to play college football, Tiyce takes being a punter from his native continent very seriously.

“The people who have come before me, they’ve set this absolute status for what an Australian punter should do,” Tiyce said. “So me trying to live up to that name of being an Australian punter and being good is a sense of pride.”

Growing up on an acre of land, Tiyce embraces his humble beginnings, which he noted to be a bit similar to the small town he played in last year of Starkville, Mississippi.

But Penn State’s a little different. He’s now received the typical Happy Valley, recently attending his first ice hockey game alongside Lustig, and getting a taste of the renowned Penn State Berkey Creamery — a place he said is “awesome.”

But there’s still a point of adjustment. As he settled into Happy Valley, he bought himself a car. He admitted he’s driven on the wrong side of the road a few times, but he said he’s now all good.

“I’m sweet now,” Tiyce said.

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State track and field's Maddie Pitts breaks pentathlon school record
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
In her final indoor Big Ten championship, Maddie Pitts earned a pair of honors for Penn State.

Pitts finished third for her third straight bronze medal, breaking the school record with 4,384 points in the process.

The senior multi-event athlete earned a personal best in the 60-meter hurdles, high jump and long jump in the pentathlon, recording a time of 8.34 seconds and distances of 1.67 meters and 6.24 meters, respectively.

Pitts will compete in the individual 60m hurdles at 6:10 p.m. on Friday, before taking her talents to the NCAA championship in March.

MORE TRACK AND FIELD COVERAGE

title: Former Penn State wrestler Jeremy Hunter named Illinois' wrestling head coach
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
Another former Nittany Lion will be a head coach in Division I wrestling.

Jeremy Hunter was named the new coach of Illinois after 25 years with the program as an assistant coach and one season as the interim head coach.

Hunter had a storied wrestling career at Penn State. In his four seasons from 1997-2000, Hunter held a 123-17 record, including a 33-1 record in his senior season en route to a national title at 125 pounds.

He earned Big Ten Wrestler of the Year in 2000, was a Big Ten Champion in 1999 and earned Big Ten Freshman of the Year in 1997. He was a three-time All-American, notching the honor in his final three seasons.

After graduating from Penn State in 2000, Hunter was named an assistant coach at Illinois in 2001. He's been on the Fighting Illini staff since 2001 and was a crucial part of Lucas Byrd's 2025 National Championship win at 133 pounds.

Now, he'll take over as the head coach of an Illinois team that was near the top of the Big Ten standings this season with him as interim coach.

MORE WRESTLING COVERAGE

title: Report: Penn State assistant QBs coach Trace McSorley leaves for Buffalo Bills
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State is reportedly set to lose another coach to the NFL.

Assistant quarterbacks coach Trace McSorley departed the program for a position with the Buffalo Bills, according to Daniel Gallen of 247Sports.

McSorley was the Nittany Lions' assistant quarterbacks coach for one season. He'll join the staff of Bills head coach Joe Brady, who was a graduate assistant at Penn State from 2015-16.

McSorley played for Penn State from 2014-18, appearing in 47 games across his career. He tallied 9,899 passing yards, 77 touchdowns and 25 interceptions in addition to 473 rushing attempts for 1,697 yards and 30 scores.

The former Nittany Lion was a sixth-round selection in the 2019 NFL draft to the Baltimore Ravens. He spent time with the Arizona Cardinals, New England Patriots, Chicago Bears, Pittsburgh Steelers and Washington Commanders as well, last getting released by the Commanders ahead of the 2024 season.

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Notebook | Mike Rhoades discusses roster retention, announces Tibor Mirtič will be out for season
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Mike Rhoades was back in the Bryce Jordan Center media room for his weekly press conference on Thursday, discussing a variety of topics.

Rhoades spoke on some program topics with the offseason coming up, while also announcing that forward Tibor Mirtič is out for the season.

Here’s more of what Rhoades said.

Roster retention

With the regular season nearing its end, Rhoades said he’s had conversations with players regarding their futures with Penn State, but he doesn’t yet have a good idea of who might be returning to the program, citing how fluid roster retention can be.

“A guy has a career high, does that change how he's thinking? All that, and then there's the outside stuff, too," Rhoades said. "I mean, there's people that are representing them and they're getting their intel and info. So to me, it's conversations. You got to have sincere, truthful conversations with your players.”

The most notable roster retention effort the Nittany Lions have will be guard Kayden Mingo, who’s averaging 13.9 points, 4.4 assists and 2.2 steals per game as a freshman. Mingo’s younger brother, 2026 5-star Dylan Mingo, committed to UNC, and rumors exist that they could reunite while on the Tar Heels next season.

In addition to that, only senior forward Josh Reed will run out of eligibility after this season. In other words, Rhoades could retain up to 12 players on the current roster.

“To me, I've said this and always say this, it's all about relationships so you can have real conversations,” Rhoades said. “The one thing about me is I'm going to speak in truths all the time. Everybody knows that. I'm not going to paint in any different way. So we'll have those conversations. We've had them. We're going to continue to move forward with that. Move forward with them.”

Financial resources

Rhoades was asked if he has enough financial resources this offseason to acquire the talent needed for the program to take the next step. He said that he does.

“That's what's been great about talking with Pat Kraft and having great conversations with him and his staff and all that stuff,” Rhoades said. “Pat wants to do right by us, and then wants to help us, and that's the direction we're trying to push to go to.”

During the 2024-25 fiscal year, Penn State Athletics reported it spent $3,004,666 on NIL revenue share for the men’s basketball team. The Nittany Lions went 16-15 and 6-14 in Big Ten play last season.

With Rhoades mentioning Penn State not always being at the same level as other Big Ten programs, he highlighted the importance of being correct in player evaluations.

“You got to be right getting the right guys on the board,” Rhoades said. “We've taken pride as a staff of finding those guys that have turned into special players and pros, and they might not be the sexy recruit at the time, but then they turn into one. You got to be okay with that.”

Tibor Mirtič out for season

Forward Tibor Mirtič hasn’t played in the last 13 games due to injury, and Rhoades confirmed Thursday that he doesn’t anticipate getting him back this season.

“He’ll be done,” Rhoades said.

Mirtič exited the Michigan game on Jan. 6 with a knee injury. While rehabbing that injury, he injured his shoulder during his first practice back. He was a rotational player in the frontcourt when he did see the court, averaging 3.7 points and 3.3 rebounds per game.

Mirtič is listed as a freshman on Penn State’s roster, but he was given two years of eligibility by the NCAA as he’s a 2003-born player who has professional experience overseas in his home country of Slovenia. Since he played in 15 games in 2025-26, it doesn’t appear he would be able to attain a medical redshirt for this season.

Week off

Penn State had more time off this week as it didn’t have an early-week game. After getting back from Nebraska late Saturday night, the team took Sunday and Monday off.

“We had a crazy stretch there, but everybody does, so no excuses, but just letting the guys be able to get their bodies to calm down a little bit, get some real sleep, some longer sleep, just get to some type of normalcy here on campus I think was great for them,” Rhoades said. “Having those two days off as good has been very good for them.”

The Nittany Lions were back practicing on Tuesday, which was a "great practice,” according to Rhoades. Penn State had Wednesday off as well, but players were still in the weight room, training room or the gym getting shots up.

It’ll be another day of practice on Thursday before the Iowa game tips off at noon Saturday in the Bryce Jordan Center.

“Just getting rid of some of the fatigue that we had to endure here the last couple weeks,” Rhoades said.

MORE BASKETBALL CONTENT

title: Mock draft roundup | Where are Penn State players projected ahead of 2026 NFL Scouting Combine?
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
The 2026 NFL Scouting Combine is underway this week, giving nine former Penn State players the chance to showcase their skills.

Those Nittany Lions will also have the opportunity to boost their NFL draft stock as scouts evaluate where they might place them on their big board.

With the pre-draft process continuing, here’s where the latest mock drafts have ex-Penn Staters getting selected.

Vega Ioane, left guard, 1st round

Left guard Vega Ioane has multiple first-round projections with ESPN and NBC Sports each expecting him to go No. 22 overall to the Los Angeles Chargers. PFF has a mock draft of Ioane going No. 14 to the Baltimore Ravens. The Draft Network slots him in at No. 24 to the Cleveland Browns.

Ioane was a starter for the Nittany Lions the past two seasons. In 2025, he played in 11 games before achieving second-team All-America honors for his efforts.

Zakee Wheatley, safety, mid rounds

Safety Zakee Wheatley has Day 2 potential in mock drafts, with PFF predicting a Round 3 pick from the Los Angeles Rams. Sports Illustrated projects a Round 3 pick to the Chicago Bears for Wheatley, while The Draft Network expects Round 4 to the Las Vegas Raiders.

Wheatley was the veteran presence in Penn State's safety group this past season, tallying 74 tackles, two tackles for loss, an interception and a pass breakup.

Dani Dennis-Sutton, defensive end, rounds 2-4

Though early indications gave a first-round grade to Dani Dennis-Sutton, a recent mock draft by A to Z Sports sees Dennis-Sutton getting drafted in the third round at pick No. 79. PFF’s pre-combine mock draft has him going No. 84 to the Green Bay Packers.

Dennis-Sutton’s calling card is his ability to cause disruption in the backfield, totaling 42 tackles, 12 tackles for loss, 8.5 sacks and three forced fumbles in 2025.

Drew Allar, quarterback, mid rounds

Drew Allar has a wide range for where he is getting selected in mock drafts with NFL.com projecting him going in Round 3 at No. 76 overall to the Pittsburgh Steelers while Saturday Blitz has him going No. 209 overall to the Los Angeles Rams. ESPN ranks him as the No. 3 quarterback, and he’s the No. 138 overall player by PFF.

Allar endured a season-ending ankle injury during 2025 but has recovered and will throw in the combine, according to Adam Schefter of ESPN. He’s passed for 7,402 yards, 61 touchdowns and 13 interceptions in his Penn State career.

Nick Singleton, running back, mid rounds

Running back Nick Singleton was projected to the Denver Broncos at pick No. 94 overall by BroncosWire while PFF expects him to get selected by the Philadelphia Eagles at pick No. 98 overall. He’s the No. 120 player overall by PFF and the No. 8 running back by ESPN.

Singleton broke his foot during a practice at the Senior Bowl and isn’t expected to participate in on-field drills at the combine. He became Penn State’s all-time leading touchdown scorer last season with 55 career touchdowns.

Kaytron Allen, running back, mid rounds

Running back Kaytron Allen ranges from late Day 2 to early Day 3 in mock drafts as the Denver Post and Sports Illustrated has him going in Round 3 to the Denver Broncos and Jacksonville Jaguars, respectively. Clutch Points also expects Allen to go in Round 4.

Allen is the Nittany Lions’ all-time leading rusher with 4,180 career yards, including 1,303 yards during the 2025 season.

Drew Shelton, left tackle, mid rounds

Left tackle Drew Shelton is another mid-round prospect in mock drafts with Draftwire projecting him to get selected in the third round. Vikings Wire has Shelton going in the fifth round to the Minnesota Vikings and Bleacher Report grades Shelton as a fifth-round prospect.

Shelton was a mainstay on the offensive line last season, starting every regular season game before opting out of the Pinstripe Bowl.

Zane Durant, defensive tackle, mid rounds

Defensive tackle Zane Durant has garnered Day 3 buzz in mock drafts as The Draft Network has him going in Round 4 to the Pittsburgh Steelers. Bleacher Report gave Durant a fourth round grade.

Durant appeared in all 12 regular season games last year, tallying 25 tackles, 4.5 tackles for loss, four sacks and an interception.

Khalil Dinkins, tight end, late rounds-undrafted

Tight end Khalil Dinkins is ranked as the No. 188 overall player by Bleacher Report and No. 398 overall player by PFF ahead of on-field drills at the combine.

Dinkins was known for his blocking in both the run and pass game during his time as a Nittany Lion. He had 14 catches for 167 yards and two touchdowns in 2025 as well.

Nolan Rucci, offensive tackle, late rounds-undrafted

Offensive tackle Nolan Rucci wasn’t invited to the combine, but his name popped up in a mock draft by Last Word on Sports, projecting him getting selected in the sixth round by the Detroit Lions.

Rucci was another starter on the offensive line for Penn State in 2025, appearing in all 12 regular season games.

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

Vikings Wire has Shelton going in the fifth round to the Minnesota Vikings and Bleacher Report grades Shelton as a fifth-round prospect.

title: No. 25 Penn State softball set for big test against No. 1 Tennessee
author: Ashley Beres | The Daily Collegian
description:
