title: QB Rocco Becht ready to ‘give everything’ for new home at Penn State
author: Lexie Linderman | The Daily Collegian
description:
After Rocco Becht decided he was going to call Penn State home, he called Derek Hoodjer and asked for every phone number the general manager had of his new teammates.

He texted each person Hoodjer gave him, and if Becht didn’t have a guy’s number, he made sure to introduce himself when he arrived in Happy Valley. The immediate initiative to begin building trust is just one way Becht has shown Penn State what kind of leadership it's getting in its new starting quarterback.

“(I asked them) what they thought of the program, who they were as a person, and what their background was,” Becht said. “It's more than just what they do on the field. It's what they did off the field, and where they came from, and just who they are as a person off the field, not just on the field. And I think that's what I pride myself by, and that's how I communicate with all the other guys, just to build that trust.”

When Becht first caught wind of Matt Campbell’s potential move to Penn State on social media, he thought it was a publicity stunt, considering the unwavering loyalty Campbell had to Iowa State for the last 10 years.

But the online buzz was real, and Becht now had a decision to make.

He called former Iowa State quarterback Brock Purdy to ask what he would do in this situation, who gave him a “great answer.” Ultimately, he decided to finish out his college career with a head coach he trusts despite drawing interest from other schools in the transfer portal.

“When he decided to move on and take this job, there was no doubt in my mind that, yes, Iowa State was another home for me, but that's the guy I committed to, and that's the guy I wanted to play for, and so he's the reason why I'm ultimately here,” Becht said. “I'm super excited that I'm here, being a part of this program and being a part of his new journey.”

The past two months have been “stressful and exciting” for Becht as he adjusts to his new reality — one that includes many more cameras and microphones waiting for him at a table than usual. He embraced it with a smile as he met with the media for the first time dressed in blue and white on Wednesday.

Becht’s enjoying all of it. The people inside Lasch Building are the highlight for him so far, but he said the food has been pretty great too.

Above all, Becht is excited for the chance to turn things back around. Last season didn’t play out the way he had hoped after a strong 2024, partially due to a torn labrum in his non-throwing shoulder and an AC joint sprain in his throwing shoulder.

“It was really hard. It was probably the lowest place that I've been in in my collegiate career, mentally and physically,” Becht said. “There's a four-game stretch when we lost those four games that was pretty tough on me, and I had to look at my family and some friends and talk to them and to kind of get myself out of it.”

Becht is allowed to resume throwing this Saturday and should be able to participate in the latter half of spring ball. He’s excited to get back out onto the field and compete, as right now he’s helping lead the quarterback room, directing traffic with the routes loaded on his phone.

And in his last season of collegiate ball, Becht isn’t necessarily worried about what lies ahead — he just wants to get back the magic he felt in 2024.

“Just go back to my (2024) season, and that's kind of just what I reminisce on,” Becht said. “I want that excitement back. I want that ability where I had fun no matter what, I want that back. I think that's kind of what I'm gaining towards, what I'm growing towards is just having fun with the game again, and it's my last year, and I'm going to go out there and give everything that I have for my final season.”

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State baseball faces Indiana State in first ever NCAA matchup in Bahamas
author: Ashley Felsberg I The Daily Collegian
description:
On Friday, Penn State will travel to Nassau, Bahamas, to compete in the first-ever NCAA Division I matchup on the islands. Penn State will meet Indiana State in a three-game series from Feb. 27 to Mar. 1.

Penn State is coming off one win and two losses against Richmond in its three-game series, with the fourth game being canceled due to inclement weather.

Ahead of its series in Virginia, Penn State competed in the MLB Desert Invitational, ranging from Feb. 13 to Feb. 15. Penn State had a split showing, as it dominated against GCU, winning 9-3, but experienced blowouts against the other two teams, losing 15-3 against the Air Force Academy and 24-5 against Kansas State.

This invitational was the blue and white’s first showing of the 2026 season, after the additions of 11 freshmen prospects. Now, the Nittany Lions are 2-4 overall in the season, all six of their games being nonconference meetups.

Aside from the site, the matchup between Penn State and Indiana State is also much-awaited, as it is the first time in over 25 years that the two teams have met. Back in 2001, Penn State lost both games, 7-3 and 8-5. Prior to this, the two programs saw each other in 1994, where Penn State won 10-6.

Sophomore Ben Hudson, a transfer from West Virginia, made his debut on the mound in Penn State’s match against GCU. The Medford, New Jersey native was able to get six strikeouts under his belt, only allowing two hits.

Isaiah Shayter started on the bump in two games for the blue and white. In his first start against Kansas State, Shayter was removed after the third inning, giving up six runs. However, in the meetup with Richmond, Shayter only allowed two runs and struck out three batters with zero walks.

While the stakes are not too high this early in the season, this series against Indiana State is a crucial opportunity to turn around Penn State’s nonconference results early in the season.

Gaining nonconference momentum could prepare the Nittany Lions for their conference start against Iowa in the second week of March.

Indiana State is 3-5 on the 2026 season, coming off a recent 10-2 win on Sunday against UMass Lowell. Like Penn State, the Sycamores have only had nonconference matchups so far and will have their first conference game against Lindenwood on Mar. 4.

For both their losses and wins, Indiana State seems to either absolutely dominate or do quite the opposite. Each of the Sycamores' three wins has had them winning by a margin of seven runs or more, beating Manhattan 10-3, Miami Ohio 11-3 and UMass Lowell 10-2.

On the contrary, they’ve lost 10-4 against NC State and 10-1 to Wake Forest. They also lost to Miami and Brown; these games only had a run difference of four.

Last season, Indiana State finished 24-31 overall, with 12 conference wins. The Sycamores were the No.8-seed going into the Missouri Valley Conference; however, they lost in the first round to the No.5-seed, Illinois State.

Both teams have similar beginning season records and could use this non-conference game to add to their win tallies, ahead of their conference matchups commencing in early March.

title: Penn State notebook | No. 25 Penn State softball prepared for big weekend ahead
author: Gianna Dowling | The Daily Collegian
description:
Following an undefeated weekend in Orlando, No. 25 Penn State is set to take a trip to Knoxville, Tennessee, for its final nonconference weekend away before returning to Beard Field.

“Offensively, we had a great weekend, for the most part, the pitchers had a good showing, just happy about what we were able to do,” coach Clarisa Crowell said in Wednesday's media session. “Looking forward to carrying that positive momentum from this past weekend into this upcoming weekend when we go to Knoxville, because we have a big weekend coming up.”

That weekend consists of the Nittany Lions having to face host and No.1 Tennessee twice.

The blue and white’s offense has shown in the early portions of the season, totaling 121 runs thus far. Last weekend alone, Penn State outscored its opponents 53-11. Additionally, the Nittany Lions have tallied 20 home runs on the young season.

Penn State has built offensive success by consistently going 12 players deep and using every inch of its lineup to produce hits and runs.

“I think the great thing is that we've got kids on the bench that we can put in, and we've got some depth on the bench where we didn't, we didn't have a lot of that last year,” Crowell said. “Whenever you know, you can have run production from one through nine, it's a really big deal.”

One name that stands out in particular for the Nittany Lions is freshman infielder Allison Oneacre. The Alpharetta, Georgia, native, was named the Big Ten Freshman of the Week for the second straight time, becoming the first Penn Stater ever to be given a Big Ten honor in consecutive weeks.

Oneacre posted a .438 batting-average on the weekend, while scoring three runs and recording 11 RBIs. The rookie now has 27 RBIs to her name this season, putting her sixth nationally.

“(Oneacre) just a very, just a calm and composed kid, and just goes out there, every time she gets a hit, I'm like, man, there she goes again,” Crowell said. “She comes up in big moments, and, yeah, it's been pretty, pretty awesome to watch.”

However, in at least two of its games this upcoming weekend, Penn State’s approach at the plate might look a little bit different. In their second contests on both Friday and Saturday, the Nittany Lions will face the Lady Volunteers, who own the nation's lowest ERA at 0.32.

Tennessee has allowed just five runs this season, but hasn’t given up more than one in each game it's played.

“This isn't going to be a weekend, at least when we take on the Vols, that you're hitting balls all over the place, it's just their pitching is elite,” Crowell said. “We need to be better at situational hitting, so the bunts need to be better, we need to run some squeeze plays, we need to put runners in motion.”

Penn State’s confidence is what has driven its success in the early parts of the season, so while the competition this weekend gets stronger, the blue and white’s mindset always stays the same.

“I feel like our kids are very confident right now in who they are as individuals and who we are as a team,” Crowell said. “They're going to show up and they're going to be ready to play.”

While the Lady Volunteers are the toughest opponent the Nittany Lions have faced so far, they’re not the only tall task at hand for Penn State this weekend.

The blue and white also face an 11-2 Appalachian State team in its early Saturday afternoon slate. The Mountaineers post a .300 batting-average, while also owning a 2.61 ERA from the circle.

“There's also App State, and those guys are legit,” Crowell said. “That is a very, very good team that has been playing well since last fall when they took down Duke.”

Penn State is prepared for the big weekend ahead, with a central focus on its own success.

“We prepare for teams, but the focus has to be on us and how we play,” Crowell said. “I think our kids do a great job of preparing and just playing Penn State softball.”

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: SEE IT: Penn State opens up the Jeffrey Field Soccer Complex after years of renovations
author: Liana Tiamzon I For the Collegian
description:
Penn State’s Jeffrey Field on-campus soccer stadium is the centerpiece of the Nittany Lions’ soccer facilities. Where both the men’s and women’s soccer teams play their home matches in the BIG10 Conference.

Opened in 1972 and named after legendary Penn State coach Bill Jeffrey, who won the Nittany Lions 26 seasons, winning ten national college championships as well as being a member of the National Soccer Hall of Fame.

The complex was set to undergo major renovations during the first phase of the university’s athletic master plan. These major renovations have come to a reality as the complex now includes new home and visiting locker rooms, upgraded training spaces, strength and conditioning, as well as hydrotherapy areas.

Introducing the Jeffrey Field Soccer Complex. Home of Penn State Soccer.🏠⚽️#WeAre pic.twitter.com/zT9LLV8fGy— Penn State Athletics (@GoPSUsports) February 25, 2026

Visitors have new things to look forward to as the complex now has covered chairback seating on the west side, expanded concessions and restrooms. New video boards, improved media facilities and upgraded lighting throughout the complex.

For every alum who built it. For every future Nittany Lion who will raise it higher. This is the next chapter of Penn State Soccer. #WeAre pic.twitter.com/Y7p3Z8ODA5— Penn State Athletics (@GoPSUsports) February 25, 2026

Jeffrey Field regularly hosts NCAA tournament games and even conference championships, and can now hold more fans as the renovated venue will have an estimated seating capacity of about 4,000 fans.

MORE SOCCER COVERAGE

title: Perfect or disappointing? | ‘Tell Me Lies’ finale reactions
author: Skylar Williamson I The Daily Collegian
description:
Every now and then a new TV show sparks up group chat chaos. The series finale of “Tell Me Lies” has left viewers split, with some praising its realism and others questioning whether it delivers a satisfying ending.

“Tell Me Lies,” created for television by Meaghan Oppenheimer and streaming on Hulu, explores the themes of manipulation, trauma bonding and blurred moral boundaries through intense — and primarily toxic — relationships and friendships. These dynamics start in the characters’ college years and shape them throughout the show.

The drama, which primarily follows the hectic relationship between characters Lucy Albright and Stephen DeMarco, played by Grace Van Patten and Jackson White respectively, concluded its third and final season last Tuesday. With unresolved tension and emotional fallout, the reactions reflect the broader debate surrounding the show.

As the season barreled toward its finale, tensions escalated between characters, raising expectations for a dramatic resolution. Instead, the finale left room for interpretation — a creative choice that seemed to divide viewers.

The last episode saw the friend group five years after college at characters Bree and Evan's wedding. During the final minutes of the reception, Stephen takes the microphone and announces everyone’s messy secrets, ruining the day.

Kayleigh Alicea felt the season finale happened too early, leaving room for plenty of unresolved situations.

“I think we were owed at least one more episode if not more than that,” Alicea, a fourth-year studying forensic science, said. “The resolution happened too fast, and it felt rushed. It wasn’t what I expected, even though it was definitely entertaining.”

Some students enjoyed the ending, commending the writer’s decision for the abrupt finale. For Brianna Scotto, the episode was a nice wrap up for the three-season show.

“I honestly thought the ending was perfect,” Scotto, a second-year studying accounting, said. “I understand where people were coming from for not liking it, but I think it was really creative how they tied a majority of the show back together.”

Scotto said she realized many things from previous seasons were left unsaid, creating mixed feelings on the final episode.

Emily Wright had the same thoughts as Scotto and said the storyline included too many horrific events that were never resolved, which in her opinion the finale should have touched on.

In the first episode of the series, Lucy’s roommate, Macy, dies in a drunk driving accident. She’s found in the driver’s seat with no one else in the vehicle.

As the show progresses, Lucy finds out Stephen was in the car with Macy but fled the scene. The audience discovers Stephen was not only in the car but the one driving it, yet none of the characters ever uncover this.

“On top of all the other horrible things Stephen did to Lucy and his friends, the whole Macy situation should’ve been addressed,” Wright, a fourth-year studying biobehavioral health, said. “It threw me off how he got away free of consequences for pretty much everything he did.”

The debate on the finale has been particularly visible on social media platforms. Specifically on TikTok, fans are creating edits romanticizing characters, making videos criticizing manipulative behavior and fighting battles in comment sections on whether the episode was good or not.

There is an overwhelming amount of questions about why a majority of the traumatic events weren’t concluded differently, including: a professor’s intimate relationships with students, sexual assault allegations and emotional abuse.

People online are also finding humor in the way Stephen blew up his friends’ wedding, and some said this is the first time in the show he was right in his actions.

The final scene of the episode shows Lucy getting in the car with Stephen after the failed wedding. Though the two haven't been in an official relationship since college, the scene signals that Lucy is still trapped in the cynical cycle Stephen created for her.

However, after stopping at a gas station to get coffee, Stephen deserts her, and Lucy just laughs. Though many other moments were highly contested, the overall consensus seems to be this scene was the perfect way to end the show.

“After I let the episode sink in, I realized this might’ve been the whole point to the show,” Alicea said. “Even though I wanted more, it makes sense that the bad people didn’t get consequences and it was forgotten about because, a lot of the time, that happens in the real world.”

People on social media are interpreting Lucy’s laugh as a symbolism for her freedom. She’s finally free from Stephen after she “let him win,” or she simply realized that’s how he’ll always be.

Van Patten said in an Entertainment Tonight interview that she’s “obsessed with the ending” and Lucy, after that moment, has finally accepted Stephen won’t change.

“Tell Me Lies” ended with truths being exposed, but many lies being swept under the rug.

Despite the divide, the episode generated conversation. Whether viewers found it satisfying or disappointing, the episode reinforced the show’s ability to provoke strong reactions — the whole point of a controversial, traumatic and somewhat real-to-life show.

MORE LIFESTYLE CONTENT

title: Thank you Black America
author: Kahlie Wray | Managing Editor
description:
At the 2026 Grammys on Sunday, Nigerian artist Shaboozey said “immigrants built this country,” as he accepted the award for Best Country Duo/Group Performance with the singer Jelly Roll. The statement drew swift backlash on social media platforms, with some viewers arguing it was dismissive of the reality that enslaved Africans built this country.

Others accused his critics of fanning the flames of diaspora wars, a term coined to describe the rise in online conversations promoting division. Participants harp on the supposed differences between Afro-Caribbeans, Africans and Black Americans, taking the stance the groups are not aligned politically or culturally.

Though I find these conversations foolish and unnecessarily divisive, they’re not wholly unwarranted. They stem from real opinions people hold about the perceived work ethic of Black Americans vs immigrants and the different cultural values of the group.

It would be dishonest of me to say that I haven’t heard some Jamaicans stereotype Black Americans as “ghetto” or “lazy,” despite the systemic barriers they’ve faced for centuries.

I understand why people might find Shaboozey’s comment disrespectful and dismissive.

But as ICE raids ramp up across the nation and response protests become commonplace, the topic of immigration is critical. It’s an especially personal one for Shaboozey who is a child of immigrants. It’s an especially personal one for me, an immigrant.

Bright-eyed and bushy tailed, I came to the U.S. with notions of what my life would look like. Though I knew I’d miss the consistent warm weather, easy access to cultural dishes and close proximity to the friends and family that shaped me, I was genuinely excited to come to the United States.

Disney Channel had prepared me for a world of Friday night lights, soft, powdery snow that landed on the tip of my nose softly and Thanksgiving, a holiday I once thought existed only inside my television screen.

What it did not prepare me for was the reality of being Black in America. It didn’t prepare me for late-night conversations about police brutality my father had with me and my brothers following the murder of George Floyd by a police officer. Disney Channel could not comfort me as I became increasingly aware that the color of my skin was a threat to some people.

But Black Americans could and they did. The Black girls in my biology class didn’t allow our white peers to poke and prod at me. They shut down all conversations that made a mockery of my accent or my heritage. In English classes when I dared to critique teachers' shallow interpretations of Black literature, they rushed to my defense when classmates insisted “it wasn’t that deep.”

They never left me out to dry when I went to war with teachers about the fraught politics of immigration and being Black in America. When I strolled into the high school homecoming dance, with my afro as big as I could make it, my Black peers were the first (and only) ones to tell me my hair was beautiful. For years, I attended schools with discriminatory policies about Black hair, and their compliments have stuck with me.

I understand what Shaboozey meant. And I understand what his critics meant, as well.

In this political climate, it's important for us to acknowledge the role of Black Americans in shaping this country. Everyone benefits from the labor of Black Americans. We’re enjoying privileges they fought for, died for, were killed for.

I can write this column because of them. I am writing this column because of them.

Because of the potent words of Chadwick Boseman “to be young, gifted and black,” I walk through this life with my head held high, reveling in the astute honor of my Blackness.

And for that I say, thank you Black America.

MORE COLUMNS

title: Column | What Alysa Liu can teach us this National Eating Disorders Awareness Week
author: Jadzia Santiago | The Daily Collegian
description:
Content warning: This column contains mention of eating disorders. Resources and support are available through Penn State’s Counseling and Psychological Services.

Alright, I won’t beat around the bush: National Eating Disorders Awareness Week is here.

Spanning from Feb. 23 to March 1 this year, the week is designed to raise awareness for all types of eating disorders and support the 30 million Americans who struggle with one, according to the National Eating Disorders Association.

Those numbers include 15-20% of college students, according to an article published by The Triple Helix at the University of Chicago.

It’s well-meaning and an important cause. And like with every day, week or month with “awareness” at the end, there’s a group of people who might wish they were less aware, due to their own personal struggles and history with the subject. This week, I’m part of that demographic.

Don’t get me wrong, lots of productive conversations can and have come out of this week every year. However, for every nuanced conversation and person educated, there’s seemingly an Instagram slideshow of hospital pictures, sick bodies, people fainting and graphic descriptions of disordered behaviors.

The way we talk about eating disorders places suffering at the forefront, which outwardly seems to make sense for a mental health condition. However, when placed into the context of our broader culture, that approach can have negative consequences.

This is where, finally, Alysa Liu comes in — the gold medal-winning ray of sunshine who recently lit up the Olympic ice and, if your feed is anything like mine, our screens.

The discussion around her and her attitude to her sport, like her skating, has been a joy to watch. As many have pointed out, including her, Liu genuinely and openly enjoys her craft and she does it on her own terms.

“No one is going to starve me, or tell me what I can and can’t eat,” she said in an interview with 60 Minutes. An X post quoting her is, at the time of writing, at nearly 85 thousand likes (one of those is mine.)

It’s clear from the response to this sentiment that Liu’s expression of joy and autonomy, and her subsequent success, aren’t what people are used to seeing. Their coexistence is a breath of fresh air, which begs the question: why?

Now, I’m not going to comment on figure skating culture, as many intelligent takes have already been put out into the world (by people who know, like, anything about figure skating). But I will say that in athletes and regular people alike, we’re so used to pairing success with deep and profound suffering.

Now, I’m not talking about the moderate misery that comes with working hard for something, which involves dedication, discipline and passion. I mean the grueling, soulless toil that sucks the life from your eyes, with absolutely no purpose behind it but the finish line, or beyond it.

I’ll admit that sometimes the lines get blurred between the two, especially in high-demand situations where it’s easy to forget what you’re doing it for. But sometimes it goes beyond that, to the point where it feels like your suffering is the very thing that earns your success.

So deeply ingrained is the association between suffering and reward that two thought processes seem to have emerged: first, that misery is necessary to achieve a goal, and second, that your success should be directly proportional to how much you’ve endured.

Apply this to the (usually) well-meaning and misguided slew of people flashing their sickness like a credential this week, and a reason why begins to emerge. With these cultural mentalities around suffering, people feel like they have to prove both that they were suffering and that they deserve not to suffer anymore.

When the burden is placed on the individual to show the world that they’ve “earned” recovery, it implies that there’s a ranking as to whose disorder deserves attention. Sure, there are some individuals that need higher levels of care than others, but as for who deserves a full life and a healthy relationship with food … not an exclusive club that suffering gets you into.

Pain alone is not your ticket out of a mental illness, and I’m sorry that that idea is so widespread.

Hell, we can’t even reliably measure “better” or “worse” based on highly-visible metrics like weight, because eating disorders are, above all, mental. So, we can put away the tape measure and the clothes sizes; there’s no points system.

This isn’t to say that recovery shouldn’t be hard. Peace and love, fighting against an eating disorder is inherently challenging in some way or another. I’d argue it’s damn hard work.

Going back to Alysa Liu, joy alone didn’t earn her a gold medal — but suffering sure didn’t, either.

As National Eating Disorders Awareness Week continues, I wonder if we can take this mentality into our conversations about eating disorder recovery.

What would happen if, when faced with the fork in the road between the struggle of recovery and the struggle of sickness, we decided that everyone is worth recovery, because it’s a struggle with purpose, with light at the end? What if we said, screw it, everyone deserves to live and fight for the love of the game? How would we approach the hard work of healing then?

Well, as Alysa Liu said in that same 60 Minutes interview I mentioned earlier: “I love struggling, actually. It makes me feel alive.”

MORE COLUMNS

title: Review | Feeling peckish? Big Chicken is here to help
author: Lily Dorf | The Daily Collegian
description:
A new chicken spot has opened up in downtown State College for students and locals to enjoy. Big Chicken, founded in 2018 by NBA star Shaquille O’Neal, opened on Thursday at 480 E. College Ave. The store is open from 11 a.m. to 9 p.m. every day.

This week, I ventured over to taste all that Big Chicken has to offer. Their menu consists of — you guessed it — chicken. They have a variety of chicken sandwiches, tenders and “Shaq Snacks,” which are chicken tenders served between garlic toast.

They also offer a little sweet treat to go along with your meal: six different milkshake flavors, from classics like vanilla and chocolate to peanut butter chip and salted caramel.

For those not in the mood for fried chicken, the establishment offers a grilled chicken option instead. If you’re wanting something spicier, there is also the option of dipping your order in Nashville Hot or Holy sauce.

For my first experience, I chose The Original chicken sandwich meal that comes with a side of fries and drink of choice.

The Original chicken sandwich

The look of the sandwich was aesthetically pleasing, with a large piece of chicken topped with pickles and their signature “BC sauce” sandwiched between two toasted brioche buns.

My first bite was good, but maybe even a little anticlimactic. The chicken itself is seasoned well, but has a similar taste to any other fast-food chicken sandwich. When I got a bite of everything altogether, it felt overwhelming, mostly due to the amount of pickles.

I would say a majority of my sandwich was covered in pickles, and though I am a big lover of them, the tang of the pickle juice took away from the chicken and the sauce on the sandwich.

To get a better understanding of what the sauce tasted like, I ordered a small side to taste alone. When I tried it, I was let down. While the sauce was flavorful and added a kick to the sandwich, it tasted indistinguishable from signature sauces offered at other fast food chains.

My overall thoughts on the sandwich are that it’s underwhelming to say the least. I thought the flavor of the chicken was balanced and paired well with the sauce, but the overpowering pickles took away from everything else.

Rating: 6/10

Fries

Every meal comes with a side of fries, so I wanted to judge them separately from the sandwich. Unlike the sandwich, which had seasoning and strong flavors, the fries were boring.

They tasted bland and ordinary, which is understandable since they offer “Dirty Fries,” which are covered in queso, chipotle barbecue sauce and banana peppers. However, I still wish they could have seasoned the fries with something other than just salt to make the fries more exciting and flavorful for those who do not want the extra toppings.

Overall, I was not impressed with the fries and think that if you wanted something with a little more bite, I would suggest one of the other sides they offer, like onion rings, fried cheese curds, elote and more.

Rating 5/10

Overall Thoughts

With a multitude of new businesses popping up in State College, I think Big Chicken is a middle-of-the-road option in terms of what they offer.

The restaurant boasts an upbeat atmosphere with the inside mirroring the colors of a basketball, a callback to its notable owner. It has a smaller but somewhat cozy sitting area with slogans and LED signs on the wall.

I was unfortunately unable to try the milkshakes, due to the store running out of them completely before I got there. The menu displays new and exciting options for chicken sandwiches. However, with the price rising for the specialty sandwiches, it would be nice for the basic chicken sandwich to offer more in terms of flavor for those who don’t want to spend over ten dollars on a meal or a late-night snack.

MORE LIFESTYLE CONTENT

title: Big Ten recap | Top seed clinched in women’s basketball conference tournament, first team eliminated
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
With one week left of the regular season, the Big Ten has come to crunch time with multiple teams on the brink of elimination and clinching a spot in the conference tournament.

Last week, one Big Ten squad clinched its bid to the postseason, while another became the first to be eliminated.

Here’s a look at some of the top action across the conference in the second-to-last week of regular-season play.

UCLA clinches top tournament seed

After winning the Big Ten championship last year, UCLA is ready and back for more.

The Bruins clinched the top seed in the tournament earlier this week in a win over Washington, silencing Michigan’s late-season push for the No. 1 spot.

Despite losing its seventh game of the year to then-No. 4 Texas, UCLA has won every game since then, including a 17-0 Big Ten record so far this season. With its last game coming against unranked USC on Sunday, the Bruins are on track to be the first undefeated Big Ten team since Maryland in the 2014-15 season.

Against Washington last week, UCLA’s Lauren Betts once again led the way with 23 points, followed by double-digit performances against Gianna Kneepkens, Kiki Rice, Gabriela Jaquez and Angela Dugalić.

On Sunday, the Bruins beat Wisconsin 80-60 behind the second straight double-digit performance from each of those five players.

Postseason elimination

After losing its ninth straight game on Saturday, Rutgers became the first team to be eliminated from Big Ten tournament contention.

The Scarlet Knights fell to last in the conference following Penn State’s win over Northwestern on Feb. 15 and proceeded to stay there after another pair of losses last week.

Rutgers’ first loss came on the road at Illinois, where it was held to just 56 points on 38.9% shooting. After trailing by just two points following the first quarter, the Scarlet Knights were outscored 46-21 in the second and third quarters.

Graduate guard Faith Blackstone was the leading scorer for Rutgers, scoring just 13 points. Between Blackstone and Nene Ndiaye — the only other double-digit scorer — the Scarlet Knights mounted a 23-16 fourth quarter, but were unable to overcome the deficit.

Those struggles continued into Saturday’s game against the Lady Lions, where the Scarlet Knights fell 87-78.

Former Rutgers star Kiyomi McMiller led the way, tying her career-high 37 points as Penn State led 21-18 after 10 minutes and never turned back. Following the loss, Rutgers was eliminated from Big Ten tournament contention, while the blue and white fought on.

Battle for No. 2

Michigan was in control of the No. 2 seed in the Big Ten tournament for a few weeks until Iowa upset the Wolverines on Sunday afternoon.

The maize and blue were held to its fewest points this season, scoring just 44 points through 40 minutes. The Hawkeyes held Michigan to single-digit scoring in the second and fourth quarters, with just eight and nine points, respectively.

Sophomore center Ava Heiden led the charge for Iowa, scoring 24 points — nearly 40% of her team’s scoring.

For the Wolverines, just five players found the net, and just two recorded double-digits. While Olivia Olson, Syla Swords and Mila Holloway each averaged double-digit scoring and a combined 45.9 points per game this year, they totaled just 31 points against the Hawkeyes.

Both teams have a pair of games left this year, with Michigan’s coming against No. 10 Ohio State and No. 14 Maryland, while Iowa will face unranked Illinois and Wisconsin

MORE BASKETBALL CONTENT

title: Report: Former Penn State QB Drew Allar will throw at 2026 NFL Scouting Combine
author: Lyle Alenstein | Wrestling Editor
description:
There's an update on Penn State's former QB1.

Drew Allar is cleared to throw at the NFL Scouting Combine coming off his season-ending broken left ankle, per Adam Schefter of ESPN.

Allar suffered the injury against Northwestern on Oct. 11, and originally was on some form of motorized vehicle with a boot. Toward the end of the season, he no longer needed a vehicle.

"Have an amazing athletic team here that's helping me out. A ton of coaches are helping me out a ton," Allar said in November on Zoom. "I got a great team around me between parents and agents and all that sort of thing that have really helped me through the process so far. And honestly, I have really good teammates that have made the transition a lot easier."

Allar was a three-year starter for the Nittany Lions and two-year captain. He finished his career with 7,402 passing yards with 61 touchdowns and 13 interceptions, adding 736 rushing yards and 12 scores on the ground.

The former 5-star product in the 2022 recruiting class is looked at as the third-best quarterback in the draft class, according to Mel Kiper of ESPN. Allar slots behind Indiana's Fernando Mendoza and Alabama's Ty Simpson on the board.

Quarterbacks will throw at Lucas Oil Stadium on Saturday.

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Around the Big Ten | An overlook at conference dual season ahead of Big Ten Championships
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
The dual season has come to an end, and with that, the Big Ten Championships are a little under two weeks away.

Penn State remained atop the Big Ten throughout the season, but other teams showed some signs of chances to compete with the Nittany Lions.

Here are storylines and updates across the Big Ten’s dual season.

The next best teams

Ohio State seemingly had a chance against Penn State. That was until three of the Buckeyes’ starters were ruled out for the dual, giving the Nittany Lions a dominant win.

Ohio State dominated elsewhere, going 18-1 on the season with a 7-1 Big Ten record. The lightweights led the way for the Buckeyes, with Nic Bouzakis ranked No. 2 at 125 pounds with a 13-1 record. At 133 pounds, Ben Davino earned the No. 3 ranking with a 21-1 record. One of the Hodge Trophy favorites, Jesse Mendez, is atop the rankings at 141 pounds with an undefeated record.

Rutgers earned its highest finish in the dual standings in program history, finishing third in the Big Ten. The Scarlet Knights don’t necessarily have a top guy, but a more well-rounded 10-man lineup. Joey Olivieri is the highest-ranked wrestler out of the starters, slotting in at No. 6 at 141 pounds. But, they lost three duals in the Big Ten to Penn State, Illinois and Michigan.

The other teams to finish with a 5-3 record in conference were Illinois, Iowa, Minnesota and Nebraska.

Underperforming Hawkeyes

Iowa was consistently the team thought to be able to eventually beat Penn State. However, this season, the Hawkeyes have underperformed.

Iowa is the No. 7 team in the country and is in severe danger of not having a national finalist for the first time in 34 years.

Angelo Ferrari hasn’t wrestled since the Hawkeyes’ dual against Penn State, while 2025 national finalist Drake Ayala has had a significant drop off from his performance in his first three years.

The Hawkeyes also seemingly had a title contender in Division III transfer Massoma Endene, however, Endene is no longer with the team for a multitude of reported reasons.

Big Ten holds top spot in all but one weight class

While Penn State has six No. 1 wrestlers, the other four weight classes have a Big Ten wrestler at the top as well.

Penn State has Luke Lilledahl at 125 pounds, Shayne Van Ness at 149 pounds, Mitchell Mesenbrink at 165 pounds, Levi Haines at 174 pounds, Rocco Welsh at 184 pounds and Josh Barr at 197 pounds.

Lucas Byrd of Illinois, the reigning national champion, is No. 1 at 133 pounds. Up one weight class, Mendez is the top wrestler at 141 pounds and his teammate, Brandon Cannon, is the top guy at 157 pounds.

The only weight class that doesn’t have a Big Ten wrestler at No. 1 is heavyweight, with Iowa State’s Yonger Bastida holding that spot right now. However, from rankings three to six, it’s all Big Ten representatives, showcasing a chance for a Big Ten sweep at the national championships in a few weeks.

However, these rankings surely provide must-see television for not only the 2026 National Championship in Cleveland, but the Big Ten Championships in the Bryce Jordan Center in two weeks.

MORE WRESTLING COVERAGE

title: Stephen Nedoroscik returning to Happy Valley for Penn State men's gymnastics' meet against Springfield
author: Keira Albert | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State alum Stephen Nedoroscik will be back in Happy Valley this weekend for the men's gymnastics home meet at 1 p.m. Sunday.

We have a special guest in town this weekend 👀Penn State alum @GymnastSteve is back in Happy Valley for Sunday’s home meet in Rec Hall and will have an EXCLUSIVE autograph session following the action. Attendance to the meet is FREE and rotations kick off at 1 p.m.!!! pic.twitter.com/xR1TryLHGu— Penn State Men’s Gymnastics (@PennStateMGYM) February 24, 2026

The men's gymnastics team will take on Springfield College from Rec Hall. The former Penn State gymnast will do an exclusive autograph session following the meet. Attendance is free for everyone.

Nedoroscik is a pommel horse specialist who recently competed in the 2024 Summer Olympics in Paris. He helped the United States bring home a bronze medal, which was the first medal in 16 years for men's gymnastics.

The Olympian competed with the Nittany Lions from 2017 to 2020. He was a two-time national title winner on pommel horse and won the Big Ten title in the same event during his sophomore season.

During his final season with the blue and white, Nedoroscik won the Nissen-Emery Award as the nation's top senior gymnast, making him the first specialist to ever receive the honor.

MORE GYMNASTICS COVERAGE

title: Penn State men's soccer freshman Ben Nash training with Canadian U20 National Team
author: Liana Tiamzon I For the Collegian
description:
It's been announced that Penn State freshman Ben Nash from Alberta, Canada, will train with the U20 Canadian National Team.

He’s off on assignment 🇨🇦We miss Ben here at home, but proud to have him training with the u20 Canadian National Team in Nicaragua 👏#WeAre pic.twitter.com/ujNpqTNQhe— Penn State Men’s Soccer (@PennStateMSOC) February 24, 2026

The defender will work hard in Nicaragua, but this feat isn’t new to him.

He was previously selected to represent Canada at the U18 national team level and participated in a two-week international training camp in Finland.

He has competed against top youth national teams in Europe, facing some of the top young players in the world.

Nash most recently competed with the Vancouver Whitecaps Academy team, as well as with the Vancouver Whitecaps II in MLS Next Pro.

He was a back-to-back Alberta Provincial Champion in 2022 and 2023. He helped lead his team to the Toyota National Championship. He was three-times voted to the BTB Best XI during his time with the club.

Without him, Penn State is set to battle Bucknell at home on February 28th at 4 p.m.

MORE SOCCER COVERAGE

title: Penn State notebook | Guy Gadowsky talks forward injuries, club team call-ups and more
author: Greg Finberg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Fresh off a sweep of Ohio State that featured a program-record eight points from Gavin McKenna in Friday’s 11–4 victory and a three-goal comeback in Saturday’s 5-4 overtime win, No. 5 Penn State is back on the road.

The Nittany Lions head to South Bend for a two-game set against Notre Dame, the Big Ten’s worst team at just 2-17-1 in conference play.

Guy Gadowsky spoke to the media on Tuesday regarding injury updates, possible club team call-ups and more.

Injury updates

Penn State has been crushed by the injury bug, especially at forward.

Alex Servagno remains out for the remainder of the regular season. Gadowsky said the team is optimistic Braeden Ford could return either this weekend or next, and sounded optimistic about Charlie Cerrato’s possible return before postseason play begins.

With those injuries, plus Dane Dowiak and Keaton Peaters being out for the season, the Nittany Lions are down to just 10 healthy forwards. Defensemen Casey Aman and Nick Fascia had stepped in to play more offensive roles, but Fascia suffered a “bad cut” in Friday’s win and didn’t play in Saturday’s 5-4 overtime victory.

“(Fascia is) out this weekend and day-to-day after that,” Gadowsky said.

Club team call-ups?

Gadowsky said Saturday that Penn State has had administration “checking things out” regarding possibly adding a skater or two from Penn State’s club ice hockey team.

The Nittany Lions have dipped into the club team to add third-string goaltenders in recent years, including Chris McFayden this season. But they’ve never added a skater from the Ice Lions.

With just 10 healthy forwards, Gadowsky said that remains a possibility, but not one they intend to explore. At least for now, as the team expects Cerrato and Ford to return within the next few weeks.

“We're holding off right now,” Gadowsky said. “This weekend was tough. However, we're optimistic that we're going to get reinforcements.”

Penn State’s long-time coach said the team would only bring up Ice Lions skaters in an “absolute emergency.” He prefers to stick with the group that’s earned a 20-9-1 record in a grueling Big Ten conference.

“There are consequences to (using club skaters),” Gadowsky said. “You play all year, and you go through so many meetings, so many practices, so many video sessions, so much training, and the experience is all to make sure you get on the same page. And it's very difficult just to bring in a body and have them assimilated perfectly.”

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: Recapping Penn State’s regular season | The Mat Return
author: The Daily Collegian wrestling staff
description:
“The Mat Return” co-hosts Lyle Alenstein, AJ Piazza and Andrew Deal came together following a commanding win for the Nittany Lions against Princeton.

What is Levi Haines’ legacy? What happened in Luke Lilledahl’s match? What’s going on at 141 pounds after Aaron Nagao medically retiring and Braeden Davis’ inconstancies?

Following that, the group dove into what wrestlers have had their stock rise during the regular season.

MORE COVERAGE

title: Penn State track and field to kick off postseason at Big Ten championships
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
After a record-breaking regular season, Penn State is ready for the postseason.

The Nittany Lions will compete in the 2026 Big Ten Track and Field Indoor Championships in Indianapolis from Thursday to Saturday.

The men’s team is considered to be in the running for a conference title, while several athletes are in a strong position to make the podium this weekend.

Sprints

Ajani Dwyer has carved his way into the record books for the second straight year and is performing at his best heading into this year’s Big Ten championship. After a false start in last year’s 60-meter finals, he currently holds the top time in the conference.

The school record holder finished in 6.52 seconds earlier this season, but will be challenged by Iowa’s Darwin Jimenez and Kalen Walker, alongside USC’s Eddie Nketia, who all have ran sub-6.60 60m races this year.

In the 200-meter, Dwyer is far from top-seeded Garrett Kaalund, who ran 20.19 seconds at the New Mexico Collegiate Classic.

However, Dwyer holds the second-best mark at 20.52 seconds and will look to improve from last year’s fifth-place finish.

Fellow sophomore Jake Palermo is positioned for a top finish in the men’s 400-meter after breaking the school record at the Tyson Invitational earlier this year, running 45.33 seconds.

He raced against the sixth and seventh-best 400m runners in the conference at that meet and holds a sizable, 0.6-second lead over them. However, USC’s Jack Stadlman and Ohio State’s Edidiong Udo come in with times of 45.62 and 45.65 and have the best chance at dethroning Palermo.

Mid-distance

While Penn State’s sprints have a pair of runners who could earn gold, the middle distance group is even more poised to take home a top spot.

In the men’s 600-meter, senior Handal Roban holds the conference’s top time of 1:14.89 — also the school record.

After Michigan’s Miles Brown with the third-best time, Niko Schultz sits as the second Nittany Lion in the top five with a time of 1:16.35.

The men’s 800-meters has three Penn Staters in the top five, with Roban leading the way with a 1:44.17.

Following the 2024 Olympian is Japan’s 800m record holder, Allon Clay. The graduate student has a time of 1:45.17, ahead of Oregon’s Matthew Erickson in 1:45.93 and Indiana’s Camden Marshall in 1:45.99. Yukichi Ishii rounds out the five for the blue and white with a season-best 1:46.37.

The Nittany Lions also have a pair of elite athletes in the women’s 800m, with Hayley Kitching holding the conference’s top mark of 1:59.22. Ritchie has the second-best time going into the championship with a season-best 2:01.60.

Distance

Senior Charlotte Costich has been having a breakout year, running her two best 5,000-meters this season. She sits fifth in the conference with a season-best 15:49.68, and could climb the ranks over Minnesota’s Ali Weimer in third with 15:39.70.

Relays

The men’s 4x400m team has competed three times this season, but found its best lineup with Palermo, Matt DeMatteo, Schultz and Peyton Sommers at the Tyson Invitational.

The Nittany Lions ran 3:07.52 for sixth in the event, but are ranked fourth in the conference, within one second of Purdue in third place.

The following weekend, the men’s distance medley relay ran a school record 9:18.58 with Clay, Roban, Schultz and Nick Sloff. Now, they’re ranked fifth in the Big Ten, within striking distance of Washington and Wisconsin.

The women’s DMR earned the second-best time in school at the Husky Classic and is third in the conference with a 10:51.68 behind Kitching, Alexandria North, Ritchie and Ada Rand. Only Washington and Oregon have run faster times this year, running 10:45.03 and 10:45.97, respectively.

Throws

Penn State has one of its best throw squads in recent years, seeing success from both the men’s and women’s teams this year.

In the men’s shot put, senior Ryan Henry is fourth in the Big Ten with a top mark of 19.81 meters and the fourth-best throw in school history. The top throw in the conference is just 0.35 meters farther by Oregon’s Lawrence Kobe, giving Henry an opportunity to win heading into the event.

Multi-events

After earning bronze at last year’s Big Ten championship, senior Maddie Pitts is close to earning that mark again this year.

Pitts currently holds the fourth-best mark in the conference with 4,239 points. She’s just shy of Illinois’ JaiCieonna Gero-Holt’s 4,250 points, and has seen improvement in her performance this year.

MORE TRACK AND FIELD COVERAGE

title: Weekly Recap | Penn State men's volleyball continues strong play with series sweep over Harvard
author: Keira Albert | The Daily Collegian
description:
Over the weekend, Penn State played a conference, two-game home series against Harvard.

The blue and white walked away with two wins, extending its winning streak to six games and moving its record to 10-4. It remains undefeated, 4-0, against EIVA opponents.

The Nittany Lions took to Rec Hall’s South Gym at 6 p.m. Friday for the first match of the weekend series against the Crimson.

The match ended in a clean 3-0 sweep for the Penn State squad, but not without some fight from the opposing Harvard team.

Entering the contest 2-7 with a 0-2 record in conference play, the Crimson would have their work cut out for them. But they made the blue and white fight for its win.

In the early moments of the first set, points were traded back-and-forth between both teams, keeping the game tight.

But once the Nittany Lions hit double digits, they took off running, forcing set point after a dominant seven-point stretch. The blue and white claimed the first set with a 25-11 victory.

Coming into this match, Sean Harvey had lots of momentum. Having been named the EIVA Offensive Player of the Week after a series against Princeton, the outside hitter didn’t let his foot off the gas. Harvey logged three service aces in the first set alone.

The blue and white had to work for it in the second set, though. Harvard took an early lead, and while Penn State was able to recover, the Crimson fought their way back.

With the set point creeping up, both sides remained neck-and-neck. At 25-25, the blue and white scored in back-to-back succession to take the second set 27-25.

Harvey logged two more service aces in back-to-back fashion, while a few other Nittany Lions got some of their own.

Within that second set, Tristan Hassell tallied his first ace of the match, while Michael Schwob got his second. Gaige Gabriel recorded two service aces in the same back-to-back style as his teammate Harvey, which eventually forced a Harvard timeout.

The third set was a much easier win, 25-15 for the blue and white.

Friday’s match wasn’t just strong offensively, though. In fact, the defensive efforts were record-breaking.

Owen Rose set a school-record 14 blocks against the Crimson on Friday, which would eventually lead the redshirt senior to win the EIVA Defensive Player of the Week honor for the fifth time in his career.

After Friday’s match, Harvey tallied 14.5 points and 5 service aces, with Rose trailing just behind with 14 points. The Nittany Lions advanced to 9-4 on the season.

Taking the main stage in Rec Hall for the Legacy Match at 3 p.m. Saturday, the Crimson would set out for what would end up being a pretty similar match to that of Friday.

While the Harvard squad was able to take a win in one set, ultimately, it wasn’t enough to kill Penn State’s hot streak. With a 3-1 victory, the blue and white completed the weekend series on a high note.

The first set started with some close scoring, but it seemed, yet again, like the Crimson were unable to stop the blue and white once they hit double digits.

The blue and white took a victory in the first set 25-19. To no one’s shock, Harvey logged a service ace in the opening set, with teammate Rose also tallying one.

It was that second set that prevented a full sweep for the blue and white this weekend.

Harvard got out in front early, and it seemed like the Nittany Lions never really recovered. The Crimson took the set 25-22, tying the game up at 1-1.

Rose recorded the only service ace of the set for the Penn State side, while the Crimson, more notably, tallied three service aces.

But it was back to regularly scheduled programming in the third set as the blue and white got back to business. Young stars Hassell and Jaidyn Bethel put up several kills each, with Harvey right there with them.

While the score remained close for a good majority of the set, the Nittany Lions went on a five-point streak to close out the set 25-16.

From there, it was all downhill.

Penn State took the fourth set 25-19 with a kill from Bethel, assisted by Schwob, closing out the weekend.

Hassell, Schwob and Rose logged a service ace each. But serving was definitely not quite as strong on Saturday as it had been on Friday. Saturday’s Legacy Match only saw 6 aces, while Friday’s saw 11.

Regardless of serving, it was a strong weekend for Penn State. After taking back-to-back wins, the Nittany Lions remained in the No. 16 spot in this week’s AVCA Poll.

The blue and white will be back at it from Rec Hall at 7 p.m. Friday against Merrimack.

MORE VOLLEYBALL COVERAGE

title: Weekly Recap | Penn State baseball falls short against Richmond
author: Brielle Gari | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State traveled to the Old Dominion State for its second weekend of the season, facing off with Richmond for a four-game series. The Nittany Lions looked for redemption after Kansas State overpowered the blue and white, scoring 24 runs.

Penn State went 1-2 this past weekend, sitting at a 2-4 overall record on the 2026 season.

As there was high anticipation for the new season, the blue and white have seriously underperformed. Whether it's at the plate or on the mound, Penn State has been inefficient, with a team batting average of .247 and a .739 OPS.

Starting off with a dominating performance to open the weekend, the Nittany Lions shut out the Spiders on Friday with a final score of 5-0.

Attempting to continue its momentum for the doubleheader on Saturday, Penn State was shut down by an aggressive Richmond bullpen. The Spiders shut out the Nittany Lions to close out their first game of the day, 3-0.

Going head-to-head for its final matchup of the weekend, Richmond took the lead in the bottom of the third, scoring two runs. Penn State responded in the fourth, with just a single run scored, but Richmond's momentum ultimately gave it the win, 4-3.

On the offensive side

Penn State’s offense had both surges of productivity and moments of struggle at the plate.

On Friday, the blue and white swung its way to a 5-0 victory against Richmond. The Nittany Lions were able to tally five runs off eight hits.

Infielder Spencer Barnett launched two solo home runs in the opening game. The Pittsburgh native marked his second career two-homer game. Barnett was the most successful Nittany Lion at the plate last weekend, going 2-for-3 with two RBI and two homers.

For their Saturday doubleheader, the Nittany Lions trailed in both games. In the first game, three Nittany Lions were able to collect runs off eight hits.

Jack Porter attempted to keep things alive for the blue and white in its final matchup against the Spiders, answering in the fourth inning with a double to right field, with infielder Kevin Karstetter following with a line drive, allowing Porter to score. Tying the game in the eighth, Porter sent the first pitch he saw over the wall.

Infielder Michael Anderson launched another homer at the top of the ninth to cut Penn State’s deficit to 4-3, but the rally was unsuccessful.

Penn State's inconsistency at the plate caused the bullpen to work overtime.

Pitching flaws

One of the main issues the 2026 squad could have is the depth of their bullpen. The inconsistency is a major fault and is resulting in tough losses.

To kick off the weekend off, right-hander Colin Fitzgerlad earned the start for the blue and white, throwing a strong game. The Lititz, Pennsylvania, native had his best performance as a Nittany Lion, recording eight strikeouts in 6.0 innings.

The redshirt senior made his second start of the 2026 season, allowing seven hits and three walks.

Right-hander Ben Hudson got the start for the first game of the doubleheader, throwing 4.0 innings, allowing three runs, one earned run on three hits. Hudson recorded four strikeouts and gave up two walks.

Ben DeMell closed out the first game with four shutout frames, giving up two hits and one walk, but was successful in securing three strikeouts.

Closing out its third game of the weekend, the blue and white put freshman Isaiah Shayter on the mound. He threw 3.0 innings, giving up two runs on four hits, and striking out three Richmond batters.

Penn State made good use of the bullpen, as Mason Horwat fired four scoreless innings, with one strikeout and three hits. Left-hander Matthew VanOstenbridge got on the mound but was unsuccessful, giving up two runs and Kyle Emmons closed out the eighth inning with two outs, striking out one batter.

The Nittany Lions take a trip to the Bahamas to face off with Indiana State in a three-game series, with their first game at 6 p.m. on Friday.

MORE BASEBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State notebook | Carolyn Kieger focuses on final 2 games with Big Ten Tournament looming
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Riding a two-game winning streak, Penn State is in the hunt for its first Big Ten Tournament appearance since 2024.

Despite the recent success, two mid-level conference foes lie in the path to the Big Ten tournament, all of which coach Carolyn Kieger talked about during this week’s weekly media session.

4th-quarter composure

Following their win over Purdue, the Lady Lions had early success over Michigan State and Maryland, which quickly evaporated in the second half and fourth quarter.

In the past two games, Penn State held a sizable lead over Northwestern and Rutgers going into the final 15 minutes. Unlike the games before that, it silenced the opponents’ late-game comeback, holding on for a pair of victories.

“Our composure down the stretch (has been good),” Kieger said. “We’ve gotten so much more confident and composed, and we’ve been talking about that all year, of how to get to that point where the last possession doesn’t affect the next one and how to be present.”

In addition to the recent late-game success, the Lady Lions have seen overall improvement from their squad over the final weeks of the season, playing their best basketball heading into March.

“We’ve continued to get better, and we proved that again with a tough environment on the road with a lot at stake,” Kieger said. “I just can’t say enough about how composed they were as a group.”

Remaining games

While the blue and white only need one win in the final two games for a chance at a tournament appearance, neither squad will be easy to emerge victorious from.

USC is ranked No. 21 in the NET rankings and is coming off a recent upset win over Iowa. Freshman Jazzy Davidson has been the go-to scorer with 18.0 points per game and could be a problem on Wednesday.

“Jazzy is super talented,” Kieger said. “We got to put different bodies around her as she’s just going to get shots off. We got to make them as tough as we possibly can.”

The second of Penn State’s games is against Indiana, which is 58th in the NET rankings. Led by former Lady Lion Shay Ciezki — who’s leading the Big Ten with 23.1 points per game and is a projected WNBA draft pick — Kieger had a lot to say about her former player.

“Honestly, I have so much respect for Shay and her work ethic; she’s one of the hardest workers around,” Kieger said. “I’m just thrilled to see everything she’s worked for her entire career come full circle, because I know how hard she’s worked. She’s as gym rat as they come.”

McMiller madness

After another week of 30-point games, Kiyomi McMiller was another focal point of this week’s media.

McMiller recorded her fifth straight 30-point game on Saturday, tying her career-high 37 points.

“She’s obviously playing super confident right now,” Kieger said. “She’s scoring at all three levels, and I love how she’s matured throughout the season. I think it’s really fun to see her development.”

With the milestone against Rutgers, McMiller became the first player since Caitlin Clark to reach five straight 30-point games. Despite that feat and lofty comparison, McMiller isn’t too focused on that, and is instead looking ahead to a potential tournament appearance.

“The only thing she really cares about right now is winning,” Kieger said. “She wants to be playing in the conference tournament, so she’s working really hard for that.”

Your #B1GHockey First Star of the Week, Gavin McKenna 🏒⭐️ 🔟 Points8️⃣ Assists2️⃣ Goals1️⃣ OT GWGSingle-game program record 8️⃣ points and 7️⃣ assists on Friday 🤯First NCAA Division I 8️⃣-point game in 39 years‼️Read ➡️ https://t.co/WnDxFQikD4#WeAre #HockeyValley pic.twitter.com/kjPyxJOeCv— Penn State Men’s Hockey (@PennStateMHKY) February 24, 2026
