title: A guide to the Final Four during THON Weekend
author: Teresa Phelan | Opinions Editor
description:
The most anticipated event of THON Weekend, Final Four, is quickly approaching. The final hours of THON are where dancers, volunteers and the student body learn the fundraising total for the year and listen to the stories of families who have been affected by pediatric cancer.

Whether it’s your first or final THON Weekend, here are some reminders as THON weekend concludes.

1. Arrive early

Due to the popularity of the Final Four, it’s imperative that spectators arrive early. Once the Bryce Jordan Center reaches its maximum capacity, no one else will be allowed in.

It doesn’t matter if you're with an organization or just want to attend the most talked about event of the weekend, once the BJC is full, it’s closed.

If you think you’re early enough, you’re probably not.

2. Think ahead

Once you’re in the BJC, go to the bathroom, fill up your water bottle and get something to eat.

As THON weekend begins to wind down, stands will begin to fill, which means that lines are longer. You don’t want to miss anything because you’ve been in line for 45 minutes.

3. Know the basics

Before attending Final Four, understand that dancers, spectators and many student volunteers have been awake and standing for over 42 hours. Refrain from asking them about the time, day or any other subjects that would contribute to their mood.

THON adheres to the BJC’s clear bag policy, and no outside food or drink is allowed. Spectators are allowed and encouraged to bring a reusable water bottle.

4. It’s emotional

During the Final Four, there are various moments where emotions are high. Attendees will watch a celebration of life video, listen to Four Diamonds family speak and witness the Executive Committee announce the 2026 fundraising total.

While the afternoon is full of love, inspiration and stories of triumph, prepare yourself for speeches, videos and photos that highlight what THON is all about.

5. Take care of yourself (and others)

Whether you’ve been in the stands or are just planning to come for the Final Four, it’s important that you're doing OK.

If you don’t feel well, because you’re sick, mentally or physically drained, reconsider attending the Final Four. The well-being of everyone in attendance is the main priority, and no one will be mad, disappointed or angry if you take a break.

6. Don’t forget to be present

At times, THON may be overstimulating, and things may feel heavy. While there’s joy and happiness, there’s also feelings that can be a lot to deal with and process.

Whatever you’re feeling, know you’re validated and your presence at THON means a lot to the community.

MORE THONTENT

title: ‘He deserves this’ | THON 2026 dancer Zach Jarrett inspires his fraternity with unwavering commitment
author: Mercedes Hamilton | Editor-in-Chief
description:
Zach Jarrett spent the first of his 46 hours at THON with one of Alpha Kappa Psi’s Four Diamonds families, the Sweitzers. As a dancer and the business fraternity’s THON Chair, quality time at the make-shift playground in the Bryce Jordan Center was just the motivation he needed to kick off a successful weekend.

“It’s exciting that she’s finished her treatment and now she’s able to come to a THON. She was so happy,” Jarrett, a fourth-year studying business analytics and information systems, said of 6-year-old Logan Sweitzer. “She’s been in the playground with the other kids and seeing all of the people dancing for her and other kids like her…I’m doing this for her and other kids like her.”

In his senior year, Jarrett said the opportunity to take his passion from the stands to the floor was a no brainer. In addition to his commitment to the Sweitzers and the Rodriguezes, THON’s mission remains relevant to his experiences and values.

“I have a lot of people in my life that have been affected by cancer,” he said. “Philanthropy has always been a very cool thing, and my mom is always talking about giving back.”

Jarrett also credits Alpha Kappa Psi alumni for instilling a love for THON in him and his peers since he joined the fraternity. This year, the organization is well represented on the floor, with two dancer representatives, two Independent Dancer Couples (IDCs) affiliated with the organization and several directors and committee members.

Last year, Alpha Kappa Psi was ranked fourth in fundraising for general organizations at $129,551.97.

“I’m really grateful that everybody’s driven toward philanthropy, and everybody wants to give back in their own way,” Jarrett said. “We have so many people here on the floor doing their part.”

Alex Greenbaum, one of the brothers, said THON Weekend 2026 is the reflection of Jarrett’s four years of hard work contributing to Alpha Kappa Psi’s fundraisers, supporting their Four Diamonds families, as well as previous dancers representing the fraternity.

“We all think that he deserves this a lot,” Greenbaum, a third-year studying accounting and finance, said. “I’m so happy for him.”

When Greenbaum visited Jarrett on the floor just a few hours in, he was confident he’d do well this weekend. He said Jarrett had prepared his mind and body in advance and walked in with a solid plan to stretch frequently and keep moving.

“It’s awesome to see him push through,” Greenbaum said. “I’m so proud of him.”

In the stands, Greenbaum stood alongside Noah Levine waving cardboard cutouts of Jarrett’s face and cheering him on.

“We’ve been sending him motivational texts on our phones like, ‘You’re the best,’ ‘You’re doing something bigger than yourself,’” Levine, a second-year studying finance, said.

When it comes to THON, Levine said the fraternity always wants to put their best foot forward and Jarrett is a reflection of this. He said he doesn’t know anyone on campus as committed to THON as Jarrett is.

For Levine, the smile on the Sweitzers’ faces make it clear that Jarrett’s commitment is paying off, making it easy for Alpha Kappa Psi to brave the full 46 whether they’re on the floor or in the stands.

“He’s probably one of the most dedicated people I know,” Levine said. “It’s just so inspiring to everyone in this fraternity.”

MORE THONTENT

title: Commonwealth campus students continue THON support amid impending closures
author: Grace Levy | The Daily Collegian
description:
For some Commonwealth campus students, THON 2026 marks a bittersweet goodbye.

Penn State’s Board of Trustees voted in May to close the DuBois, Fayette, Mont Alto, New Kensington, Shenango, Wilkes-Barre and York campuses after spring 2027, citing steep enrollment declines, aging infrastructure and financial strain.

Students at Penn State Dubois hoped to conclude their final THON on a high note. Gabby Horner, one of the active chairs for the weekend and a former THON dancer, said Dubois beat its previous fundraising total and the school’s record.

“I’m pretty sad because I was a dancer last year, so I have a lot of good memories with THON,” Horner, a second-year studying occupational therapy, said.

Horner’s relationship with THON is very personal, having survived cancer herself two years ago.

“It was really meaningful because I kind of maybe understood it more than maybe someone else that didn't have cancer would, and it was really emotional, but I'm really glad I was a dancer last year” Horner said. “It was really a great experience.”

Even before the closures, branch campuses had a different experience getting students involved in thon.

“It's different. It's a lot harder to get people involved because they are not (as) knowledgeable of it,” Josh Price said. “I know this year it was difficult getting the new students involved, but once you get them involved and you get a story to them, and they know what it's about, they get really into it, and they really like it.”

After raising $31,045.50 last year, students at Penn State Dubois hope to conclude their final THON Weekend on just as high a note. Horner said Dubois has already beat last year's fundraising total.

“I’m pretty sad because I was a dancer last year, so I have a lot of good memories with THON,” Horner said.

Horner’s relationship with THON is very personal, having survived cancer herself. She's been in remission for two years.

“It was really meaningful because I kind of maybe understood it more than maybe someone else that didn't have cancer would, and it was really emotional, but I'm really glad I was a dancer last year” Horner said. “It was really a great experience.”

Even before the closures, Penn State Dubois had a difficult experience getting students involved in THON.

“It's different. It's a lot harder to get people involved because they are not knowledgeable (about) it,” Price, a second-year studying human development and family studies, said. “I know this year it was difficult getting the new students involved, but once...they know what it's about, they get really into it.”

Penn State York THON'S organization is full of graduating seniors, and without incoming freshmen, the group will cease to exist.

Ashlyn Smith, three-time THON chair, said the organization has 10-15 members, who she called a “really solid group of people.”

“It's sad that no one can take over, and that money is not being raised for the kids," Smith, a fourth-year studying accounting, said. “I’m happy to be here for our last year. Hopefully we’ll go out good, but it's sad obviously.”

While Penn State Fayette can pull together a group for the upcoming school year, fundraising has become increasingly difficult.

“Last year, it was pretty good,” Price said. “We fundraised a lot, and I enjoyed it. But this year we tried our best, and it was just really rough.”

Fayette was the largest fundraiser of the Commonwealth campuses in 2025 with $76,046.43. Since 1998, the organization has raised over $1 million.

Price said he’ll be one of the four returning members to Fayette’s THON organization next year and hopes to be THON chair. Since there won’t be new students enrolling at his campus, he predicts challenges with recruiting new members.

“Knowing that we're not getting new students is rough, so it's more just going to be, ‘Who can we get to join that hasn't been in it yet?’” Price said.

Amid the challenges, Price remains hopeful about what Fayette can achieve for THON in the coming year.

“Even if I'm not the chair, I'm going to help out as much as I can,” Price said. “I love THON. I really love what it's about. I think that it's a really special place and it's a special thing in my heart.”

Kerstin Glebis, a former family relations captain and dancer for Fayette, described the relationship with the Faust family as "a special bond" she doesn't want to give up.

“I’m going to do what I can do to keep it (and) continue my support for the dancers," Glebis said. "Even though it may not be a Fayette dancer, they're still a dancer...whatever I can do to be there for them, I’ll do.”

Glebis described her relationship their Four Diamonds Family, the Fausts, as "a special bond" she doesn't want to give up.

While everyone's unsure what will happen once the campus closes, Justin Byers, another alum and former dancer, said they remain dedicated.

Byers said they hope to return to THON each year as a “little organization of Fayette alumni” to continue to support the organization and their family.

“I know Tandy from the family is going to be up here, so we'll get to see her for a little bit, and we always cherish her son that lost his battle to cancer in 2009,” Byers said. “We just keep moving forward and we support our family that we have and just want to stay in contact with them.”

MORE THONTENT

title: Down to just 9 healthy forwards, No. 6 Penn State completes ‘gritty’ comeback win over Ohio State
author: Greg Finberg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Injuries are commonplace in sports.

Take the fast-paced, physical nature of hockey, and a few injuries across a 34-game regular season are expected. But not to the extent No. 6 Penn State has faced this season, especially at forward.

Dane Dowiak’s recent season-ending injury brought the Nittany Lions to just 10 healthy forwards, with defensemen Casey Aman and Nick Fascia playing more offensive roles as a result.

Guy Gadowsky’s shorthanded squad ripped through Ohio State in Friday’s 11-4 win. But the injury bug persisted, as Fascia suffered a “bad cut” that kept him out of Saturday’s rematch.

Down three goals and without several offensive playmakers in Game 2, Penn State clawed back to knot the contest at 4-4 before Gavin McKenna capped his historic weekend with the overtime winner.

While McKenna’s dirty dangle was the lead story, it doesn’t begin to encapsulate the effort required by Gadowsky’s squad to create that game-winning opportunity.

Penn State held a 12-6 edge in shots on goal after a 1-1 opening period. But Ohio State responded with a dominant second period, outshooting Penn State 19-9 and taking a 4-2 lead into the final period.

The Nittany Lions believed their play wasn’t the issue. It was their effort, or lack thereof, that needed changing.

“Guys are beat up, guys are tired when you have such a short bench. (But) you’ve got to play through it,” defenseman Jarod Crespo said. “Guys just kind of grabbed their pants and pulled them up and played blue-collar hockey.”

The comeback started with Lev Katzin, Penn State’s gritty forward who drew three penalties in Saturday’s contest, the final one bringing his team within a goal.

The freshman forward took a brief detour from his scrappy playstyle when he dropped a nifty feed to a streaking JJ Wiebusch, who beat Kristoffer Eberly far side to make it 4-3 in the third period.

“There are guys who have made a lot of money in the National Hockey League for doing similar things,” Gadowsky said of Katzin’s play. “He's a really tough kid, and he plays that way. He plays on the edge, and he gets under people's skin, and he does a really good job.”

Before the PA announcer could announce the previous goalscorer, Crespo scooped a loose puck and beat Eberly glove side to send Pegula Ice Arena into a madhouse. But the senior defenseman’s heroics weren’t done there.

With under five minutes remaining in regulation, the Buckeyes embarked on a 3-on-1 break, Crespo the lone Nittany Lion between them and goaltender Kevin Reidler. Crespo dove for an aggressive poke check, sprawling out to bat the pass into the corner and keep the contest tied.

“(Crespo) is a leader. There's no ifs about it,” Gadowsky said. “Going into third, it wasn't about strategy or systems. It was just that we had to want it. We just had to find a way to stay in games like that. You look to a Jarod Crespo type of player that usually gets it done. And he did.”

The player Penn State looks to for energy, Katzin, and the one they look to for leadership, Crespo, helped send the contest into overtime. So it was only fitting that the team’s star, McKenna, was the one to close it out.

McKenna’s overtime winner capped a 10-point weekend that featured multiple broken records. It also put the exclamation point on a six-point weekend for the Nittany Lions, who improved to 20-9-1 despite a plethora of injuries.

“I was really impressed with the grit of this group tonight,” Gadowsky said. “For the team to be down 4-1. We started two guys down, lost another one during the game, had only nine forwards, and then an unbelievable goal by Jarod Crespo to come back. And then you guys saw (McKenna’s goal) in overtime. I don't even know what to say. You guys can use all the superlatives you have, but I’ve run out.”

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: Kol Shields dances in THON 2026 to reconnect with his sister
author: Rachael Keller | News Editor
description:
When Penn State’s Institute of Electric and Electronics Engineers (IEEE) entered the dancer lottery in the fall, Kol Shields raced at the opportunity to represent the organization and honor his sister.

Shields, a fourth-year studying electrical engineering, said THON has always been a big part of his time on campus because of his younger sister, who was diagnosed with B-cell lymphoblastic lymphoma.

“This is something that I should be a part of,” Shields said. “It’s a good thing to be a part of.”

IEEE has four pillars: Corporate, outreach, projects and social. Projects is Shields’ favorite.

As a part of the projects team, Shields said he’s done a number of cool tasks, including building an LED Matrix his freshman year for THON Weekends. He described the sign as a very pixelated television that the team can control the lights on to display videos and messages throughout the 46 hours.

During his second THON, Shields held the sign and supported from the stands. During his third, IEEE got dancers for the first time, one of which was a close friend of his.

Moving his efforts to the floor this year was a decision inspired by his sister.

“She was diagnosed with cancer and passed,” Shields said. “I thought it would be a good way to honor her and a good way to raise money to help kids …”

Tyler Tartabini, a Dancer Logistics captain, said it’s really special that some dancers, like Shields, get the chance to stand for their families who have been impacted by cancer.

“It’s such a unique opportunity for these dancers to really be able to do it for such a great cause,” Tartabini, a third-year studying supply chain and information systems, said.

Billy Richardt, IEEE’s assistant THON Chair, said having a dancer represent the organization at THON is a huge deal.

“It shows we’re not only in THON, we also actively participate,” Richardt, a third-year studying cybersecurity analytics and operations, said.

Richardt said watching Shields get the dancer spot in THON 2026 was a powerful moment.

“We held a vote, and he won, and everybody was very happy,” Richardt said. “He deserved it.”

For Shields, dancing in THON 2026 not only is a rewarding moment, but a cathartic one as well.

“It means a lot to me,” Shields said. “It’s like a way to reconnect with my sister almost. It’s just a nice way to remember her.”

MORE THONTENT

title: Men's soccer team pulls off a win at the THON 2026 Pep Rally
author: Zeyda Frye | Multimedia Editor
description:
At the annual THON Pep Rally, Penn State's sports teams perform a dance and compete for a chance at the trophy. This year the men's soccer team went home winners after a close battle against the men's gymnastics team.

MORE COVERAGE

title: 'I’m so grateful' | Gianna Giorgianni dances in THON Weekend 2026 as an alum
author: Alex Fischer | The Daily Collegian
description:
With around 700 current students dancing in THON Weekend 2026, there are a few former students who are dancing too.

Gianna Giorgianni graduated from Penn State in spring 2025 with a bachelor’s degree in telecommunications and media industries. The Pittsburgh native said she participates in THON for her older brother.

“My entire life has just been in and out of hospitals and learning the real side effects (of cancer), and how pediatric cancer can affect not only the patient, but also the family, the siblings and everything in between,” Giorgianni said.

Giorgianni’s brother was diagnosed with acute lymphoblastic leukemia at the age of three and a half. Now, her brother is 25 years old and cancer-free.

“I initially THON(ed) for my brother, but I continue to THON for all the families that I’ve met throughout this organization, and continue to fight until we find a cure,” Giorgianni said.

Giorgianni helped start a Mini-THON at her high school, where she coordinated fundraisers and events to raise money.

Once Giorgianni got to Penn State she immediately got involved in THON. She started as a Rules and Regulations Committee member her freshman year, then moved to be a Dancer Relations Committee member, a Dancer Relations events coordinator captain, and, in her senior year, an entertainment crowd captain.

“I was super passionate about (THON) just due to my personal connection to pediatric cancer,” Giorgianni said. “(It’s) just incredible being able to share my passion with THON.”

After graduating, Giorgianni joined the Dancer Marathon Alumni Interest Group (DMAIG).

DMAIG, a part of the Penn State Alumni Association, is dedicated to continuing to support and raise funds for THON beyond graduation.

Giorgianni is one of six alumni dancers representing DMAIG this year. She’s paired with Nina Royer, her best friend and the other half of her dancer couple. DMAIG requires prospective dancers to fundraise in pairs, so if potential dancers meet the requirements, they’re automatically assigned a dance partner.

Giorgianni and Royer both served as THON student volunteers during their four years in college.

“(Dancing was) the only thing we didn’t do in our time as students,” Giorgianni said. “We thought, ‘What a perfect opportunity.’ And a lot of our friends and co-captains are still here as students. So we just decided to do it, and we went for it.”

Giorgianni said she’s inspired by the lyric “Everyday our mission calls, taking THON beyond these walls” from the 2024 THON line dance.

“I think that just means a lot, as an alum sharing my story about THON to my now-coworkers and family members,” Giorgianni said. “I just think it’s important to continue doing (THON), and it’s not going to end until we find a cure and we’re able to dance in celebration. So, I think, student or not, we’re still fighting for it, (for the) same cause.”

Maddy Pryor, the president of DMAIG, said it’s important to “keep the mission going” outside of Centre County and central Pennsylvania.

“As we know, childhood cancer is pervasive everywhere,” Pryor, a 2013 Penn State alumna, said. “The spread of alumni around the country and around the globe therefore, spreads the mission of THON around the globe too. And so, to connect people to THON after graduation keeps that mission going.”

Pryor first met Giorgianni on Saturday of THON Weekend.

“She’s just one of those people who’s so passionate to the point where just shaking her hand today and saying ‘Thanks for doing this,’ you could feel the energy off of her,” Pryor said.

Pryor said she finds it really special that DMAIG’s dancers, despite having graduated, still want to support the cause and dance for 46 hours.

“Something we really try to drive home is that THON does not stop once you cross that stage for graduation,” Pryor said. “Your journey has just begun.”

Mike Richardson, the alumni events director for DMAIG, said he hopes all alumni get out and volunteer for the cause.

“Being here, especially this weekend, reminds me that there’s so much hope for the future,” Richardson, a 1981 Penn State alumni, said. “You’re never too old to reengage with (THON).”

Richardson was a dancer in 1979 and was THON’s overall chair in 1980. Now, over 40 years later, he’s seen the way the organization has grown and changed over time.

“(THON) continues to be the most amazing thing you can be a part of, whether you’re a volunteer, you’re a dancer, you’re a captain, a director,” Richardson said.

As she stands 46 hours, Giorgianni said she’s thrilled to be back in the BJC for THON Weekend.

“The experience is just something that I could have never dreamed of, and I’m so grateful to be here,” Giorgianni said. “And then, getting to come back after I graduated is just so surreal, and it makes me so emotional, and I really am just so grateful.”

MORE THONTENT

title: Landon Simpson finishes 15th at Winter Cup, Josh Karnes named to Senior Men’s National Team
author: Caroline Riley | The Daily Collegian
description:
The 2026 Winter Cup is not just a one-day competition of the best gymnasts in America, but the sole determinant of making the USA Men’s National Team. Penn State sent only Landon Simpson to the event, but he had a familiar face in blue and white alum Josh Karnes.

Simpson opened his competition on the vault alongside three gymnasts from the University of Michigan, including Olympian Fred Richard. He slightly underrotated his two and a half, taking just a small hop back for a 13.800.

After his big vault, Simpson sat in 4th place after the 1st rotation. The junior moved to the parallel bars in the second rotation, where he managed just a 12.050, dropping him to 12th after two events.

On highbar the Bellefonte, Pennsylvania, native gained a spot, slotting into 11th place after his 12.400 performance. This was the 17th-best score on highbar in the competition. Simpson then moved to the floor. Despite one out of bounds, he managed a 12.500.

Simpson closed out the competition on the pommel horse and still rings. He put up an 8.100 execution score on the horse for a 12.600 total. He doesn’t have the most difficulty-packed routine on the rings, but competed it well for an 11.900.

Simpson finished the competition in 15th place, short of initial selection to the Men’s National Team with a 75.250 in the all-around. Karnes, who did not compete in every event, was named to the team after a successful day at the cup. The alum managed a 14.408 on parallel bars, a 13.308 on high bar, a 13.505 on pommel horse, and showed off a much more difficult rings routine for a 13.602.

Simpson’s rotation-mate and Wolverine Richard won the competition with an 84.359. The remaining athletes for both the Senior Men’s National Team and Senior Developmental Team will be named later.

Simpson and the rest of the Nittany Lions will be back in action at 1 p.m. on Sunday, March, 1 in Rec Hall as they welcome Springfield College.

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: McKenna pots game-winning goal in No. 6 Penn State men’s hockey's win over Ohio State in overtime
author: Chase Fisher | The Daily Collegian
description:
Sixth-ranked Penn State and Ohio State battled it out on Saturday night for the final time in the 2025-26 regular season campaign. While Game 1’s storylines weren’t replicated, Saturday’s series finale went down to the wire in a late-match thriller.

The sixth-ranked Nittany Lions defeated Ohio State in overtime by a final score of 5-4, sweeping the season series against the Buckeyes after a three-goal comeback on Saturday.

“Just pumped on this weekend,” head coach Guy Gadowsky said. “Obviously, what a great THON weekend. And for us, the crowds were awesome. I think this weekend had a little bit of everything. Obviously, saw some extremely special performances last night. But for the team to be down 4-1. We started two guys down, lost another one during the game, had only nine forwards. And I mean, they just dug deep.”

Here’s how Penn State took down Ohio State on Saturday, along with an injury report and what’s up ahead for the Nittany Lions.

First period

The action on Saturday night began in Ohio State’s favor following a slower-paced first five minutes of game time. Left defenseman Sam McGinley fired a wrist shot from the point straight to the boards behind Nittany Lions goaltender Kevin Reidler during an extended offensive-zone time period.

The puck ricocheted from behind Reidler to the front of the net, where fourth-line center Sam Deckhut controlled it and beat Penn State’s netminder up high, giving the Buckeyes a 1-0 lead 5:24 into the game.

Ohio State’s momentum was stymied rather quickly, as the Buckeyes committed three consecutive penalties, with the third taking the severity of a five-minute major, after captain Davis Burnside delivered three crosschecks, including one to the face, to center Nic Chin-DeGraves.

Penn State was unable to convert on its first two power-play opportunities; however, four minutes into its five-minute major, left wing Shea Van Olm potted his third goal in the last two nights, going bar down after a feed from center Reese Laubach, tying the game 1-1.

Van Olm’s tally came just minutes after Reidler stopped a breakaway and two other chances Ohio State had shorthanded. The Swedish goaltender kept things from unraveling for the Nittany Lions, which led to a score and an eventual 1-1 tie heading into the first intermission.

Second period

Ohio State retook the momentum quickly in the second period. Shortly after right-wing Adam Eisele rang the post near the three-minute mark, Nittany Lions’ left wing Gavin McKenna was sent to the penalty box for a minor penalty of hooking.

The Buckeyes scored a minute into the man-advantage, with right wing Felix Caron beating Reidler on a wrist shot from the left dot, retaking a 2-1 lead.

Two minutes later, Ohio State stayed on the attack as center Max Montes won an offensive-zone draw to left wing Niall Crocker, where Crocker quickly dished the puck to defenseman Broten Sabo at the point.

Sabo fired a one-timer to the net that Deckhut redirected past Reidler, extending the Buckeyes lead to 3-1 with his second goal of the game.

The second-period dominance continued for Ohio State with just under eight minutes remaining in the frame. Left wing James Hong retrieved a loose puck at the bottom of the left circle before feeding Montes across the crease.

Montes controlled the puck and beat Reidler on his glove side, padding the Buckeyes’ lead to three.

However, Penn State answered back at the 5:24 mark, as left wing Lev Katzin went coast-to-coast before feeding right wing JJ Wiebusch at the left circle.

Wiebusch went backhand to forehand, shooting a wrist shot past Ohio State netminder Kristoffer Eberly and cutting the Nittany Lions' deficit to 4-2.

“It's nothing new for JJ, he's a goal scorer,” Gadowsky said. “He has a knack for big moments as well. He's another one that changed very well for the team. He does a lot of things that he doesn't get credit for from a cultural standpoint with the team.”

Third period

Just seconds into an interference infraction by defenseman Ethan Straky, McKenna faked a one-timer and threaded a slap pass to Wiebusch on the backdoor of Eberly to cut Penn State’s deficit to 4-3.

Shortly after, Laubach beamed a cross-ice pass to left wing Matt DiMarsico, where he got sandwiched by two Ohio State defenders near the slot and lost the puck.

Right defenseman Jarod Crespo controlled the loose puck and fired a wrist shot, beating Eberly on his glove side to tie the game 4-4.

The remaining 15 minutes of the frame were back-and-forth action with minimal offensive chances. Ohio State ended regulation on a power play, which leaked into an overtime period.

Overtime

Caron rang iron at the beginning of the overtime period; however, Ohio State was unable to score on its man-advantage.

Minutes later, McKenna dangled around a Buckeye defender and potted the game-winning goal, beating Eberly on his blocker side, gifting Penn State a 5-4 win.

“When (all the injuries) started, we just put a challenge to the team that you got to find a way to get some wins,” Gadowsky said. “Obviously, we have a lot to play for, both in the Big Ten and on a national scene, but like I said, we’re leaking oil right now, and they just have to find ways to win, and yesterday was a completely different game. And tonight, I'm just again — I'm just so impressed with them tonight to come back and find a way to win, it's just really impressive.”

Injury report

Penn State defenseman and right wing Nick Fascia, who exited the game last night after being cut by a skate, was inactive on Saturday.

“We were concerned that it would be a long-term thing, and we're optimistic that it won't,” Gadowsky said.

Center Ben Schoen hit the boards hard and was dangling his right shoulder while skating slowly off the ice. He returned to the game in the third period but exited again later on. Gadowsky said he would’ve been able to return if the team needed him.

Up next

Penn State will travel to South Bend for a two-game set with Notre Dame next weekend. Game 1 is set for 7:05 p.m. ET Friday. Game 2 is scheduled for 6 p.m. ET Saturday.

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: From mini-THON to the big leagues | Braylee Klinger represents Penn State Harrisburg
author: Reese Igou | The Daily Collegian
description:
Dancer representatives from many Penn State Commonwealth campuses have gathered in the Bryce Jordan Center during THON Weekend 2026 to dance in support of THON’s mission to find a cure for childhood cancer. Penn State Harrisburg had three representatives, one being Braylee Klinger.

Klinger is a second-year studying biomedical engineering and is the Primary Chair of Penn State Harrisburg Benefiting THON. While it’s only her second year at Penn State, Klinger has been involved in the THON community for many years.

“Dancing from Penn State Harrisburg means representing my school here,” Klinger said. “We are here to represent the Commonwealths, so I’m really glad to be here… also really glad that I can dance because I've been doing THON for so long.”

Kaley Haldeman and Laura Thangi, executive board members from Penn State Harrisburg Benefiting THON, could not imagine a better representative for their campus.

“Her overall dedication to [THON] is really just inspiring,” Haldeman, second vice president of Penn State Harrisburg Benefiting THON, said.

Described as passionate and positive, Braylee was selected by her committee because of her energy and true love for THON and everything it represents.

“She just has such a helping heart and is just an amazing person overall,” Thangi, Rules and Regulations representative, said. “We’re very excited for her.”

As a Pennsylvania resident, Klinger participated in Mini-THON from kindergarten through high school. Four Diamonds Mini-THON is a K-12 student leadership empowerment opportunity that helps students gain philanthropy, communication and critical thinking skills.

After attending a leadership summit through this program, she was inspired to become a leader to help support Mini-THON during her senior year of high school. That passion continued to grow, eventually leading her into the executive position she holds at Harrisburg today. Fueled by enthusiasm and love, Klinger works with her colleagues to plan fundraising events for the cause and works through logistics.

“Dancing for THON has been a big dream of mine,” Klinger said.

Klinger’s passion for THON not only stems from her love for supporting the families her organization sponsors, but also from her mom.

In elementary school, Klinger’s mother was diagnosed with thyroid cancer and still has cancer today. This life-changing diagnosis has motivated her even more to help search for a cure and fundraise for cancer research through THON.

“THON gives people a lot of hope that one day there will be a cure,” Klinger said. “I carry that hope with me.”

To keep her dancing throughout THON Weekend, Klinger thinks about the families and children who have been continuously supported throughout all of THON’s efforts to fundraise and create a welcoming and loving environment.

“THON has done so much, not only in fundraising, but also for the families,” Klinger said. “THON gives a great community to their families and I think something very important is when the kids come to THON … they don’t have to feel like they’re a child with cancer, they can just feel like they’re a child. It’s very important to give that support to our families.”

From her mom to the Four Diamonds families Penn State Harrisburg supports, Klinger's deep love for THON and its mission is evident through everything she does. As a dancer, she’s ready to stand for 46 hours to stand behind the THON mission.

“My favorite THON quote is that ‘one day we will dance in celebration, but today we dance for a cure,’” Klinger said. “I feel like that really encapsulates what THON means. It’s hope for the future and what we’re doing right now is impacting the future.”

Penn State Harrisburg Benefiting THON stands in the crowd, cheering on Braylee and her fellow dancers as she dances for the remaining hours of the weekend.

“She’s doing great,” Haldeman said. “She really is the glue holding them all together and is being that bright, positive energy.”

title: No. 19 Penn State falls to No. 20 Iowa on the road in Big Ten matchup
author: Caroline Riley | The Daily Collegian
description:
With No. 20 Iowa hot on its heels in the rankings, No. 19 Penn State entered Iowa City needing a big day. Despite scoring 49.000+ on all four events, the Nittany Lions weren’t able to overcome the Hawkeyes on their turf.

Penn State lost 196.525-197.075, but improved from last week’s 196.000.

Rotation One

Penn State opened the meet with a solid 49.200 on the uneven bars to take an early .125 lead over the Hawkeyes.

Jessica Johanson had a few errors in the leadoff spot. The graduate student had low amplitude on her Tkatchev, was short on the handstand of her half pirouette on low bar, and finished with a big step back on her double layout dismount for a 9.775.

Ellie Monahan rushed her pak salto, which caused her to labor her half pirouette a bit on the low bar, and she slipped her hand. She was a bit shaky throughout and took a few shuffles on her full-twisting double back dismount, scoring a 9.575.

Elizabeth Leary, coming off last week’s career-high, got the team back on track. She nailed her Jaeger to shootover combination. She was short on her blind full but stuck her dismount to keep up her 9.800+ streak with a 9.850.

Leary’s stick was contagious. Ava Cash, also coming off her career-high 9.900 last week, nailed her huge piked jaeger with great distance from the bar. She was also short on her blind full but stuck the landing for a 9.875.

Ava Piedrahita had a nice Maloney to Pak Salto combination. She nailed her handstands throughout and stuck the full-twisting double back dismount for another 9.875.

Dani Latronica anchored with a 9.825. She had some bent elbows on her piked Jaeger but held her shootover to handstand. She had just a small hop back on her dismount, but did her job to erase Monahan’s low score.

Rotation Two

Despite a decent rotation on the vault, a huge 9.950 and 9.925 from Daisy Bowles and Aurelie Tran on the bars for Iowa catapulted the Hawkeyes ahead of the Nittany Lions 98.525-98.200 at the halfway point.

Leary opened with a stuck Yurchenko full in the leadoff spot for a 9.800. Kayla Bender, in her collegiate debut, had a lot of power on her Yurchenko full. The freshman wasn’t able to dial it in, taking a big step back for a 9.750.

Latronica piked down on her Yurchenko full, causing her to take a small hop back for a 9.775. Cash, coming off a 9.825 last week, dialed the landings back in for Penn State. She took just the smallest shuffle, scoring a 9.850.

Johanson scored just a 9.725. The graduate student had a nice vault in the air, but a huge hop and an additional step back on the landing. Piedrahita anchored with just the smallest hop on the landing for a 9.825.

Rotation Three

Needing to start chipping away at Iowa’s lead, Penn State was solid on the floo,r trailing the Hawkeyes by just .175 heading into the final rotation.

Leary led off with a 9.750. She opened with a clean front double full to a punch front and stuck her second one and a half to front layout cold.

Monahan followed with a 9.800. She overrotated her front double twist just a bit and had some soft knees in the air. Her back leg was a little low on the first jump in her combination. She was solid on her front lay to front full to end the routine.

Amani Herring, in her first floor routine of the year, nailed her punch front to double back. She was a bit shy on one of her leaps, but the senior nailed her one and a half to front layout for a 9.825.

Johanson followed with a big 9.850. She had a small shuffle with the front foot on her opening double pike. She controlled her one and a half to front half to finish strong.

Kalea McElligott opened with a great double back. She hit her ring jump series and danced right out of her one and a half to front layout for a big 9.875.

Alyssa Kramer anchored with another 9.875. She perfectly stuck her front double full. She did lose balance during her leap series, but finished strong with a Rudi to layout-stepout.

Rotation Four

Penn State went six for six on the beam, but it wasn’t enough to overtake Iowa, which had a huge rotation on the floor.

Monahan led off with a 9.775. She was a little slow on her combination, but pulled it together for a solid leadoff. Ashley Maul bounced back from last week with a 9.750. She had a tiny balance check on her triple series and the smallest shuffle forward on her dismount.

McElligott also bounced back from a miss last week. She nailed her triple series. She was a little short of 180 degrees on her second leap, but took just a small hop forward on her one and a half for a big 9.875.

Herring kept the momentum going with a 9.750. She had to bend at the hips during her leap connection but was able to hold on to her dismount. Kramer nailed her backhandspring, layout stepout series. She was shy of the split position during her leap series, but almost stuck her dismount for a 9.775.

Latronica ended the meet with a career-tying and event-winning 9.925. She was fluid in her front aerial to beat jump and solid in her acro series. She stuck her gainer full off the beam to end the night on a high note. The sophomore continued her 9.800+ streak on beam.

Up Next

Penn State hosts the 2026 B1G 4 Meet, welcoming Michigan, Nebraska and Washington at 6 p.m. on Saturday, February 28th.

title: 'It’s really helped us to heal' | The Britigans praise Four Diamonds programming
author: Emma Naysmith | The Daily Collegian
description:
While Four Diamonds is well known for providing financial support, Paige Britigan and her family say Four Diamonds has given them experiences like no other.

The Britigan's made their way up to Happy Valley from Quarryville, Pennsylvania, for their second in-person THON this weekend.

17-year old Paige was diagnosed with Burkitt lymphoma in 2022 after seeking medical assistance for what she and her family initially thought was appendicitis.

“It all set in, I had cancer. But I was really panicking about my family and how we were going to pay the hospital bills,” Paige said in an interview her mother allowed her to participate in. “And that’s when our social worker came in and she told us about (Four Diamonds), and it felt like there was a weight lifted off my chest.”

Paige’s mother Dawn said life stopped after the diagnosis. She said she began to live in the hospital, even having packages delivered there.

Dawn called the organization’s support “overwhelming” and even went as far as weekly massages for her and her husband.

“It's just everything's cancer and it's in your face and just that little bit of 15 minutes they take you to a quiet spa room and that's all provided,” Dawn said. “Without Four Diamonds, those things would not happen.”

Likewise, Paige’s sister, Mackenzie, said a lot of her experience with Four Diamonds has been about supporting the family as a whole.

Last summer, through Four Diamonds, Paige had the opportunity to travel to Montana to attend Camp Māk-A-Dream alongside other children. The camp provides cost-free programming for cancer survivors and their families.

“We were there for five days,” she said. “We didn't have any electronics, and we just got to go out in the sun, there was no humidity, and it was so nice.”

This THON Weekend, Paige said she was able to reconnect with some of the campers she had flown out west with.

Paige plans to continue attending in the future because it not only gives her the opportunity to continue healing herself, but she gets to meet others with similar stories.

“What made me like Montana so much is that we went around and shared our stories and what happened to us,” Paige said. “Hearing that other kids my age, kind of went through the same thing … I feel grounded in a way.”

Dawn recalls first attending the Family Carnival breakfast and being impressed with THON volunteers and the “meaningful” work they do at such a young age.

The Britigan family is paired with Penn State Brandywine Benefitting THON through the “Adopt-a-Family” program.

Paige said the students supporting her from Brandywine are “really sweet.” She said its ONE PIECE theme this year is inspired by her and her father’s shared love of the show, and that the group even traveled to her high school’s mini-THON last weekend.

Penn State Brandywine Benefitting THON Chair Sara Hallahan said her organization was excited to have been invited and to support Paige.

“It was amazing, seeing her interact with her friends and do something that was close to her, and that she wanted us to be a part of,” Hallahan, a third-year studying human development and family studies, said.

Hallahan said she admires Paige’s confidence and is proud of the Britigans for everything they continue to overcome.

“They give us purpose for everything,” Hallahan said. “Seeing them come to events and seeing them at THON Weekend, we have a meaning for what we do.”

After being called a “favorite” and one of the “easiest” patients by her nurses, Paige rang the bell in symbolism of remission in May 2023.

From watching THON virtually to their journey to attending in-person events, Dawn, on behalf of her family, said, “It’s really helped us to heal.”

MORE THONTENT

title: Penn State women’s basketball keeps postseason hopes alive with win at Rutgers
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Hot off a win against Northwestern, Penn State traveled to New Jersey with a Big Ten tournament bid on the line.

Despite a poor start, the Lady Lions earned the nail-biting victory on the road, defeating Rutgers 87-78.

Turnovers and fouls were an early issue for the blue and white, who lost the ball three times and sent the Scarlet Knights to the line twice

Up 3-0 from three-of-four shooting from the charity stripe, Rutgers earned a fast-break three-pointer to further extend its lead. After blocking Kiyomi McMiller’s shot, the Scarlet Knights went up 8-0 with no sign of life from the Lady Lions.

Following four minutes of silence from Penn State, freshman Tèa Clèante put the first points on the board with a triple.

The Lady Lions earned a 7-0 run to make it a one-point game with a pair of free throws from freshman Nyla McFadden and another basket from Clèante.

Coming out of a timeout, 6-foot-3 forward Imani Lester hit a pair of triples for Rutgers to re-extend their lead, but five straight points from McMiller, a Merkle basket over a triple team and Cleante’s second triple of the day gave Penn State a three-point lead to end the quarter.

Rutgers opened the second quarter with another 5-0 run — looking unstoppable — but was quickly shut back down by McMiller’s homecoming performance.

McMiller scored six of Penn State’s next 10 points as it went on a 10-1 run. Her, Merkle and Clèante recorded all but four points for the blue and white midway through the quarter.

For the Scarlet Knights, Lester was the primary offense with 15 points to that point. When the two squads met in January, Lester recorded a career-best 19 points.

Up seven points with 2:45 left in the quarter, Penn State was scoreless for over two minutes as Rutgers went on a 3-0 run, but it was ended with McMiller’s 18th point of the half.

Both sides exchanged fire to open the third quarter with shots from Lester, Nene Ndiaye and Zachara Perkins for the Scarlet Knights. Merkle and McMiller once again did most of the heavy lifting for Penn State, paired with a Moriah Murray triple.

After seven straight points from the blue and white — another five coming from McMiller — Rutgers had its turn on a run as it went 7-2 to make it an eight-point game.

As much of Saturday’s game was, it was apparent it was a match between the current No. 16 and No. 18 teams in the Big Ten. Another period of slow offense and high turnovers occurred at the end of the third quarter, as only 10 points were scored between both teams in the final five minutes.

The Scarlet Knights opened the quarter with a quick layup to cut their deficit to eight points. After a jump ball was called between Merkle and Swann, the 6-foot-6 center was issued a flagrant as Rutgers cut the deficit again with a pair of free throws.

Following the foul, Merkle was targeted on the next two offensive possessions, going to the free throw line on back-to-back drives before McMiller made it a 10-point game — crossing 30 points on the day for her fifth straight 30-point game.

However, the deficit was cut again as Perkins and Kaylah Ivey made back-to-back triples for the Scarlet Knights, followed by Penn State’s own 3-pointer, courtesy of McFadden.

McMiller’s high-scoring outing continued as she ended the day tying her career-best 37 points, shooting a season-best 66.7% as she helped the Lady Lions keep their lead over the final minutes.

While Rutgers continued to score and battle in the final minutes, the blue and white found Merkle in the paint and McMiller with her flashy ball handling which earned the victory for the second straight game and kept Penn State’s postseason hopes alive.

Up next

The Lady Lions return home to host USC at 6 p.m. on Wednesday.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State OL Anthony Donkoh doesn’t believe he’s reached his ‘full potential’
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
With multiple departures and additions on the offensive line, Penn State is set to have a new-look group up front in 2026, but it’ll have one returning starter in Anthony Donkoh.

Matt Campbell raved about offensive lineman Anthony Donkoh earlier this month, praising his team leadership and saying he “got a chance to be one of the absolute best players in our football program.” Donkoh sees that opportunity heading into his redshirt junior year.

“There's a lot of meat left on the bone for me,” Donkoh said at the THON Explorers Program on Saturday. “I've not even reached my full potential. That’s really exciting, especially when the new staff coming in and talking to these guys, and these guys are great people. That's one of the biggest reasons why I stayed. I love these guys.”

Three days after Penn State’s Pinstripe Bowl win over Clemson, Donkoh announced his return to the team for 2026. He said the Nittany Lions’ new coaching staff made him a priority during roster retention efforts.

Donkoh has been a starter for the Nittany Lions the past two seasons, and he’s showcased some versatility, playing both tackle and guard. This past year, Donkoh played 482 snaps at right guard and 195 snaps at right tackle, according to Pro Football Focus.

“Personally, I love playing tackle,” Donkoh said. “Tackle is just fun because I can be able to just play the way I want to play. I felt like playing guard, I was very constricted a lot of times and not able to play to my strengths, per se, but at the end of the day, I was going to play whatever position that I needed to play and I was gonna play at a high level.”

Donkoh gave credit to former Penn State offensive line coach Phil Trautwein, who departed for the same role at Florida following Campbell’s hiring. He said Trautwein was “process oriented” and “technique heavy,” which is what Donkoh is going to keep with him as his career moves forward.

The 6-foot-5, 320-pounder said new offensive line coach Ryan Clanton harps on fully using your body to play a position at a high level and to use a player’s strengths. This made Donkoh “very excited” to have a conversation with Clanton, saying he has confidence in him to be his offensive line coach.

Donkoh, a 247Sports composite 4-star out of high school in the 2023 class, has quickly progressed into a consistent starter for Penn State. Now, as one of the veterans on the offensive line, he believes he could make another leap in his development.

“I feel like I can take that next step this spring, this summer, going in this season,” Donkoh said Saturday. “With the leadership stuff, I'm just going to continue to be myself. I'm not going to play on an act or try to put my fun to try to impress everybody, because people gravitate towards you and your most genuine self.”

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State players spend time with THON families during Explorers Program
author: Lyle Alenstein | Wrestling Editor
description:
Penn State has had a lot of changes with the program over the last few months, but on Saturday afternoon, there was a familiar event that took place in the Lasch Building.

The Nittany Lions hosted their annual Explorers Program in support of THON, the world's largest student-run philanthropy that aims to provide emotional and financial support to Four Diamonds families who have been impacted by childhood cancer.

Despite all the moving parts and moments of uncertainty around the team, Penn State came together for an afternoon bigger than football.

“I think anytime you get to make a difference on kids and impact their lives, you should automatically take that opportunity,” offensive lineman Cooper Cousins said. “There’s kids out there that aren’t fortunate and there’s kids out there that are going through worse stuff than we’re going through right now.”

The players took families around the facilities in an effort to brighten their day. Cousins whacked wiffle balls, offensive lineman Owen Aliciene had his face painted, defensive tackle De’Andre Cook sang karaoke and Matt Campbell signed autographs.

There was laughter, ice cream and smiles for those who got to spend an afternoon with the football players.

“We’re granted and given a lot of things in this world,” right tackle Anthony Donkoh, who had his face painted for the third year in a row, said. “For us to see these families and their daily struggles, is extremely humbling. These people are fighting every day for their child. Their kid is fighting every day to stay alive. Really brings perspective.”

The afternoon also gave an opportunity for players to strengthen their bond with each other. While there was a group of players who had participated in an Explorers Program, or even multiple, there’s a large number of transfers who hadn’t.

But they embraced it with open arms. Syracuse cornerback transfer Ibn McDaniels rode around a mini bike with a child hanging onto him, while Oklahoma State defensive tackle transfer Armstrong Nnodim threw a football with another kid.

Iowa State linebacker transfer Caleb Bacon reminded the kid he was with that “this is your day.”

“It’s good to see how these guys interact in these sorts of things,” offensive tackle Garrett Sexton said.

Cousins talked about how players are now using their NIL money and resources to launch a team fundraiser. The idea was inspired by defensive end Max Granville, who brought it up at a team meeting. For whatever amount the players raise, Campbell will double it by the time their links close.

It’s more than just THON that the Nittany Lions are getting themselves involved with. They’ve been going to elementary schools and packing lunches every Tuesday for those in need. It’s all part of an effort to contribute to the greater good.

“Anytime you get to give back to your community and really bring kids in and see what they're going through, and being able to spend a day with them, it's huge,” Cousins said. “As a team and a new team, as players, it's also a challenge for us too. It's us going out in the community, seeing what we can do for these kids, not only for the kids, but the community of Penn State.”

There were returning faces to the Explorers Program, and new ones for both players and coaches.

The one thing that hasn’t changed for Penn State through a whirlwind of an offseason is how much the afternoon meant for them and the THON families they interacted with — that was on display Saturday.

“It means everything to us and the team, and we have a platform like this — the greatest thing you can do is give back,” long snapper Blaise Sokach-Minnick said. “Penn State's community is all about supporting each other, so it’s always a special day to spend time with the families.”

MORE THON COVERAGE

title: Penn State men’s volleyball wraps up weekend series with victory over Harvard
author: Chase Mitchell | The Daily Collegian
description:
After picking up a victory in a 3-0 sweep that saw Owen Rose set a program record 14 blocks, Penn State welcomed Harvard back to Rec Hall for a Saturday afternoon match to wrap up the weekend series.

Not much changed overnight, though, as the No. 16 ranked Nittany Lions once again emerged victorious, picking up a 3-1 win over Harvard for their sixth-straight victory to advance to 10-4 on the season.

The Crimson got on the board first in the opening set with a quick Adrian Shevchuk kill, but points were traded back and forth in the following moments until Penn State eventually went on a 3-1 scoring run backed by kills from Sean Harvey and Jaidyn Bethel.

Thanks to that run, the blue and white grabbed an early 7-5 lead in the first set as Bethel became the first Nittany Lion with multiple kills on the day.

Siding out at a 100% rate, Penn State extended its lead to 12-8 after an impressive Tristan Hassell kill and more action from Bethel and Harvey.

Harvey was recently named EIVA Offensive Player of the Week for the second time this season after last weekend’s pair of wins over Princeton. He hit .333 with 4.25 kills, 1.38 digs, and 0.75 blocks per set over Penn State’s two wins.

The blue and white were really starting to pull away when it furthered its lead to 20-14 after an ace from Harvey that followed several service errors by the Crimson. The growing Penn State momentum forced Brian Baise and Harvard to take a timeout.

Wearing pink and black ‘Happy Valley’ alternate jerseys, the Nittany Lions captured the first set 25-19, though the Crimson started making some late noise, with Sawyer Nichols notching a kill on top of forcing some blue and white errors.

The second set got off to a nearly identical start as the first, with Harvard drawing first blood, but trading scores with the blue and white.

Nichols notched two more kills as Hassell got in on a triple block with Bethel and Rose before recording four more kills, leading to a 9-9 tie in the early stages of the second frame.

The high-flying Hassell has had a very strong start to his freshman campaign. The 6-foot-6 Toronto native reached a season-high mark of 13 kills against Lewis in January and had four digs and four blocks against Harvard on Friday night.

Mark Pavlik was forced into a timeout as the Crimson went on a 4-0 scoring run and went up 13-9 over a rattled Penn State unit, sparked by two aces on Nichols’ serves.

It was all Harvard in the second frame as it moved up to a 16-11 lead, but the Nittany Lions closed the gap and went on a 4-1 scoring run to cut their deficit to 15-17, due to three consecutive errors by the Crimson.

A 23-17 Harvard lead made it seem like the second set was all but over, but a Rose ace and another Hassell kill made things interesting again as it shifted into a 23-21 narrow lead for Baise’s squad.

Despite a late push, the Crimson shut the door on the Nittany Lions and stole a 25-22 victory in the second set by way of a Zach Berty kill off the assist from Shevchuk.

Again, the third set began in a very similar fashion as the previous two, but with Penn State kicking off the scoring this time with another Hassell kill as he moved up to 10 on the day.

Hassell’s 11th kill of the day made it a 5-5 stalemate, with much of the scoring coming from errors from both ends.

Penn State held a slight 14-12 lead after six kills from Harvey in quick succession before a media timeout in the ever-important third set.

A weird series of events led to a replay of a point that ended up going in the Nittany Lions’ favor that gave them an 18-14 lead, but the Crimson were quick to respond to close the gap before another Baise timeout.

Backed by a 5-0 scoring run in which Hassell, Bethel, Rose and Harvey all recorded kills, Penn State seized control and won the third set, crushing Harvard 25-16 to move one frame closer to a victory and series sweep.

Once more, Harvard opened up the scoring in the fourth set thanks to a Nichols ace that was followed by back-and-forth scoring plays.

The point trading continued until Hassell wrote back with an ace of his own to tie his career and season-high mark of 14 kills as Penn State moved up to an early 7-5 lead.

A Michael Schwob ace made it a 10-7 lead for the blue and white before the Crimson went on a 3-2 scoring run to tighten the gap, though Penn State wasn’t letting its momentum fully dissolve.

Just before a media timeout, a Gaige Gabriel kill gave the Nittany Lions a two-point lead after Harvard went on another little run that made it an even tighter game.

The sophomore recorded a season-high mark of 13 points against St. Thomas Aquinas earlier this season. He was named an honorable mention All-EIVA last season.

After notching seven kills in the third set, Harvey tallied another in the fourth frame as he extended his squad’s lead to 20-15 as they moved closer to the victory.

There wasn’t much drama from that point on as Rose notched another ace before Bethel put it to bed as Penn State picked up a 25-19 win in the fourth set to secure a 3-1 match victory and put a cap on the weekend.

Up Next

Penn State hosts Merrimack next Friday at Rec Hall before a lengthy absence, with no more matches until the first week in March.

That match with Merrimack is scheduled for 7 p.m.

MORE VOLLEYBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State softball’s offense continues to roll, earns big victory over Notre Dame
author: Ashley Beres | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State looked to extend its even-game winning streak in its fourth game at the Space U Classic in Orlando on Saturday afternoon. Meanwhile, the Fighting Irish entered the contest still searching for its first victory in the tournament after losing two straight.

The Nittany Lions are now riding an eight-game winning streak after they earned another lopsided 14-2 win over Notre Dame behind their red-hot bats.

Coach Clarisa Crowell started ace Bridget Nemeth, while Notre Dame tasked sophomore Brianne Weiss with shutting down the Nittany Lions' scorching offense. The left-handed pitcher couldn’t slow down the blue and white early.

After a scoreless top half of the opening frame, Penn State took the lead in the bottom half. Center fielder Natalie Lieto led off with a four-pitch walk, and then the Nittany Lions' home run leader took care of the rest.

First baseman Allison Oneacre smothered a two-run home run to left center, giving Penn State the early 2-0 lead. This marked the freshman’s seventh home run as opposing pitchers still can’t contain her offensively.

A few small ball hits and a costly error allowed the Fighting Irish to strike back quickly in the second. Sydny Poeck poked an infield single to second baseman Michela Barbanente and Caroline O’Brien followed by reaching base off a successful bunt.

After a fielder’s choice out to shortstop Kaylie Walters, Kaia Cortes stepped into the box with runners on first and second. The sophomore powered a one-out double to right center, driving in Poeck from third. However, right fielder Cara Bohner swiftly got to the ball and relayed the throw to second baseman Barbanente, and the second runner was out at home.

However, following a good defensive play by the blue and white’s defense, Avery Houlihan reached on a throwing error by Walters at shortstop. The mistake allowed Notre Dame to tie the game at two, as Cortes scored from second.

Despite the sloppy end of the second frame, Penn State struck back in the next inning and piled on the runs. The Nittany Lions’ offense has been putting up crooked numbers all season, and that theme continued with six runs plated.

Catcher Macy Chamberlin snuck a leadoff single through the left side of the infield. Bohner and Breanna Hanik followed with two singles of their own. Walters made up for her error in the previous inning with an RBI single down the left field line.

Oneacre drew a one-out walk, scoring Hanik and keeping the bases loaded. Weiss felt the pressure while facing the heart of Penn State’s lineup and hit designated player Brooke Klosowicz.

With two runs already scored and just one out, Notre Dame turned to its bullpen. However, senior Micaela Kastor couldn’t stop the bleeding. Barbanente lined a two-RBI double to right.

A few batters later, Bohner walked in another run and extended Penn State’s lead, 8-2. Now with a six-run lead, Crowell brought in junior McKenna Young.

While the Fighting Irish went down quietly in the third, the Nittany Lions’ bats continued pouring it on. Walters led off with a walk, and Lieto followed with a single through the left side. Oneacre grounded out, but advanced both runners into scoring position.

Klosowicz laid down a perfect bunt single and scored Walters from third. The onslaught continued as Barbanente poked an RBI single through the right side. Notre Dame turned to freshman Molly Conner, but she felt the pressure and threw a wild pitch that reached the backstop and scored Klosowicz from third.

The blue and white now held a commanding 11-2 lead going into the fourth and the Fighting Irish had no answers on either side of the plate. Barring a leadoff walk, Young retired Notre Dame with ease once again.

The Nittany Lions added three more runs in the fourth frame off three hits, two walks and a throwing error by Houlihan at shortstop. After holding off the Fighting Irish from scoring in the fifth, the blue and white earned its 12th win of the season.

Up Next

Penn State takes its eight-game winning streak into its finale game at the Space U Classic against Charleston Southern at 9 a.m. Sunday.

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State baseball ends Richmond series with Saturday afternoon defeat
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State’s slow start to the season continued on Saturday afternoon as it wrapped up its first series of the year against Richmond.

After winning Friday night’s battle and getting shut out on Saturday morning, the Nittany Lions fell to the Spiders 4-3 to finish the weekend.

Freshman pitcher Isaiah Shayter took the mound for the second game this season. Last week, he pitched 3.1 innings against Kansas State with one strikeout and six runs.

However, the young pitcher found more success in his start against Richmond. After a pair of walks and three straight outs to end Penn State’s at bat, Shayter pitched one hit alongside three outs.

The Nittany Lions saw brief success to start the second inning as graduate first baseman Kevin Karstetter singled to first base.

After left fielder Jesse Jaconski struck out and Karstetter was thrown out with an attempted second base steal, Joey DeMucci recorded the blue and white’s second straight strikeout to return back to the field.

Richmond’s second inning was just as uneventful as the first, but grew more exciting at the bottom of the third.

With Shayter on the mound, the Spiders hit a double to center field and took third on shortly after with Shayter’s first error of the game. With a sacrifice fly from Richmond’s Trevor Dosenbach, the Spiders earned their first run as Noah Haught came in from third base.

After a pair of balls and a strike, Dylan Winebrenner swung and scored with the first home run of the game, putting Richmond up 2-0.

The Spiders earned another pair of outs to end the inning before the Nittany Lions began fighting back on the scoreboard. Right fielder Jack Porter led the way with his first hit of the day on a double, followed by a double from Karstetter, who ultimately scored Porter.

Penn State earned three straight outs and sent junior Mason Horwat to the mound, who threw out three straight hitters after starting his day by hitting Ryan Recio.

Richmond put reliever Mauricio Rodriguez in to start the fifth inning, who struck out three straight Nittany Lions. As Horwat went back to the mound, he allowed a pair of hits, but the blue and white ultimately earned the double play courtesy of Preston Yaucher and Karstetter.

Rodriguez struggled to start the sixth inning after walking third baseman Bryce Molinaro, hitting Porter and walking Karstetter. With bases loaded and no outs, Richmond pulled senior Chayse Gruber from the bullpen.

Despite the chance to score, the Nittany Lions recorded two strikeouts and a fly out to close the top of the sixth inning with the score unchanged.

After an inning and a half produced just a single from Richmond, Penn State evened the score at the top of the eighth with Porter’s first home run of the year. While the Nittany Lions had both Jaconski and Karstetter on first and third base, respectively, neither was able to find home to end the top of the inning.

With the score tied, coach Mike Gambino turned to sophomore lefty Matthew VanOstenbridge to take the mound with freshman Avery Smith subbing in at catcher.

However, things didn’t go as planned, as the Spiders earned a pair of home runs to take a 4-2 lead. With the game on the line, Penn State changed things up on the mound again, this time putting junior Kyle Emmons on the mound.

After Emmons closed out the eighth inning with no more action, the Nittany Lions found themselves clawing back at the lead with another home run. Designated hitter Michael Anderson cut the deficit to one run, but couldn’t find home again as they fell to 2-4 on the year.

Up next

The Nittany Lions take on Indiana State in a weekend series, kicking off at 6 p.m. on Friday in Nassau, Bahamas.

MORE BASEBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State men's basketball thumped by No. 9 Nebraska on the road behind Pryce Sanford's career night
author: Myles Hannak | The Daily Collegian
description:
The 2026 regular season winds down on the hardwood, and for the Nittany Lions, their quaternary finale, a great way to say fourth contest from closure, with a daunting outlook against No. 9 Nebraska on its home turf.

Despite winning the tipoff, Penn State didn’t stand a chance from the jump, and eventually lost to the Cornhuskers 87-64. Nebraska, which put forth a formidable season so far, only kept it rolling with an emphatic win over the blue and white.

Mike Rhoades sent out a first unit of Kayden Mingo, Freddie Dilione V, Dominick Stewart, Josh Reed and Ivan Jurić. That group got out to a short 7-4 lead after a quick two and a half minutes of action, yet the Cornhuskers rebounded early, as did sloppy blue and white possessions.

On the offensive side of the ball, the first half was an absolute headache for the Nittany Lions, who logged 11 turnovers, only made one three and trailed by 18.

The sole make from downtown for the blue and white came from wing Josh Reed, who uncharacteristically led the Nittany Lions in shot attempts at the half with ten. Unfortunately for the senior, he only made three. Jurić provided needed rebounding, with those figures near level between the two units; he’d grabbed a tied game-high six off the glass at the break.

Mingo started slow, with three misses and no makes; he’d only logged an assist and rebound in 16 minutes of first-half action. Nebraska forward Pryce Sandford might as well be nicknamed “microwave”, the way he got hot quickly. The senior swished his first five three-point attempts en route to a game-high twenty points at the half, with only one miss.

The Nittany Lions, after the break, looked to energize an offense that was plagued by slowed possessions, and Jurić started well on that effort with a forceful slam. Although that highlighted the blue and white’s start to the half, as Nebraska went on a nine-point run with guard Sam Hoiberg throwing dimes.

Jurić eventually broke that run with a bucket inside, just for the Cornhuskers to go on an eight-point stretch. Mingo got a couple of buckets, and the Nittany Lions got two consecutive possessions with points inside, only their second ‘run’ of the game, almost halfway through the second half.

Penn State put a few minutes of solid play together and attempted to further carve the deficit, but a pass upcourt by Mingo that missed wing Mason Blackwood on the fastbreak visually stopped momentum before a media timeout.

There was practically no chance of a comeback for Penn State after Hoiberg drove to the rack and drew an and-one to push the lead to 20. With a little over five minutes left, the Nittany Lions just had to play for pride.

Penn State did so, attempting to score and fight until the final buzzer, as they got showered with boos after guard Melih Tunca drew a foul call with under ten seconds of play.

For the blue and white, when the play finished, Jurić logged an efficient 13 double-double on 6-of-8 shooting. Mingo, who dished out a team-high six assists, and Reed joined him as Penn State’s double-digit scorers.

For Nebraska, Sanford set a career high with 33 points. The junior received a standing ovation when he checked out.

With the result, the blue and white fall to 2-15 in Big Ten play and 11-17 overall, giving them a firm hold on the bottom seed of the conference tournament as the postseason looms.

Availability Report

Remaining listed as “out” is Tibor Mirtić.

Up Next

Penn State returns to Happy Valley for its “Autism Awareness Game” against Iowa, exactly a week away next Saturday. Tipoff is scheduled for 12 p.m.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: Winter storm headed to State College amid THON Weekend
author: Neel Ved | The Daily Collegian
description:
The National Weather Service has placed State College under a Winter Storm Watch beginning at 5 a.m. Sunday through 1 p.m. Monday.

Snowfall totals are expected to be anywhere between 3-8 inches with wind gusts as high as 40 mph. Travel is expected to be difficult with hazardous conditions that could impact the remainder of THON Weekend and the Monday morning commute.

MORE NEWS

title: A ranking on how THON Organizations spell out their pride
author: Katie Herron | Lifestyle Editor
description:
Year after year THON Weekend, it’s tradition for the Bryce Jordan Center to transform into a dancing alphabet with floating letters bouncing around stands.

THON fundraising organizations display their pride by creating larger than life letters representing themselves, their Four Diamond’s families and their theme for the year. Each one more unique than the last, it’s hard to pick just one that stands out from the crowd.

Here is my ranking of some of the letters brightening up the BJC this THON Weekend.

Delta Kappa Epsilon

Among the Pixar features, Delta Kappa Epsilon opted for a theme that shows their drive to cross the finish line at 46 hours with icons and patterns depicting “Cars.”

Although their letters pull from the essence of the popular children’s movie rather than relying on stars like Lightning McQueen, the lightning bolt, race track and headlights adorning each letter wins them the Piston Cup in spirit.

Kappa Sigma

THON Weekend already feels like a magical place, but with Kappa Sigma’s letters, the BJC should expect owls delivering the letters this year.

Inspired by the Harry Potter franchise, these letters bewitch the eyes and spirits of all its viewers. Kappa Sigma may have proven to be the chosen one with these.

Epsilon Sigma Alpha x Alpha Sigma Phi

A new king of the jungle has entered the BJC. Epsilon Sigma Alpha and Alpha Sigma Phi let out their wild side with furry (and slimy) friends hanging around the organization’s letters.

Monkeys, koalas, frogs and plenty of animal print give these letters a very natural appeal. Let’s hope the BJC floor isn’t as much of a zoo.

Penn State Club Cross Country

Pulling inspiration from the lives and interests of their fundraising families, Penn State’s Club Cross Country honored them by drawing meaningful icons on the letters.

A microphone, donut, paint palette and football are all among the images representing the people they THON for. With only two letters, the club team made the most of the space they had.

Penn State Trilogy x Phi Sigma Kappa

The members of Penn State Trilogy and Phi Sigma Kappa encapsulated all the emotions dancers may feel over the course of 46 hours. With notable characters from Pixar’s “Inside Out,” the colorful cast is eye-catching in the crowded stands.

The bright cartoon characters paired with the even brighter lights trimming the letters made these letters an easy pick for a fan favorite.

Alpha Tau Omega x Zeta Tau Alpha

Saving the best for last, spotted soaring in the air at the BJC, the lovable beasts from “How to Train Your Dragon” made an appearance on Alpha Tau Omega and Zeta Tau Alpha’s letters. With beady eyes peering down at the dancers, the dragons definitely add to the stadium's roar.

Although they may be missing their fiery breath, the dragons still light up the stands with colorful lights surrounding each letter.

MORE THON COVERGAGE

title: Penn State softball explodes on offense in dominating win over Ohio
author: Ashley Beres | The Daily Collegian
description:
After extending its winning streak to six games, red-hot Penn State faced Ohio in its third game at the Space U Classic in Orlando on Saturday afternoon. The Bobcats couldn’t slow down the blue and white’s explosive offense as the Nittany Lions raked once again.

Penn State’s offense poured it on late in the game and earned its seventh straight win with a dominating 16-1 victory over Ohio.

Sophomore Ally Meyers was tasked with settling Penn State’s offense on the mound, but the blue and white plated three runs in the first inning. Center fielder Natalie Lieto pulled a lead-off double down the left field line and despite Meyers retiring the next two Nittany Lions, they put together some two-out magic.

Second baseman Michela Barbabente worked a long walk, and then stole second. However, the Bobcats’ catcher Jordyn Wycuff made a costly error and threw the ball away on the steal, which allowed Lieto to score the game’s opening run and Barbente advanced to third.

Feeding off the momentum, first baseman Kirsten Finarelli smashed a two-run home run deep into left, and extended Penn State’s lead, 3-0. Meyers eventually stopped the bleeding by striking out right fielder Cara Bohner, but sophomore Abigail Britton started with some run support on the mound.

The blue and white added to its early lead with small ball in the second. Left fielder Breanna Hanik poked a lead-off single to center and the Nittany Lions stayed aggressive on the base paths as the freshman wasted no time swiping second. A few batters later, Lieto plated Hanik with a one-out sacrifice fly to right center.

Meanwhile, Britton was dealing early and preserved Penn State's 4-0 lead.The LaVale, Maryland, native recorded three strikeouts in the second.

The Nittany Lions had another opportunity to extend their lead in the third, but they stranded runners on second and third with two outs. While Penn State was finally retired with no runs scored, the Bobcats broke through for their first run.

Britton surrendered a couple of walks to Belle Hummel and Colleen Bare, setting up Brenna Farmer with runners on first and second. The junior delivered with a one-out RBI single to left center and cut Penn State’s lead, 4-1. Yet, Britton retired the next two batters and limited the damage to just one run.

The right-handed pitcher continued her dominance and mowed down the Bobcats' bats in the fourth. Britton totaled four strikeouts and allowed just one run off two hits and three walks over four innings.

Penn State’s offense stayed rolling in the fifth and added more insurance runs. Barbanente slapped a one-out single to right and swiftly recorded her second steal of the day. After Finarelli popped up to shortstop, catcher Madison Seidel knocked a two-out RBI single to center and widened the Nittany Lions’ lead, 5-1.
