Jansen ended the game with a 60% save percentage, significantly higher than Manning’s 37.5%. However, Treanor said there were no plans to move Manning from the starting lineup, and that Jansen’s appearance was just a chance to get some younger players some time during a blowout.

Things changed on Wednesday after another poor first-half performance from Manning. While Manning has started 30 out of 36 games in her career, that streak could soon be over.

Penn State is in a new era under Treanor, and just because former coach Missy Doherty gave the net to Manning despite sitting in the bottom of the Big Ten in goalie stats, Treanor could be looking to switch things up and explore her other options.

MORE LACROSSE CONTENT

title: The Association for Women Geoscientists 'rocks'
author: Ava Krysko | The Daily Collegian
description:
As the old saying goes, "diamonds are a girl's best friend," but for members of the Association for Women Geoscientists (AWG), expensive, shiny rocks are definitely not the only ones that matter.

AWG is a club open to anyone (no matter the gender) at University Park who takes an interest in the study of geoscience, which includes a variety of subjects from rocks to volcanoes.

The club's mission is to promote inclusivity and acceptance to underrepresented groups in a field usually dominated by men. Most of all, the club stands as a place for community building through outreach, mentorship and fundraising.

Anastasia Piliouras said the ability to interact with students she might not have met otherwise is one of her favorite things about advising the club.

"AWG really serves that purpose of giving people space to interact with other people who share those values, and then providing some resources for people to just help them participate," Piliouras, faculty advisor for the club, said. "AWG at Penn State brings a lot of different students together, who you might not expect to see."

The club usually meets once a month to go over announcements and hold events such as resume workshops, mentor/mentee mixers, graduate student panels and more.

Besides these meetings, AWG hosts special events and fundraisers every now and then. Some events include their yearly pie-making competition in the fall and the chili cook-off in the spring, which is taking place this year on March 1.

The club also partners with Elementary Explorers, a K-5 science outreach program and local Girl Scout troops to give informative presentations and get future generations excited for careers in geoscience.

These special events allow those outside of AWG, as well as faculty and staff to get involved and see what AWG is all about.

AWG at University Park is a chapter of a larger, international organization under the same name. The international organization helps fund the AWG chapters and their scholarships by matching any donations the local chapter has received.

Ava Spangler, fundraising chair, said this money along with what the club makes during fundraisers, goes towards things like Field Camp scholarships, which is an expensive study program for those in the geoscience field.

"The national organization provides a lot of support," Spangler, a third-year Ph.D. candidate studying geoscience, said. "We pay dues to become a member, and there's a lot of benefits in doing so."

Bailey Hoplight, co-president, said recently the club has been trying to emphasize relations between graduate and undergraduate students to keep undergraduates informed about their future, and help stay up to date on things like application deadlines.

Additionally, AWG presents undergraduates with the option of furthering their education in geoscience.

"I really like that (the) gap is being bridged in AWG," Hoplight, a second-year Ph.D. candidate studying organic geochemistry, said. "We're just trying to make things more accessible."

Similarly, Denali Kincaid, co-president, loves that even though the club's area of study is so niche and extensive, it's still such a welcoming place where everyone can get involved — as long as they're okay hearing everyone talk about rocks too much.

"Geology is a very interdisciplinary science," Kincaid, a third-year Ph.D. candidate studying geosciences with a specialty in volcanology, said. "There are some people who actually don't study rocks whatsoever. We have such a wide variety of students who would love to talk with people from other departments who still might have overlapping interests."

Spangler is very appreciative of the welcoming attitude of the club, as it can be difficult to get involved in a new field of study when that's not your background. She was initially nervous about joining the geoscience community, because she wasn't as familiar with the subject as everyone else.

However, after joining AWG, Spangler said she made a lot of friends and felt included. She's happy she can make others feel similarly through the club's activities. No matter who you are, AWG encourages anyone who is interested to come relax and enjoy some good food and good company.

"Anytime we see a new face, we get really excited," Kincaid said. "You'll be embraced from the beginning."

MORE LIFESTYLE CONTENT

title: Column | Connection for a cause
author: Julia Kushner | The Daily Collegian
description:
Editor's Note: The opinions expressed in this column are the columnist’s own and do not represent The Daily Collegian or its board of editors. While Julia works on our opinion staff, she is not involved in THON coverage, which our newsroom approaches independently and impartially.

“One day we will dance in celebration, until then we will dance for a cure.”

As Penn State students, we all know what THON is a 46-hour no-sitting, no-sleeping dance marathon raising funds for childhood cancer.

THON is the world's largest student-run philanthropy that aims to provide emotional and financial support to Four Diamonds families who have been impacted by childhood cancer.

But more than supporting children and their families, THON is additionally a student unifier where almost the entirety of the student body comes together for this philanthropic cause.

You don’t even have to be affiliated with anyone or anything other than the university in general to attend. Not only do Penn Staters rally around both the cause and the event, but students run the affair from beginning to end.

If you’ve never put on any type of large event before, the amount of labor, coordination, communication and community outreach it takes to put something like THON together is absolutely astronomical.

I would say the organizers take it on the chin, but they do so much more than that. They love what they do with every fiber of their being.

There are numerous student organizations solely dedicated to the smooth operation of THON think Dancer Relations, Rules and Regulations, and even OPPerations). Plus, all of Greek Life and the clubs solely dedicated to raising money for their THON families make this event one like no other.

THON gives students, alumni and parents a sense of unity.

We raise money together, we dance together, we fight together. And all for the most worthwhile of causes.

What other collegiate event even comes close to that?

THON is also one of the only Penn State happenings that brings outsiders in and treats them as family.

Sure, at football games, the opposing team’s fanbase may show up to support their boys, but never once have we invited any Ohio State fans to do anything on our campus besides show up, beat us and leave.

THON is the antithesis of this.

Not only do we welcome our THON kids and families with open arms, but we spend as much time with them as possible, both on the Bryce Jordan Center floor and off. Some of these children have even grown up to attend Penn State and provide the same opportunities for other kids that were so generously granted to them.

And there’s nothing like the Final Four. Those last hours culminating in the monetary reveal are some of the most special moments you’ll ever experience.

College kids aren’t known for their respect or quiet attention, but when members of THON families bravely climb the stage to tell their stories, you can truly hear a pin drop.

The BJC is massive, but the only sounds you’ll hear in these moments are the storytellers’ voices and the quiet, gut-punching sobs that echo throughout the arena. During these speeches, there’s only quiet introspection and prayer. There’s only raw emotion and hope for the future. A future without cancer, and a future without childhood illness. These moments are sacred in the most literal of senses.

Good luck to all of this year’s dancers, and thank you to everyone who organizes, participates in and funds Penn State’s most impactful event of the year. Here’s to breaking $18 million.

MORE COLUMNS

title: No. 11 Penn State men’s lacrosse set for second consecutive road game against Navy
author: Elliott Inscho | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State bounced back in a major way following their upset loss to Villanova two weeks ago, responding with an upset of its own against then-No. 3 Princeton on Valentine’s Day.

The No. 11 Nittany Lions benefitted from another hat-trick by star attackman Hunter Aquino in the 13-7 win over the Tigers, but the Easton, Pennsylvania, native wasn’t the only standout performer for the blue and white.

Aquino was one of four Penn State players tied for the team lead in points, as midfielders Luke Walstrum, Michael Faraone and John Jude Considine each matched his tally of three on the day.

A strong defensive performance was also instrumental in the win over Princeton, and after a season-low of just seven goals allowed for the unit, a pair of players were recognized for their performance.

Defenseman Alex Ross was named the Big Ten Defensive Player of the Week, with goaltender Preston Hawkins being recognized as the Big Ten Freshman of the Week after holding the Tigers to their fewest goals since 2024.

The aforementioned Aquino joined the duo in receiving honors, being named the Big Ten Offensive Player of the Week.

This Saturday, the Nittany Lions will stay on the road for a matchup with Navy. The Midshipmen should present less of a challenge to Penn State, though they sit at 2-1 on the young campaign.

The team defeated Iona 17-7 in its season opener in Annapolis, but would suffer a 12-8 defeat to Towson the next week. A bounce-back victory over VMI brought Navy into the contest with the Nittany Lions with a winning record.

Last season, the Midshipmen went 8-7 with a 4-4 record in Patriot League play. One of their losses came at the hands of Penn State, with the blue and white winning 13-7 in Happy Valley.

Star attackman Mac Haley leads Navy with 14 points. Haley has scored 53 goals at Navy and has recorded 102 total points to this point in his career. Sophomore attackman William Goers leads the Midshipmen with nine goals, a total that is approaching his freshman mark of 13 after just three games in 2026.

Navy holds a lopsided historical record over the Nittany Lions, leading 37-4 in the all-time series. That record is largely due to a 33-game winning streak by the Midshipmen spanning from 1919 through 1961 that saw Navy outscore Penn State 500-81, nearly 30 of which came in a 1951 matchup that saw the Midshipmen claim a 29-3 victory.

The Nittany Lions’ breakthrough victory came in 1992, when the blue and white were able to record a 16-9 win at home. That winning has been a more common theme in more recent years, as Penn State holds a three-game winning streak over the Midshipmen.

MORE LACROSSE COVERAGE

title: Penn State women’s basketball looks for redemption win at Rutgers
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Most teams don’t have the opportunity to face the same team twice in a season and learn from their mistakes.

For Penn State, however, it will travel to New Brunswick, New Jersey, for its second matchup of the season against Rutgers at 4 p.m. Saturday.

The Lady Lions fell to the Scarlet Knights earlier this year, but a lot has changed since then for both squads as they battle for a spot in the Big Ten tournament.

In the teams’ first matchup, Rutgers came out victorious after a seven-point advantage in the fourth quarter. With a final score of 76-72, the blue and white lost its eighth straight game, but has since won a pair of games to put it back in contention for the tournament.

On the other hand, the Scarlet Knights have been on a cold stretch since their win in Happy Valley. Rutgers has lost its past eight games by an average of 25 points, with its only conference victory coming against the Lady Lions.

A large part of Rutgers’ recent woes has been due to the injury bug, as three of its four highest scorers — who average a combined 29.3 points per game — were questionable heading into Tuesday’s game versus Illinois.

While Lauryn Swann, Faith Blackstone and Imani Lester all played, possible nagging injuries could cause a big issue when Penn State comes to town on Saturday.

For the blue and white, it did fall victim to injury earlier in the Big Ten season, as Gracie Merkle and Amiya Evans each missed a handful of games. Last week, the duo was back on the court together for the first time since Jan. 8, and averaged a plus-16.5 in their win against Northwestern on Sunday.

The Lady Lions have been playing their best basketball of the season over the last two weeks, with a win against Purdue, an eight-point halftime lead over ranked Michigan State, a four-point halftime deficit to ranked Maryland and a victory over the Wildcats.

Much of that recent success has come with Kiyomi McMiller playing some of the best basketball of her career.

McMiller has scored over 30 points in each of the last four games, becoming the first Lady Lion in school history to accomplish that feat. In addition, she recorded her third double-double of the season on Sunday and was named to the Big Ten Weekly Honor Roll on Monday.

Penn State’s season is on the line heading into Saturday’s game, and it’s the perfect opportunity for McMiller’s success to continue against her former team.

As she said in postgame media following the win against Northwestern, Saturday’s game will be “A big win.”

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State women’s lacrosse travels on road, faces Drexel
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State is on fire to start the season, sitting 4-0 with a top-25 victory in coach Kayla Treanor’s first season.

The Nittany Lions will have their second road battle of the year when they travel to Drexel for a noon game on Sunday.

While the blue and white have looked strong over the past two weeks, here’s a look at this weekend’s matchup for Penn State.

All-time matchups

Despite being a historically weaker team, Drexel has had decent success against the Nittany Lions, especially in the last few years.

After Penn State won eight straight matchups to start the rivalry, the Dragons have won three of the last four, giving the blue and white a 9-3 lead all-time.

Last year, the Nittany Lions fell 14-9 against Drexel at home, amidst a six-game losing streak. The Dragons pulled ahead in an early 8-3 first-half lead, and added another five goals in the third quarter to give plenty of cushion to Penn State’s five-goal fourth quarter.

Erika Ho was a major contributor in last season’s contest, as she tallied over half of the goals for the blue and white. With five goals and an assist, Ho led the team with six points, while the next-best only had one point.

2026 Drexel

The Dragons have had a fairly strong start to their season, posting a 2-1 record through two weeks.

Drexel opened its 2026 campaign with a double overtime victory against UConn, winning 12-11. The following week, it won again in a 16-3 domination of Wagner.

However, the Dragons are coming off a last-minute collapse against No. 11 Penn. Drexel was up 10-9 with 5:12 left in the game, but gave up a pair of goals in the final minutes to earn its first loss of the year.

Despite the loss, Drexel has had over a week off to prepare for the Nittany Lions — its second straight ranked opponent.

Sunday’s matchup

Both teams have had no issues scoring this season, making this weekend’s matchup a potential high-scoring shootout.

However, a big issue for Penn State is the availability of Ho. She led the team in goals in last year’s contest, but was out for Wednesday’s win against Pitt.

Fortunately for the Nittany Lions, they’ve had plenty of other goal scorers this year, including freshman Rocquette Allen. Through the first three games of the year, she had 12 goals.

The first 60 minutes against Pitt seemed like it would be slow for the Lone Tree, Colorado, native after scoring just one goal. However, she earned the overtime victory for Penn State with her 14th goal of the year and second of the day.

While none of Drexel’s players have nearly as many goals as Allen, it has a trio of high-scoring athletes who could do some damage to the questionable goalie room at Penn State.

Kate Quinn, Annie Madden and Bea Buckley lead the Dragons in scoring, combining for 22 goals in their three games. With Penn State putting Arden Jansen in the net after starter Sydney Manning recorded a 14.3% save percentage, Drexel’s trio could score their way to a victory on Sunday.

MORE LACROSSE COVERAGE

title: Penn State men’s basketball set for road test against No. 9 Nebraska
author: Chase Mitchell | The Daily Collegian
description:
This season, Penn State has played teams that are currently ranked No. 24, No. 15, No. 10, No. 7, and No. 1, Twice.

Now, the Nittany Lions are set to face off against the ninth-ranked team in the nation, Nebraska, which reached as high as No. 5 in the rankings in early January.

The blue and white hit the road and head for Lincoln, where it will take on the Cornhuskers inside the Pinnacle Bank Arena. Tipoff is scheduled for 2 p.m. Saturday.

Nebraska, now 22-4 on the year after going undefeated through its first 20 games, is coming off a five-point loss to Iowa on Tuesday. Its previous two games included a win over Northwestern and a three-point overtime loss to then-ranked No. 13 Purdue.

The Cornhuskers are led by former Hawkeyes forward Pryce Sandfort, who is averaging 17.3 points per game – good for 14th in the Big Ten and a tie for 128th in the NCAA.

Sandfort was held to just 13 points in Nebraska’s loss to his former team and posted zero rebounds or assists, committing three fouls along the way.

He notched 29 points and six boards in the victory over Northwestern and looks to have a bounce-back performance against the Nittany Lions, who’ve allowed several huge scoring nights from players of the opposition this season, including 43 points from Maryland’s Diggy Coit and 44 from Indiana’s Lamar Wilkerson.

“Another thing is letting a good player get going early,” Mike Rhoades said in his postgame press conference after the Rutgers loss. “When you see that ball go in early, that rim just gets bigger and bigger.”

Penn State’s leading scorer is guard Freddie Dilione V, who is averaging 14.3 points per game. Dilione dropped a career-high 25 points against the Boilermakers earlier this season and matched it in the Nittany Lions’ first conference win over Minnesota.

Freshman Kayden Mingo just barely lags behind, averaging 14 points per game. The highest-rated recruit in Penn State program history remains the only freshman in the country with that mark while also recording four-plus assists, three-plus rebounds, and two-plus steals per game.

Mingo is also the third-highest scoring freshman in the Big Ten behind USC’s Alijah Arenas and Illinois’ Keaton Wagler, with Arenas a projected first-round pick and Wagler a projected top-10 pick in the upcoming NBA Draft.

One of the youngest teams in the country, the blue and white have turned to Mingo as its on-court leader, with Rhoades commending Mingo alongside forward Josh Reed for being the leaders on the floor.

“Josh and Kayden, you know, they have stepped up throughout the year as being the best on the court leaders and communicators and connectors,” Rhoades said in that same postgame press conference. “We’re asking Kayden to do a lot of guarding good players … throughout the year, they’ve done it the right way.”

Reed has quietly had a very solid season, overshadowed by the blue and white’s struggles. He is coming off a career-high 22 point night against Rutgers and is second in total rebounds behind only Ivan Jurić.

As a team, Nebraska scores 78.3 points per game while the Nittany Lions allow opponents to score 79 per game on average, so just around the same.

Penn State, which had 15 turnovers in Rutgers’ first road win of the season, will look to control the ball better against the Cornhuskers, who force 13 turnovers per game on average.

Saturday’s game is a tale of two completely opposite ends, with Fred Hoiberg and Nebraska looking to add another home win to their résumé, while the Nittany Lions look to play spoiler and hurt the Cornhuskers’ eventual Big Ten Tournament seeding, as the blue and white can only possibly go up from where it is now.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: Review | The ultimate clash of Philly cheesesteaks: Penn Steaks v.s. Uncle John’s Jawns
author: Veronica Figg | The Daily Collegian
description:
As an honorary Philadelphian (an hour away by car), I can say that I’ve had plenty of authentic Philadelphia cheesesteaks, and was very prepared to take on the task of who has the better cheesesteak in State College. Our two contenders for the crown are Penn Steaks and Uncle John’s Jawns.

Penn’s Steaks

Steak Cheesesteak

The steak cheesesteak at Penn Steaks contained beef with cooper American cheese and onions on an Italian roll. The highlight of the sandwich was the cheese because it was very creamy, although mildly sharp. The onions added the sweet and sour tang that was missing from the cheese, and were crunchy and refreshing in the sandwich.

The steak and cheese were a great flavor combination, but sometimes made for a heavy bite, with the onions as a great add-on to balance out the rest of the sandwich. Overall, the meat was juicy and fresh, while the roll had a nice chew.

Rating: 9/10

Natural Shoestring Fries with Housemade Crab Fry Sauce

The fries are cooked in 100% all-natural tallow, but were very greasy and although crispy lacked salt. The Housemade Crab Fry Sauce was too tangy, and the crab seasoning was nowhere to be found. While sometimes a great sauce can make up for a lackluster fry, unfortunately this wasn’t the case.

Rating: 5/10

Chicken Cheesesteak

The chicken cheesesteak came with chicken, cooper sharp American cheese and onions on an Italian roll. In my opinion, the cheese and chicken didn't pair well together in this sandwich. The cheese overpowered the chicken, and the two just weren’t a great flavor combination overall. The onions saved the sandwich with their crunchy and juicy taste, but this dish was not one of my favorites.

Rating: 6/10

Uncle John’s Jawns

Steak Cheesesteak

Uncle John’s Jawns steak cheesesteak comes with onions, salt and pepper. The steak in the sandwich was top notch — it was chewy in a good way and super tender. The cheese was gooey and soaked the steak in cheesy deliciousness, and blended well with the crunchy and juicy onions.

The best part of the sandwich in my opinion was the roll. It’s toasted on the grill with the steak, cheese and onions, and was crunchy and crispy in the perfect way to sandwich together all the ingredients.

Ranking 10/10

The Verdict

In my opinion, Uncle John’s Jawns has the superior cheesesteak. The meat was tender and the cheese had a great flavor that complimented it well. The onions were crunchy with just the right amount of sweetness. However, the roll is what made the sandwich stand out for me, and was the perfect wrapper for the cheesesteak.

Veronica’s Food Sensitivity Corner

As a student who was recently diagnosed with a dairy sensitivity, I’m always on the lookout for foods that fit my new dietary needs. I’m also aware there are other students who may have similar needs as well.

Penn Steaks has a gluten-free bun option.

For both Penn Steaks and Uncle John’s Jawns, you can remove the cheese from your sandwich.

Penn Steaks is located at 119 S Pugh St. and is open Monday, Wednesday and Sunday from 11 a.m. to 8 p.m., while on Thursdays through Saturdays, it is open from 11 a.m. to 2:30 a.m. Penn Steaks is closed on Tuesdays.

Uncle John’s Jawns is located at 324 E Calder Way and is open Monday through Saturday 11:30 a.m. to 2:30 a.m. and Sunday from 11:30 a.m. to 9 p.m.

Head to Uncle John’s Jawns or Penn Steaks to get a taste of Philadelphia right here in State College. Then afterwards, go take a jog up the Old Main steps and throw some punches. It’s not exactly 72 steps, but it's close enough.

MORE BLOGS

title: SPA hosts Malcolm Todd to perform at Bryce Jordan Center
author: Rebecca Fein | The Daily Collegian
description:
The Student Programming Association (SPA) will host singer-songwriter and producer, Malcom Todd, at the Bryce Jordan Center on March 24.

Malcolm Todd is known for his eccentric, R&B and indie pop fusion music. Todd released his first album in 2025 entitled “Malcom Todd” and quickly rose to fame with the album's single, "Chest Pain (I Love),” making the Billboard Top 100.

University Park students can purchase general admission floor tickets and lower-bowl tickets for $20, and the general public can buy lower-bowl tickets for $45.

Tickets go on sale Tuesday, Feb. 24 at noon on Ticketmaster.

MORE LIFESTYLE COVERAGE

title: Penn State wrestling's Marcus Blaze, Cole Mirasola sweep Big Ten Wrestler of the Week awards
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
Another week, another set of Big Ten weekly honors for Penn State.

After wins against Ohio State, Marcus Blaze was named the Big Ten Freshman of the Week for his victory over then-No. 2 Ben Davino, while Cole Mirasola earned Big Ten Wrestler of the Week after upsetting then-No. 3 Nick Feldman.

Congrats guys!Cole Mirasola named B1G Wrestler of the WeekMarcus Blaze named B1G Freshman of the Week#PSUwrhttps://t.co/lb1SalG2ji pic.twitter.com/lDXjL9l7T5— Penn State WRESTLING (@pennstateWREST) February 19, 2026

Blaze went to sudden victory with Davino in a top-5 matchup, with that time frame not enough to declare a winner. In the second tiebreaker, Blaze secured a reversal on Davino to pull off the victory and defeat the Buckeye.

Mirasola and Feldman also went to sudden victory, with Mirasola securing a takedown shortly after the period began to pull off the upset.

The two awards were the eighth and ninth weekly awards for the Big Ten Regular Season Champions.

It was Blaze's second Big Ten Freshman of the Week award of the season, while Mirasola earned his first Big Ten Wrestler of the Week, but earned Freshman of the Week honors on Jan. 22.

MORE WRESTLING COVERAGE

title: Penn State women's hockey captain Tessa Janecke, Team USA win women’s hockey gold medal
author: Evan Smith | The Daily Collegian
description:
The United States of America won the gold medal in women’s hockey at the Milana Corta 2026 Olympic Games. Penn State’s Tessa Janecke will bring a medal back to State College alongside the team. Janecke was on the ice for both the game-tying and game-winning goals in the final game.

🥇 Tessa Janecke 🥇Women's Ice Hockey 🇺🇸 @TeamUSA #OlympiansMadeHere x @NCAAIceHockey pic.twitter.com/rdisAsWYdr— NCAA (@NCAA) February 19, 2026

The United States trailed 1-0 with less than three minutes remaining in regulation. With 2:04 on the clock Laila Edwards slapped a shot from the blue line, which Hillary Knight deflected past Canadian goaltender Ann-Renee Desbiens to send the game to overtime.

Just four minutes into overtime, Megan Keller skated by two Canadian defensemen and slide a backhander past Desbiens to clinch the golden goal.

Team USA only let up two goals throughout the tournament, while scoring 31. Janecke put up five points in the six-game tournament run, becoming the first Penn State hockey player to bring home a gold medal.

MORE OLYMPICS COVERAGE

title: Penn State softball heads to the Sunshine State for its second time this season
author: Brielle Gari | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State travels to the Sunshine State to face off in the SpaceU Classic.

The Nittany Lions traveled to Houston for the Houston Invitational last weekend and came out victorious. Going 4-1 over the course of five games, picking up quickly after their first loss in the weekend opener.

Returning to Florida for their third weekend of the regular season, the blue and white are looking for another successful weekend after only suffering two losses so far this season.

Stepping back in the dirt, the Nittany Lions will have another softball-filled weekend at UCF Softball Complex. Starting their tournament off with a double header, facing off Charleston Southern and then host UCF on Friday.

Charleston Southern

Facing off for the first time ever in program history, Penn State will open its weekend in Orlando against Charleston Southern.

The Buccaneers are currently 2-9 in the 2026 season.

Playing in the Big South conference, going 9-9 within the conference and 20-29 overall for their 2025 campaign.

The Nittany Lions have two games scheduled against Charleston Southern throughout the weekend.

Not having too much offensive momentum, the Buccaneers did have two shutout games so far this season against Sacred Heart and Marist that ended in five innings.

Being their only victories thus far, Blakley Kingsmore has been the frontrunner for the Bucs. A power hitter from St.Matthews, South Carolina, having a .469 batting average. Kingsmore appeared in all 48 games last season, starting each one.

University of Central Florida

To close out their first day of the SpaceU Classic, the blue and white face off against host UCF at 7 p.m.

The Knights are 6-1 when they have been on their home turf. Going 9-3 so far in their 2026 campaign, UCF has a chance to continue their winning streak alive.

Within their 12 games so far, the Knights have faced off against four top 25 teams, suffering their only losses against ranked opponents.

UCF has fallen to the No.28 spot in the NCAA rankings, the Knights' first statement win of the season, defeating then No.10 LSU for the first time in program history.

This will be the fifth meeting between programs on the dirt. A current split between both teams as they have each won two games.

In the 2019 season, it was the last time the blue and white went to face off against the Knights. Penn State fell short in eight innings, 3-2.

The Nittany Lions look to progress with another weekend at the plate. The first pitch of the day will be at 2 p.m., taking on the Buccaneers, then facing offagainst the host, the Knights, at 7 p.m.

MORE SOFTBALL CONTENT

title: ‘I am who I am’ | Terry Smith reflects on changing role, life after interim head coach
author: Lyle Alenstein | Wrestling Editor
description:
The first time Terry Smith met Matt Campbell was when he was the head coach and athletic director at Gateway High School, and Campbell was the head coach at Toledo. When Campbell had his introductory press conference at Penn State in December, he brought up that past relationship with Smith.

Campbell has made it clear he trusts Smith, who enters his 13th season on the Penn State staff. Not only did he allow him to return as the cornerbacks coach, but he also updated his responsibilities as associate head coach, a position he held prior to the firing of James Franklin.

“My associate role is different and bigger than under James,” Smith said at National Signing Day media. “Coach Campbell has conversations with me about how he wants to do certain things, and I'll share my insight to certain things. Ultimately, he has to make that final decision. James would ask me certain things, but it’s different under Matt. Everyone in the building knows that I’m the associate head coach. Under James, that wasn’t clear.”

Campbell may have had the prior relationship with Smith, but the new members of the coaching staff are aware of how important Smith has been to the Penn State culture and helping them adapt to State College.

As new safeties coach Deon Broomfield put it, Smith has been “pivotal to us coming in and getting the lay of the land.”

“He knows everything of how this place works,” Broomfield said. “When you talk about it from a recruiting standpoint, every coach that you run into on the road, they're like, ‘Terry's our guy.’ To have somebody who's been a prominent figure within the program, and for him to choose to stay around, it's been pivotal to us getting off to a fast start.”

He’s very clearly an important piece of the program. That part hasn’t changed with the new regime, just like it hasn’t changed since he stepped foot on campus in 2014 as the cornerbacks coach.

There is still an adjustment going back to his old role. Smith joked about taking a step back from being the interim coach, laughing as he said “I’m going through mourning.”

“I’m just kidding,” Smith said. “Just going back to life as normal. I'm a quiet, reserved person. I like to mind my own business.”

With that being said, by no means is Smith taking a backseat in carrying out his job. The lessons he learned as the interim head coach made him realize he learned a lot about himself.

“Be patient. Be diligent. Trust your process. Believe in it. Be a servant leader,” Smith said. “Continue to be a servant leader. Serve people. When you serve people, they'll serve you back. And good things can happen.”

As the interim head coach, he didn’t get to work with the cornerbacks as much as he was accustomed to. Now, Smith returns to the room as the leader of the unit, which features key returnees in Audavion Collins, Daryus Dixson, Zion Tracy and Jahmir Joseph.

Upon his return, the cornerbacks shared a message with Smith. It’s something that Smith will hold close to his chest as he embarks on a new chapter in a new era of Penn State football, but one that’ll motivate him more than ever.

“The cornerbacks that I coach every day, they shared a statement to me. They said ‘Coach T, the world got to see what we see every day,’” Smith said. “I am who I am. I coach my guys really, really hard and coach them with fairness and love and family and faith. I’m who I am. I have a different role now. I don’t have as big a role, and I can be even greater in my smaller role.”

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: A first-timers’ guide to THON lingo
author: Neel Ved | The Daily Collegian
description:
As the Penn State community approaches THON, there are many key terms to know, whether you’re dancing, attending in-person or watching online. Here are some of the most commonly used terms.

Bryce Jordan Center (BJC)

The BJC is where THON will be happening for all 46 hours, from Friday at 6 p.m. to Sunday at 4 p.m.

Spectators will be able to enter the BJC at 4 p.m. on Friday.

FTK (For The Kids)

“For The Kids,” or FTK, is a slogan that’s used for the annual panhellenic dance marathon. The slogan has been used since at least 1991.

In January of 2020, Penn State trademarked it.

Four Diamonds

Since 1972, Four Diamonds has covered 100% of all cancer-related medical expenses not covered by insurance for eligible children, with support from the community.

The organization has been the sole beneficiary of THON since 1977. Families involved in THON are often referred to as “Four Diamonds Families.”

Independent dancer couples (IDCs)

Independent dancer couples, also known as IDCs, dance in THON Weekend with no affiliation to any organization.

The dancers are chosen through a weighted lottery, with chances determined by how much money the dancers raised during the year.

Line Dance

The line dance is a new dance created every year based on current events that make it interesting and fun for the crowd.

The line dance is performed sporadically throughout the weekend and those in the stands and on the floor join in for the dance.

National Act

The national act is one of the most anticipated moments of THON Weekend, involving an hour-long performance from a nationally known artist or band. The act remains a secret until the minute they take the stage.

Final Four

Final Four is the last four hours of THON weekend, where the BJC takes time to hear from Four Diamonds families and reflect on THON’s purpose.

Family Hour

Family Hour is an hour within the Final Four that talks about the families and children affected by childhood cancer.

Pep Rally

This is one of the most popular events during THON weekend. It features multiple performances by Penn State Athletic Teams and appearances from special guests.

There is also an arena-wide Penn State “White Out,” an honor to the annual Penn State football tradition.

Graveyard Shift

The graveyard shift occurs on Saturday and Sunday during THON from 2 a.m. to 5 a.m.

MORE THON CONTENT

title: Column | Greek life runs on THON
author: Emma Lessick | The Daily Collegian
description:
Editor's Note: The opinions expressed in this column are the columnist’s own and do not represent The Daily Collegian or its board of editors. While Emma works on our photo and opinion staff, she is not involved in THON coverage, which our newsroom approaches independently and impartially.

When people think of Greek life, philanthropy often isn’t the first thing that comes to mind. But at Penn State, thousands of students spend an entire year fundraising, organizing and then showing up every February to represent something bigger than themselves.

Penn State THON is more than just 46 hours in the Bryce Jordan Center for many individuals. It’s a reflection of many Greek organizations’ commitment to giving back to the community and turning letters on a sweatshirt into powerful action for families who need it most.

THON is the world's largest student-run philanthropy that aims to provide emotional and financial support to Four Diamonds families who have been impacted by childhood cancer.

With 35 Interfraternity Council (IFC) fraternities and 21 Panhellenic Council (PHC) sororities, THON demonstrates the power of collective action. Greek organizations collaborate and celebrate with the broader Penn State community, setting aside differences in pursuit of a shared mission. In doing so, Greek life reveals a network of students committed to making a meaningful and lasting impact.

It represents the promise to give back; students redefine what leadership looks like as 6,800 members stand in representation of those who cannot.

The Adopt-A-Family program allows Four Diamonds families to be paired with student organizations year after year. These efforts aren’t fueled by competition, but by connection. Connection to the families who have been affected by childhood cancer. Members learn names, faces and stories long before they even step onto the floor as a dancer, or in the stands to represent their organization.

As part of a Greek organization myself, I can say that spreading awareness, participating in community service and raising money for childhood cancer research is a central part of what it means to be in Greek life.

After standing for hours on end, a child’s smile or a parent’s quiet thank you during the weekend makes the exhaustion feel completely worth it.

To know that in some way, I’m here to support those children and families, to put a smile on their face and to transport them into a world where everyone has a shared purpose. During THON, kindness above all else is given without expectation for anything in return.

Of course, the fundraising matters and the sheer amount of money Penn State is able to provide is life-changing in many ways, but the emotional support that Greek Life provides is remarkable.

Sorority recruitment concluded just a few weeks ago and a common question I was asked was “What is THON like, and what is your favorite part?” Truthfully, I think I got pretty good at answering this question, so, let me have another go at it.

THON isn’t something I can put into simple words. It’s a feeling and connection each one of us is able to build with the families we support.

Our THON families hold a special place in my heart and we follow them throughout their lives.

For example, I know that Charlie Karchner, who actually danced in THON in 2019, will be attending this upcoming weekend. He has a girlfriend, Tonilynn.

I know that McKenna Speed turned 14 this past October and she plays several musical instruments. My sorority sisters and I even got to see a picture of her attending high school homecoming this past fall.

Gavin Howe and his dad are huge Seattle Seahawks fans.

And then there’s the Samuel Grimm family; parents Ashley and Brian. Samuel was born on December 31, 2018, and died on March 11, 2019.

Our THON angel baby.

There are truly no words for a loss such as this, but what I’ve learned through THON is that even in the deepest grief, there’s love that endures and a community that refuses to forget.

THON lives in the names we remember, the stories shared and the families we carry with us long after the weekend ends. It’s why we dance, why we show up exhausted and delusional, yet hopeful and why this cause becomes a bigger part of who we are.

Greek life shows up for the community, and will continue to show up for THON.

THON isn’t just an event or a fundraiser. It’s a promise to stand beside the families in joy and in heartbreak, today and always. For The Kids.

MORE COLUMNS

title: THON Weekend 2026 line dance lyric predictions
author: Nadya Solometo | Lifestyle Editor
description:
It’s that time of year again where chicken baskets become fuel, sitting isn’t an option and the Bryce Jordan Center is boomin’. As THON Weekend 2026 approaches, so does the reveal of this year’s Line Dance lyrics.

THON is the world's largest student-run philanthropy that aims to provide emotional and financial support to Four Diamonds families who have been impacted by childhood cancer.

Written and performed by the THON’s Dancer Relations captains, the Line Dance is performed every hour to help maintain the spirits of the dancers and those in the stands. The lyrics often encapsulate campus, local and national news.

Here are my predictions for some of this year’s lyrics.

Penn State News

This past year, Penn State had a lot of memorable moments — for better or worse.

Back in October, the university faced the one-two punch of Drew Allar suffering a season-ending injury, and the subsequent firing of James Franklin. While not the cheeriest moments of the school year, they could be shouted out in the song.

Moving on to happier sports moments, this January the university hosted outdoor hockey games in Beaver Stadium for both the men’s and women’s teams. Men’s wrestling recently clinched their Big Ten regular season championship.

Movin’ On 2025 featured artists like the All-American Rejects, Claire Rosinkranz, Baby Tate and Pusha T. The Student Programming Association (SPA) had notable performances and speakers as well, like Flo Rida and Caleb Hearon, and artists Riley Green and Jason Aldean performed in the BJC.

Beaver Stadium is still under construction, and buildings around campus like Pollock Halls and Sackett Building are as well.

National and International News

With a lot of negative news this year, it’s hard to imagine there’d be a heavy focus on this section in the line dance. However, there are a few lighthearted moments that could be worth a mention.

This year saw the usage of Artificial Intelligence skyrocket, making it a topic that could potentially get a shoutout or two. Large language models such as ChatGPT, Claude and Microsoft Copilot could all be featured in the lyrics.

The Winter Olympics in Milan and Cortina d'Ampezzo are currently going on right now, with the U.S. currently with 24 total medals. Athletes like Lindsey Vonn, Mikaela Shiffrin and the “Blade Angels” — consisting of Figure Skaters Amber Glenn, Alysa Liu and Isabeau Levito — are all prominent this year in headlines.

Pop Culture

Within the past few months alone, so many moments have a chance of being featured. Taylor Swift released an album and documentary series this year, “Wicked: For Good” released and Harry Styles is officially releasing new music.

“Sinners,” the 2025 American horror film made Oscars history by receiving 16 nominations at the upcoming awards, the most ever awarded to a single film. Bad Bunny earned the Grammy Award for “Album of the Year,” which marked the first time the award was given to an exclusively Spanish-language project, for his album “Debí Tirar Más Fotos.”

Online there was the resurgence of 2016 trends and music, people trying to see if they’re a part of “Group 7” and the use of the phrase “wabi-sabi.” Any of these could be easily integrated into quick and fun lyrics.

Whatever moments are included in this year’s lyrics, they’ll be sure to make the dancers last all 46 hours.

MORE LIFESTYLE COVERAGE

title: 6 easy ways to shake up your daily routine
author: Lexi Cannan | The Daily Collegian
description:
At some point in the semester, your days start feeling copy and pasted.

You wake up and immediately debate skipping class. You go anyway, grab coffee, scroll for “just a minute” and promise yourself you’ll be productive later. Somehow, it becomes a daily routine.

Nothing is technically wrong — it just feels like the same episode on replay. That’s usually your sign you need a hobby, not a personality change or full glow-up. Just something small so your week has a plot twist.

Here are a few activities that will shake up your daily routine.

1) Journaling

You don’t need a leather notebook and perfect handwriting. You just need a perfect place to put your thoughts so they stop living rent-free in your head.

Write about your day, complain about your group project, make a to-do list you may or may not ignore and rate your mood like it’s a dramatic online review: “Two stars. I would not recommend this Monday.”

Some days it’ll be a full page and other days, it’s three sentences that basically say you're tired. But it still counts because it does more for you than scrolling for an hour and calling it “winding down.” It gives your brain somewhere to put everything instead of letting it bounce around while you’re trying to fall asleep.

2) Master one good recipe

You don’t need to become a meal-prep influencer — just learn one solid meal you can make without checking the recipe every 30 seconds, something slightly more impressive than buttered noodles but still realistic for a random Wednesday.

It saves money, makes you feel weirdly accomplished and gives you an excuse to have people over without ordering takeout again. A regular weeknight starts to feel a little more put together, even if the rest of your life isn’t.

3) Long walks once it’s warm again

The second it hits 55 degrees, campus acts like it’s summer, so join them. Put on a playlist, call a friend and walk for no other reason than you can.

Suddenly, the same sidewalks you’ve been rushing across all winter actually feel kind of nice. It’s free, clears your head and technically counts as being “for your mental health,” so it feels productive.

Take a quiet lap through the Arboretum, or hike Mount Nittany if you need physical proof that you’ve been outside this week.

4) Eat somewhere different

Yes, your usual order is safe, but branching out gives your week a little personality. Pick one new spot, order a drink you can’t pronounce or split something random with a friend and hope for the best.

Worst case, it’s not great and you never go back. Best case, you discover your new favorite place and a random Tuesday feels like an event. At the very least, you now have something to talk about besides deadlines and Canvas notifications.

5) Try one new club (just once)

You don’t have to sign up for everything or turn into a campus celebrity overnight. Choose one club that made you think it could be fun, and actually follow through for once.

Just go to one meeting, even if you spend the first few minutes pretending to scroll on your phone so you don’t look “too early.”

Sit down, stay for an hour and just see what happens. You don’t have to raise your hand once. No one’s grading you. Trying something new is enough to shake up your routine, and if you actually like it, you suddenly have plans that aren’t just class, the library or lying in bed.

6) Read something just for you

Not a textbook, not a discussion post, just one book you actually want to read, whether it’s romance, mystery, fantasy or anything that sounds interesting to you.

Read a few pages before bed and let your brain worry about fictional characters instead of exams and group projects for once.

You start looking forward to finding out what happens next. You care more about plot twists than your deadlines. For once, the cliffhanger in your life is in a book, not your Outlook.

MORE BLOGS

title: Penn State men’s gymnastics Landon Simpson, Luke Esparo travel to Louisville to compete in Winter Cup
author: Sami Altmire | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State men’s gymnastics is back from a two-week break, but only two Nittany Lions will be seeing gymnastics action this weekend. Landon Simpson and Luke Esparo are headed to Louisville to compete in the Winter Cup and are joined by a number of fellow Big Ten athletes.

Multiple Michigan, Illinois, Ohio State and Nebraska gymnasts are headed to Kentucky, but Simpson and Esparo will be the only two there representing the blue and white colors.

The Winter Cup is a qualifying chance for the 2026 U.S. Gymnastics Championships and will feature a multitude of past champions and Olympians. There will also be multiple club teams thrown into the mixture with the collegiate teams.

Simpson has been a standout athlete for Penn State this season, with a 13.850 on pommel horse, 13.700 on parallel bars and 13.950 on vault. Simpson has always had a great run on vault, with it historically being his strongest apparatus, and it can come in as another really strong focus point for the gymnast this weekend.

Esparo has also contributed to a lot of Penn State's success by putting up great scores on rings with multiple 14.000s. His personal best so far this season was 14.300 at the Sam Mikulak Invitational on Jan. 24, and this will definitely be the event to watch for Esparo.

Schools like Stanford and Oklahoma will also be heading to Louisville, and will be a test for not only Simpson and Esparo, but also a way for the Nittany Lions to see how well their athletes do against competition from outside of the conference.

Simpson has been putting up solid scores this season, contributing to the blue and white’s success so far. The Bellefonte, Pennsylvania, native has put up multiple high 13.000s in multiple events, but has really excelled in his historically strongest event, vault.

Esparo is consistently in the Nittany Lions lineup for pommel horse and rings. The Wallingford, Connecticut native has also been putting up solid scores for floor during the past few meets, even debuting a routine that got him a 13.700 at the Sam Mikulak Invitational.

Simpson’s also had a great run on parallel bars this season, with a 13.800 coming out of his performance at the Feb. 7 meet against Nebraska. He also put up a 13.650 against Illinois on Feb. 1, and a 13.700 at the Sam Mikulak Invitational.

Simpson and Esparo have the ability to represent the Nittany Lions well this weekend and possibly grab a spot at the 2026 U.S. Gymnastics Championship.

MORE GYMNASTICS COVERAGE

title: What to know before THON weekend
author: Emma Cherubini I The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State’s highly anticipated THON weekend is upon us. From rules to general information on what to bring, here's everything you need to know for a fun and successful weekend.

Entrance

At THON, there will be one main entrance line and two gates for entrance access. At the Bryce Jordan Center (BJC) ticketing office, there is a General Spectator line at Will Call and this is where individuals can receive free wristbands.

From there, you will be guided to enter either Gate A or Gate B. Gate A is located on the right side of the line and Gate B is located on the left side.

Entry through these gates will only be allowed with a wristband obtained through the spectator line.

How long it is

THON is recognized as the longest student-run event at Penn State each year. This event runs for 46 hours straight.

The weekend begins Friday at 6:00 p.m. and ends Sunday at 4:00 p.m.

What you can’t bring

As THON is hosted in the BJC, the rules on what you cannot bring align with the BJC’s clear bag policy and their regular rules of entrance and attendance.

Prohibited bags include non-clear bags, purses larger than the size of a clutch, backpacks, diaper bags, coolers, briefcases, luggage, drawstring bags and cinch bags.

Outside beverages and food are prohibited. Inflatable objects, cameras with a detachable lens, weapons, liquids, large flag poles and large sports equipment like basketballs, footballs and baseballs are also not allowed.

Training dogs and emotional support animals are not allowed, nor are transportation items such as skateboards, hoverboards. Visitors are not allowed to bring items like glitter, silly string, alcohol, narcotics, vape devices, lighters and tobacco products.

Bringing any of these items to the BJC can result in denied entry, and items will be confiscated and disposed of.

What you can bring

There are plenty of safe items you can bring in order to watch THON in the BJC.

Bags following clear bag rules should be wallets and clutches at most 6.5”x 4.5”, clear tote bags at most 12”x 12”x 6” and plastic zip top bags 11”x 11”x 0” or smaller.

Other items such as phones, empty reusable water bottles, disposable cameras and digital cameras without a removable lens are all allowed inside the BJC.

What you should bring

THON is a lengthy event, so some items are essential to have the full experience.

Personal hygiene items such as a toothbrush and toothpaste, deodorant and other small items will help you stay fresh while attending and a good pair of shoes can ensure your feet don’t hurt.

A non-monetary ID will allow you to access your floor pass. Disposable, digital, or phone cameras will allow you to capture any moments. Portable chargers can ensure all devices are charged.

Dancers are encouraged to bring a tennis ball to roll out sore or cramped feet and muscles.

MORE THON CONTENT

title: A guide to THON’s 17 student-run committees
author: Emma Naysmith | The Daily Collegian
description:
Over 16,500 students volunteer their time to help plan, support and execute Penn State’s annual THON weekend.

THON is the world's largest student-run philanthropy that aims to provide emotional and financial support to Four Diamonds families who have been impacted by childhood cancer.

Here’s a look into the 17 committees that help make THON happen.

Communications (COMM)

The Comm Committee is credited as the primary facilitator in the relationship between THON volunteers and the Penn State community. Its mission is to educate and guide students, faculty and staff in the fight toward a cure for pediatric cancer.

They can be found in green shirts this weekend.

Director: Maggie Gilbert

Contact: communications@thon.org

Entertainment (ENT)

The Entertainment Committee is responsible for creating and executing the video and audio elements to THON’s entertainment at all THON events throughout the year.

Found in black shirts, this group is also credited for planning event performances throughout the year and THON Weekend.

Director: Alexander Aumen

Contact: entertainment@thon.org

Special Events (SE)

Those on the Special Events committee plan and host events such as the THON 5K, 100 Days ‘TIl THON and Family Carnival leading up to THON weekend.

The committee’s shirt color is maroon.

Director: Kristin Bayuk

Contact: special.events@thon.org

Rules & Regulations Event Safety (R&R)

Known for their red shirts, Rules and Regulations committee members are trained to ensure safety within the Bryce Jordan Center for Four Diamonds children and families, volunteers and spectators.

They’re also responsible for facilitating floor access and pass check in throughout the weekend.

Director: Lauren Trippetti

Contact: event.safety@thon.org

Rules & Regulations Fundraising Safety

Members of this red-bearing committee collaborate to foster fundraising growth and transparency while sharing any applicable guidelines and ensuring safe fundraising practices.

Director: Praharsh Upadhyaya

Contact: fundraising.safesty@thon.org

Dancer Relations (DR)

Found repping yellow throughout the weekend, Dancer Relations Committee members provide designated and individualized support to dancers.

Additionally, these volunteers are trained in emergency medical services and dancer safety practices to help ensure dancers’ physical and emotional wellbeing.

Director: Anabella Begley

Contact: dancer.relations@thon.org

Family Relations (FR)

The Family Relations Committee focuses on providing year-round assistance and support to Four Diamonds families and helping to make their weekend a positive experience.

They’re also responsible for the Adopt-A-Family and planning family speakers and events. They wear light blue shirts.

Director: Carley Mykut

Contact: family.relations@thon.org

Hospitality (HOS)

Found in pink shirts, Hospitality Committee members collaborate with donors to obtain and serve food and beverage donations at THON events and during the weekend.

Director: Nathan Reish

Contact: hospitality@thon.org

OPPerations (OPP)

Wearing dark blue, the “first in and last out” of the weekend, OPPerations Committee members are dedicated volunteers who are responsible for maintaining a clean and safe environment in the BJC.

Some of their many tasks before and after the weekend include event maintenance like floor and bathroom cleaning, efforts toward recycling and more.

Director: Ava Wolf

Contact: opperations@thon.org

Supply Logistics (SL)

Donning light green, the Supply Logistics Committee works to receive and organize all in-kind donations and details regarding the THON Raffle.

Director: Erin Gnall

Contact: supply.logistics@thon.org

Donor & Alumni Relations Alumni Engagement

The Alumni Engagement Committee educates university alumni and community about fundraising awareness campaigns and opportunities. It also works to uphold these relationships to THON.

They will be in blue throughout the weekend.

Director: Julia Skrip

Contact: alumni.engagement@thon.org

Donor & Alumni Relations Development

The Development Committee of Donor and Alumni Relations works to create and build relationships through sharing THON education to donors.

They help facilitate business, foundation and corporate donations and tours during the weekend.

Members will be wearing blue shirts.

Director: Jeffrey Walsh

Contact: development@thon.org

Finance (FIN)

Seen in green shirts, the Finance Committee dedicates monetary donations to THON’s mission and finalizes the weekend total.

Director: Areea Rahman

Contact: finance@thon.org

Merchandise (MERCH)

Dressed in light pink, the Merchandise Committee is in charge of maintaining the THON Store located in the HUB-Robeson Center.

Director: Reese Butler

Contact: merchandise@thon.org

Public Relations Digital Content

The Public Relations Digital Content Committee spreads THON’s message from local, state and national levels with digital content creation and promotion.

Committee members can be found in purple.

Director: Lauren Mellas

Contact: digital.content@thon.org

Public Relations Media Management

The Media Management PR Committee helps build and maintain relationships with the media and sharing of the organization’s and Four Diamonds stories.

Members of these committees will also wear purple and assist in facilitating family and dancer interviews by the press.

Director: Vraj Patel

Contact: media.management@thon.org

Technology (TECH)

The Technology Committee provides software solutions and technical support so THON is able to further its mission and fundraising effort at its large-scale.

TECH committee members will be wearing lime green.

Director: Riya Shah

Contact: technology@thon.org

MORE THON CONTENT

title: No. 16 Penn State men’s volleyball prepares for EIVA matchup against Harvard
author: Keira Albert | The Daily Collegian
description:
After coming up big with back-to-back wins against Princeton last weekend, No. 16 Penn State now looks to shift its focus to an upcoming two-match series against Harvard this weekend.

The Nittany Lions will face the Crimson at 6 p.m. Friday from Rec Hall’s South Gym and 3 p.m. Saturday from Rec Hall for the Legacy Match.

At 8-4 on the season, the blue and white have a chance to tally two more wins this weekend against the 2-7 Harvard squad. And with Penn State at 2-0 in conference play after last weekend, it’ll look to keep its undefeated streak within the EIVA alive.

Sean Harvey is coming off a big weekend himself. After a career-high 22 kills in Friday’s five-set match, the outside hitter was named the EIVA Offensive Player of the Week.

The redshirt sophomore left Princeton with an average of 4.25 kills and 1.38 digs per set.

Currently, the outside hitter has 148 kills and 66 digs on the year, and the numbers continue to grow. With the third-best kills per set average in the EIVA, Harvey will absolutely be an issue for the Crimson this weekend.

Harvey isn’t the only player making strides in the EIVA, though.

Currently, libero Ryan Merk has the best digs per set average in the conference. With an average of 2.38 digs per set, the redshirt senior currently has an even 100 digs on the season.

Owen Rose leads the EIVA with the best blocks per set average, with his teammate Gaige Gabriel trailing just behind with the fourth-best average. Together, the two have a combined 93 blocks on the season.

The Nittany Lions also have four players in the top ten best hitting percentages within the conference. Gabriel leads the EIVA, with Tristan Hassell coming second. Harvey ranks fourth, with Jaidyn Bethel at sixth.

Despite dominating some of the statistics in the EIVA, there are still some things the blue and white might have to look out for as it prepares to face the Crimson.

Harvard’s Sawyer Nichols has 92 kills on the year and leads the team with 103 points this season. The outside hitter also leads the team in digs, with 45 on the year. As a dynamic player on the court, the Nittany Lions will need to keep their eyes on him.

While Rose leads the blue and white in service aces with 15, Harvard’s leader isn’t too far behind. Quinn Bishop has 11 service aces on the year, averaging 0.46 per set. This bests Rose by a tenth, and the Crimson have played three fewer games than Penn State.

But a few star players might not be enough to beat the blue and white.

The Crimson are 0-2 in EIVA play, 0-3 on the road and are currently stuck in a three-game losing streak.

With the Nittany Lions on a four-game winning streak and a home record of 5-2, it looks like the odds might be stacked in favor of the Penn State squad.

MORE VOLLEYBALL COVERAGE

title: No. 19 Penn State women’s gymnastics travels to No. 20 Iowa for Big Ten matchup
author: Caroline Riley | The Daily Collegian
description:
Heading into Iowa this weekend, Penn State has more than one goal to keep in mind. Despite tying the program’s fifth-highest bar score in the loss against Minnesota, the blue and white’s team score lowered by a little over .700.

Penn State has had a strong start to the season, scoring a 196.000+ score in every meet this year. However, in order to improve the Nittany Lions’ National Qualifying Score (NQS), they need a big score on the road like the 197.250 they scored at home in late January.

Of course, taking down the No. 20 team in the nation would be a perk as well, but for Penn State, managing a score closer to the 196.500+ range minimum is more important than anything in the win column.

Iowa has similar goals, but has been plagued with much more inconsistency. Despite a strong 197.225 score against Nebraska in early February, two weeks earlier, Iowa failed to even reach the 196.000 mark.

Nonetheless, Iowa arrives off a strong 196.725 showing against Rutgers on the road, and if the Hawkeyes can repeat that performance, the Nittany Lions will have their work cut out for them.

Vault continues to be a place for improvement for the blue and white. Unlike other top-contending teams across the NCAA, which have six gymnasts competing 10.0 start value vaults, Penn State starts at an inherent disadvantage with its many Yurchenko fulls starting out of a 9.950.

Iowa typically has three 10.0 start value vaults courtesy of Sydney Turner, Sophie Schriever and JerQuavia Henderson. Nonetheless, clean form and minimizing landing deductions could bring Penn State past that 49.000 mark and keep it competitive heading into its strongest event: the uneven bars.

The blue and white is a 9.900+ machine on the bars. Freshman Ava Cash continues to make her mark, scoring a 9.900 last week with textbook form. However, the key to much of Penn State’s early success has been sophomore Dani Latronica.

Last week, Latronica scored a big 9.925 on bars and her third straight 9.900 on beam. Repeating that performance against Iowa would help not only the Nittany Lions, but the sophomore’s chances for an event title.

Iowa’s also lights out on the bars. Last week, the Hawkeyes scored just below Penn State’s record-tying score with a 49.425. Despite not having any huge 9.900+ scores in the lineup, Iowa counted three 9.875s and two 9.850s for its big rotation.

As per usual, no competition is won until the beam is done. Last week, Penn State really struggled on the beam, scoring just a 48.675 compared to Iowa’s big 49.250. The Nittany Lions will have a chance to bounce back this week, so expect an extra focus on the beam.

On the floor exercise, the Nittany Lions have room for improvement. All six gymnasts have 9.800+ scoring potential, but frequently dip into the 9.700 range. They’ll have to focus on not only taking controlled lunges out of their tumbling passes but also their leaps to minimize deductions.

Iowa will likely be led by standout freshman all-around star Schriever. She was the 2025 USA Gymnastics Development Camp Champion on bars and last week scored a 9.950 on the floor. She has huge scoring potential on each event despite being so early into her collegiate career.

In order to weather the Schriever storm and more, Penn State will need to rely on a few different key performers. Beyond Latronica, Elizabeth Leary has been big for Penn State this season. Last week, she scored a season-high 39.175 in the all-around, including a career-high on uneven bars.

Kalea McElligott, in her more limited role this year on beam and floor, hasn’t been putting up some of the massive numbers she did as an underclassmen but always has that potential which the Nittany Lions need right now.

With both teams hunting high NQS scores and Big Ten accolades, Saturday's matchup at 6 p.m. has the chance to be a big one.

MORE GYMNASTICS COVERAGE

title: 11 Penn State student-athletes set to dance at THON 2026
author: Daniel Studer | The Daily Collegian
description:
This year, 11 Penn State student-athletes are exchanging their uniforms for their comfiest dancing clothes to participate in THON, the largest student-run philanthropy event.

THON is the world's largest student-run philanthropy that aims to provide emotional and financial support to Four Diamonds families who have been impacted by childhood cancer.

Here’s a look at those who are taking the extra step this year in the fight against childhood cancer at the Bryce Jordan Center.

Jocelyn Nathan – women’s volleyball

Jocelyn Nathan, a defensive specialist, will stand for 46 hours this weekend in honor of her

best friend, Reena Robinson. Robinson dealt with stage-four ovarian yolk sac cancer during her freshman year of high school. Robinson is now seven years cancer-free.

In her junior season, Nathan appeared in 32 matches, totaling 133 digs, for 1.317 digs per set as part of the back row. Behind the service line, the Wilmington, Delaware, native totaled four aces.

On her way to capturing women’s volleyball’s eighth National Championship in 2024, Nathan saw time in 22 matches, notching 69 digs, while playing a key role down the stretch in the lineup. She also received a spot on the Academic All-Big Ten Team.

Ella Jennes - field hockey

After wrapping up her senior year by earning a spot on the NFHCA’s All-Mideast Region second team, midfielder Ella Jennes will be dancing for the kids at THON.

“Dancing in THON means standing for something bigger than myself,” Jennes said in a statement released by the team. “Pushing past exhaustion to support families and honor the fighters.

A product of Aerdenhout, The Netherlands, Jennes capped her collegiate career with the best numbers she produced during her time at Penn State. Her seven goals tied for the most on the team, and her 14 points ranked second.

Jennes recorded her first career hat trick in an 8-0 victory over Lock Haven, scored the game-winning goal in double overtime against Delaware in the 2025 home opener and scored her final goal for the blue and white last season against Rutgers in the final game of the season.

Mackenzie Gress- women’s soccer

Women’s soccer starting goalkeeper Mackenzie Gress will trade in her cleats for a pair of dancing shoes.

“As a THON dancer, I am standing for 46 hours straight as a promise to every child still fighting,” Gress wrote on her donor page. “I stand as a symbol of strength when their strength is running low. I stand for the families who never get a break. I stand for my aunt. And I stand For The Kids.”

Gress’ contribution towards the fight against childhood cancer comes after her aunt died from cancer

“While my aunt was undergoing cancer treatments, she was such a positive light and hope for the cancer community around her, and it inspired me to do the same for those affected by childhood cancer here at Penn State through THON,” Gress said in a video.

The redshirt senior finished her 2025 season with a 10-8-3 record, her 10 wins being tied for fifth most in the Big Ten, a .706 save percentage, three shutouts and was named to the Big Ten All-Tournament team.

Erik Bolang - men’s swimming and diving

This year, Erik Bolang won’t just do freestyle in the pool; he’ll also be freestyling it on the dance floor at the Bryce Jordan Center.

The Pinckney, Michigan, native had a career-best year in 2024. Aside from being named to the Academic All-Big Ten list, Bolger set two collegiate bests at the Big Ten Championship that year in the 50 Freestyle and 100 Butterfly.

Penn State Cheerleaders

Instead of cheering from the sidelines, four Penn State Cheerleaders will be at the center of attention at THON this year: Brady Berger, Hailey Harker, Casey Segin and Olivia Vocaire.

In an Instagram post, the three each shared their motivation for participating.

Berger is the mic-man for Penn State during athletic events, mainly known for opening the presentation of games prior to the entrance of the respective sports teams.

“I THON so that kids can be kids and wake up happy and healthy. I also THON for my Mom,” Berger said.

Harker, a senior, is on the all-girl team as a flyer.

“I want every child to be able to live their life to the fullest and make all of the amazing childhood memories. I THON for Junie and every family impacted by childhood cancer!” Harker wrote.

Segin is in her senior season with the all-girl team who carries back spotting responsibilities.

“I THON because children deserve to be children, being able to play, laugh, dream, and grow up without worrying about their illness defining their childhood,” Segin said. “I dance now in hopes that one day, others will be dancing in celebration of a cure!”

Vocaire is also a senior on the all-girl team. She represents the base position for the cheer squad.

“I THON so all little girls and boys can be kids, for more laughs, and for all the future cheerleaders. I THON for our families and for the hope one day we will dance in celebration of a cure!!” Vocaire said.

Penn State Lionettes

Nobody at Penn State knows dancing quite like the Lionettes dance team, who will send three of their members to the Bryce Jordan Center. Alexa Lombardo, Chloe Nixon and Sydney Waldner will be dancing on behalf of the Lionettes’ THON families of Ashley Fazeka and Shiloh Crimmel.

“The Lionettes and I are dedicated to making sure that Ashley, Shiloh and their families can continue to experience all incredible (things) and life milestones that are in store for them,” Lombardo wrote on her donor page before her third year being involved with THON.

Waldner brings additional experience and excitement to THON weekend, with this being her fourth year in THON.

“I can say with confidence that THON weekend is truly one of the best weekends of the whole year,” Waldner said. “The magic of THON and all (that) we are able to give to the kids and families impacted by childhood cancer is nothing short of incredible and being a small part of something so much bigger than myself has been one of my greatest honors.”

MORE SPORTS COVERAGE

title: No. 6 Penn State men’s hockey continues conference play, set to host Ohio State
author: Maggie Hardy | The Daily Collegian
description:
With just three regular-season series left, and a possible NCAA tournament bid looming, No. 6 Penn State heads back to Hockey Valley to host yet another Big Ten opponent.

Ohio State will take the ice at 8:30 p.m. on Friday and 8 p.m. on Saturday at Pegula Ice Arena for a rematch with the Nittany Lions.

Penn State’s Injury Bug

To say Penn State has been plagued with injuries would be an understatement, and the team just took another major blow.

After exiting early in the Nittany Lions’ first of the two-game series at Michigan last weekend, junior captain Dane Dowiak announced on Tuesday that he would be out for the remainder of the season with an upper-body injury.

“It’s a tough one for us, however, it’s hockey, we’ve dealt with it before,” coach Guy Gadowsky said. “He’s still our captain, he’s still going to be a huge part of the team.”

The blue and white are already down one of its premiere players in Charlie Cerrato, after an announcement in late January that the sophomore would miss at least the remainder of the regular season. Gadowsky added recently that Cerrato’s recovery isn’t “a perfect linear line”.

“When it’s going (up), (Cerrato) is awesome, but when it’s going (down), you wonder, that’s the way athletes are,” Gadowsky said. “I can tell you what, this week, he’s been great.”

Both Alex Servagno and Braedon Ford have also missed large parts of the season after sustaining injuries.

Servagno was announced to have undergone season-ending surgery earlier this month, and Ford is still recovering from an injury back on Jan. 10 that will cause him to miss “a significant amount of time.”

The lack of forward depth has already caused Gadowsky to rearrange the lineup, slotting traditional defensemen Nick Fascia and Casey Aman into the forward group to fill some of the missing spots.

“Not everyone can change positions and do it well, because they’re not open and they’re not embracing it,” Gadowsky said. “Those two guys do, and that’s the number one thing.”

Another Conference Contest

Penn State continues its gauntlet of Big Ten contests, welcoming the Buckeyes to Hockey Valley for a THON weekend series.

Ohio State has struggled in conference play this season, sitting at No. 5 in the Big Ten with a 7-11 record.

The blue and white swept the Buckeyes back in October, and will want to do the same heading into the final stretch of the regular season and in the midst of a four-game winless streak.

Ohio State is led offensively by Washington Capitals prospect Jake Karabela, tallying nine goals and 16 assists through 28 games played this season. The freshman helped the unranked Buckeyes upset No. 13 Wisconsin Saturday night, scoring the tying goal in the third period.

Ohio State is solid on the power play, ranking No. 17 nationally and converting on 22.7% of chances. They’re also strong at the faceoff dot, ranking No. 19 nationally, with a 51.6% success rate.

“They are a really well-coached team, really good defensively, they got a lot of really good centers that can win a lot of draws,” Penn State defenseman Jackson Smith said. “I think the power play needs to stay hot, we’ve been doing well lately…and then the (penalty kill) needs to get back on the horse.”

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: Guest column | The day cancer won — and why I refuse to let it win again
author: Column by Sarah Grosch
description:
This letter was written by Sarah Grosch. Grosch is a fourth-year studying journalism

March 3, 2022 was one of the worst days of my life; it was the day cancer won.

I will never forget the way my principal looked at me as I walked into the bathroom during my third period on that awful Thursday. It was a few minutes later that she rushed in to find me barely able to stand, and uncontrollably sobbing.

My best friend, my role model, my MomMom was gone forever.

I was nine when MomMom was diagnosed with metastatic breast cancer. During the seven years she fought the battle, I watched her go through rounds of chemo treatment, lose her hair and try everything to keep her going. While she looked entirely different, the woman who I knew and loved, was still on the inside.

As time went on, I learned to care for her — cleaning her vomit, helping her sit or walk when she had leg pain and visiting her while she laid to rest became second nature to me.

After she had been fighting the battle for a few years, I became increasingly aware that my time with her would eventually come to an end. She would go in and out of the hospital, for weeks at a time, and it never got easier to deal with. My family scrambled, taking shifts staying with her during the day, and staying overnight. When she was put on hospice I spent every waking moment in her house.

Laying in bed with her, holding her hands and telling her about my days despite her being in a nonverbal state.

Losing someone you saw every single day is life altering to say the least. It was an adjustment that no amount of time could have prepared me for. But when I suffered this loss during my senior year of high school, I felt comfort in the fact that I was soon to be going to a school which had significant efforts to help find a cure.

My passion for cancer research was sparked long before I walked on Penn State’s campus, but it has only increased since participating in THON. Each year, since I was a freshman, I have pushed myself to attend as many fundraising events and to raise as much money as possible. Dancing in THON has been a goal of mine since I was a freshman. While it is painful, I think about the insufferable pain felt physically by cancer patients each day, and the mental toll it takes on the families supporting them.

I was blessed with a happy and healthy childhood. My MomMom did not have to rush me

around to appointments everyday, rather we played in the pool outside. She did not have to give me medicine at certain times a day, rather we enjoyed our “eeyore blue star wars toast” and “orange creamsicle smoothies.” The children fighting cancer do not have that luxury.
